Raising with AK (one of the two premium hands) from the small blind vs. Flash's button raise. The pot is 450, Flash raised to 300, and I need to call 250. A raise to 900 (approximately 3x Flash's bet) accomplishes several things: (1) builds value with a hand that's ahead of most button raising ranges, (2) exerts pressure on both Flash and the big blind Minni, (3) creates fold equity while keeping my stack reasonably managed, and (4) represents strength, potentially simplifying the hand postflop. This is a standard aggressive play with premium cards in position.

I have 8♠2♣ in the big blind facing a BTN open to 300 and an SB 3-bet to 900. The pot is ~1300 and it costs me 800 to call, so I need roughly 38% equity to continue (800/(1300+800)≈0.38). 8-2 offsuit has virtually no equity against a BTN open plus an SB 3-bet and will be played out of position postflop. There’s no reasonable implied odds or fold equity here, and a light 4-bet bluff is too risky against two opponents and this dynamic. Fold and wait for a better spot.

I have pocket Queens (Qs Qd) on the button, which is a premium hand. I opened for 300, and Claude (Small Blind) 3-bet to 900. In a 4-handed game, QQ is exceptionally strong and well ahead of most 3-betting ranges. Claude has a stack of approximately 5,300 remaining. By 4-betting now, I build the pot for value and set up a potential stack-off against hands like JJ, TT, or AK. 4-betting also prevents Claude from realizing equity cheaply with overcards. I will raise to 2,200, which is a standard 4-bet size that leaves Claude with a clear decision to either fold or commit the rest of his stack.

Calling Flash's re-raise with A♥K♦. This is one of the two premium hands in poker and warrants continued engagement. Analysis: (1) Pot odds: 3200:1300 (~2.46:1) requires only 28.9% equity to call, and AK has roughly 50-55% equity against Flash's re-raising range (which includes AA, KK, QQ, AK, AQ, and lighter hands like AJ); (2) I'm currently getting good value for this call; (3) Calling keeps the hand in play and allows for postflop decision-making based on actual board texture—if I hit any pair or broadway cards, I'll have strong showdown value; (4) If the flop completely misses (unlikely with AK), I can reassess; (5) Stack efficiency—I have 5325, Flash has 3900 left after his bet, creating shallow stacks that make postflop play manageable. While pushing all-in is mathematically viable (~55% equity vs ~37.5% needed), calling preserves flexibility and doesn't overcommit with an unpaired hand on what could be an unfavorable flop.

Betting 2200 on the T♣7♠6♥ flop with A♥K♦ is a well-reasoned semi-bluff play for several reasons:

**Board Analysis:** The flop is relatively dry for AK. I currently have ace-king high (no pair yet) but hold 14 outs: 8 to make a straight (any Q or 9), plus 6 more outs to pair my ace or king.

**Flash's Action:** Flash checked after 3-betting to 2200 preflop. This check is somewhat ambiguous—it could indicate weakness (whiffed with AQ/AJ), a slowplay (holding TT or a monster), or pot control. Regardless, I should capitalize on this opportunity.

**Strategic Rationale:**
1. **Semi-bluff value:** If Flash folded to my preflop aggression, a check here would cede control and allow Flash a free card to improve or apply pressure on future streets
2. **Bet sizing:** Betting 2200 (about half the current pot of 4500) shows strength, applies meaningful pressure, and commits most of my stack without going all-in immediately
3. **Fold equity:** Flash's check gives me some room to take down the pot here, especially if Flash is floating with weaker holdings
4. **Equity if called:** With 14 outs, I have roughly 54% equity in an all-in spot, which is favorable
5. **Stack efficiency:** With 1825 remaining, I'm essentially pot-committed, but the betting sequence shows strength and respects proper tournament strategy

This aggressive line is consistent with premium hand play and leverages the positional advantage from my preflop aggression.

I hold pocket Queens on a Ten-high flop (Tc 7s 6h) in a 4-bet pot. Pre-flop, I 4-bet to 2200 and Claude called. The current pot is 6700, and Claude has led out (donk-bet) for 2200, leaving himself with only 1825 chips. My hand is a very strong overpair, and in a low stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) situation like this, I am committed to the pot. Claude's lead could represent a set of Tens or a semi-bluff, but it more likely represents a vulnerable overpair like Jack-Jack or a strong Top Pair that is trying to 'stop and go'. By raising to 4025, I am effectively putting Claude all-in. This maximizes value from his calling range and prevents him from realizing equity with overcards (like AK/AQ) for free on the turn.

All-in with AK on Tc7s6h. Pot odds require only 14.5% equity; AK has ~50% equity against Flash's range (premium pairs, broadway). Even against stronger hands, I retain 35-40% with 14 outs. Stack is already committed; this is a standard all-in call with a premium hand and favorable odds.

Three-handed on the button I should open very wide. J♠5♥ is marginal but position is powerful and both blinds have shown a tendency to fold or defend rather than shove; I want to seize the initiative and avoid cheap multiway flops. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3×BB) rather than just min-raising to maximize fold equity and make postflop play easier in position. If called I’ll c-bet favorable dry boards and give up to heavy resistance when I miss; if I face a sizable 3-bet preflop I’ll usually fold because J5o has poor equity vs standard 3‑bet ranges. Overall this is an exploitative, position-first open to accumulate chips without bloating the pot with a weak offsuit hand.

Qh5s is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability out of position in the small blind. Minni's button raise is standard and her range is wide but strong enough that Q5o has insufficient equity (around 30-35% vs typical button open) and bad post-flop prospects, especially OOP against potential continuation bets. Pot odds to call 250 into 450 require about 35% equity, which we don't have reliably, and Flash can still squeeze or call behind. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this tournament setting with deep stacks relative to levels.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. Minni opened from the Button to 300 (3BB). In a 3-handed game, the Button's opening range is typically very wide. A8o is a strong enough hand to defend against a 3x open, especially with our deep stacks. While 3-betting is an option, calling allows us to see a flop and realize our equity without bloating the pot out of position against an aggressive opponent. I will call the 200 additional chips to see the flop.

On this flop (Qd Td Kd), I have an overcard (Ace) and an open-ended straight draw (any Jack gives me the Broadway straight). However, the board is extremely dangerous as it is monotone (three diamonds) and contains three high cards that hit a Button opening range very hard. I do not hold the Ace of Diamonds, meaning any diamond on the board or in my opponent's hand significantly reduces my equity. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react to this board texture. Leading here would be ill-advised against a range that contains many flushes, sets, and straights already.

Flop: Qd Td Kd — a monotone, highly coordinated board. I hold Js5h: no diamonds, no pair, only backdoor straight outs (A or 9). Flash called preflop and now checked the flop, so his calling range will include lots of diamonds and broadway combos that either have me crushed (made flushes, two pair, sets, or strong pairs) or will call a c-bet. Betting as a bluff here has very poor fold equity and betting for value is impossible. I have 8 raw outs to a straight (A or 9) but two of those are diamond-outs that can still leave me behind to a flush, so my real equity is small (<~15% to improve on the turn). Best play is pot control and see how villain reacts — check and fold to meaningful pressure. If Flash checks back I can take a free turn to try for my outs or plan a controlled bluff later; if he bets I will fold most of the time (only very tiny bets would merit a call). Action: check.

I have completed a Broadway straight (A-K-Q-J-T) on the turn with the Jack of Hearts. However, the board is extremely coordinated with three diamonds (Qd Td Kd). Any diamond in my opponent's hand makes a flush, which beats my straight. Minni checked back the flop, which could mean she lacks a flush or a strong made hand, but she could also be trapping on such a high-card heavy board. Since I do not hold a diamond, my hand is essentially a bluff-catcher or a thin value bet if she has a lower straight (like a 9). Checking is the safest play to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts to the straight completing on the turn.

The turn (Qd Td Kd Jh) paired my Jack, so I now have second pair (Js) but the board is extremely dangerous: three diamonds on the flop give many flushes, and a single A or 9 on the turn already completes broadway straights. Flash’s line (call preflop, check flop, check turn) is ambiguous — it can be both slow-played monsters (flush/straight/two‑pair) or missed/medium hands. My hand has some showdown value but very little fold equity and is easily dominated. Betting here would either get called by better hands or fold out only pure air; bluffing is thin and value is limited. I’ll pot-control and check to the river, preserving stack and allowing a free card to try to improve; I will fold to meaningful turn aggression (only call tiny probes), and if checked to I’ll re-evaluate on the river for a small value bet or a controlled bluff depending on the card and opponent.

The board is Qd Td Kd Jh 8c. I hold As 8h, giving me the Broadway straight (A-K-Q-J-T). However, the board contains three diamonds, meaning any diamond in Minni's hand makes a flush. Minni (Button) checked back both the flop and the turn. This passivity suggests she likely doesn't have a high flush, but she could easily be holding a small diamond or a 9 for a lower straight. Since I don't have the Ace of diamonds, I have no blockers to the flush. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands given how scary the board is, and I might get raised by a flush, putting me in a difficult spot. Checking is the most prudent play to reach showdown and realize my equity in a small pot.

I hold J♠5♥ on Q♦ T♦ K♦ J♥ 8♣ — I made second pair on the turn but the board is extremely dangerous and very connected. Flash called preflop then checked the flop and turn, which is more consistent with a medium/weak hand or a slowplay, but he has shown a tendency to bet/raise when he’s strongly ahead. There are many combos that beat my pair (Qx/Kx, two pair, sets, and completed broadway straights with an A or 9); three diamonds on the flop also create draw-heavy ranges (a made flush would require two diamonds in hand but is possible). Betting here has poor fold equity and limited value — better hands will call and worse hands that I beat are often indifferent to a small bet. With a small pot relative to our stacks I want to avoid bloating it with a marginal hand and take it to showdown. Action: check (and plan to fold to any meaningful river shove or large probe bet; call only very small bets).

3-handed on the button, first to act preflop with 8s3d. While not a premium hand, button stealing range is extremely wide in short-handed play to apply pressure and win dead money from the blinds frequently. Opponents have shown aggression but also check/fold tendencies postflop in spots. Risk-reward favorable: raise small to ~2.5BB (250 total), winning 150 immediately often due to fold equity. If called, 83o has some backdoor potential and fold equity continues postflop as a bluff. Folding trash here is too tight long-term to maximize stack growth. Deep stacks support wide ranging.

I am holding 2c 4h in the Small Blind. This is a very weak starting hand, often considered one of the worst in Texas Hold'em. Elon has raised from the Button, and I am out of position. Defending with such a low-equity hand would be unprofitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I’m in the big blind with 5♠6♣ facing a button open to 250. The pot is 400 and calling 150 more commits 250 total; that gives me 150/(400+150) ≈ 27% break-even equity. Elon’s open range from the button is likely wide, and 5-6 offsuit has decent flop playability and implied odds on connected/low boards. A 3‑bet with this hand is thin (little value, limited fold equity heads-up), so I’ll defend by calling to see a flop and then proceed based on texture: fold to heavy action on dry, high-card boards where I have no equity, or continue/float on low/connected boards and pursue straight draws or small pairs.

I have 5♠6♣ on a 6♠-4♣-A♥ flop — middle pair with a weak five kicker. The Ace heavily favors the button’s opening range, the board is fairly dry, and I have no reliable draws (only remote backdoor club/runner outs). Plan is to fold to significant C-bets, call small bets to realize equity and see a cheap turn, and re-evaluate if the turn improves my hand or the board becomes safer for a small value bet or bluff-raise.

Heads-up on the flop after my button raise and Minni's BB call/check. Board is Ah6s4c rainbow, ace-high, which hits my wide raising range (aces, broadways, suited connectors). 8s3d misses completely: no pair, no strong draws, just backdoor spade potential. However, as preflop aggressor in position, continuation betting is standard to maintain fold equity against her likely capped range (weaker aces, pocket pairs below ace, draws, or bluffs that called pre). Checking risks giving free cards and losing initiative; betting exploits my range advantage and positional edge to grow stack long-term.

I’m OOP with 5♠6♣ on 6♠-4♣-A♥ — middle pair with a weak five kicker. Elon (button) c-bet 275 into a 550 pot (half‑pot); calling 275 requires ~25% equity. Against a button opening/c-bet range there will be a lot of Ax, broadways, some pocket pairs and some bluffs; my hand has reasonable equity and good implied odds to improve to two‑pair. Raising here is thin (little fold equity OOP, no strong draws to semi‑bluff) and folding surrenders too much given the pot odds. I’ll call 275 to control the pot and see a turn, then fold to large turn aggression if an Ace/strong card hits or continue/call small turn bets and re-evaluate if I improve.

I have 5♠6♣ on 6♠-4♣-A♥-J♠ — middle pair (sixes) with a weak 5-kicker, out of position. Elon opened wide preflop and c-bet the flop, so his range contains a lot of Ax and broadway hands; the turn J heavily favors his range and cuts my relative equity. I only have 5 clean outs (two sixes + three fives) ≈ 5/46 ≈ 11% to improve on the river, so purely as a draw I need better than ~136 chips of offered pot odds to make a call profitable (B/(1100+B)≈11%). I don’t want to lead out OOP with a marginal made hand or get check-raised, so I’ll control the pot by checking. Plan: call a trivial turn bet (very small, ≲150 chips) to see the river, fold to any meaningful turn aggression, and if I improve on the river (two pair/trips) extract value; otherwise keep the pot small.

Heads-up pot on turn after wide button raise preflop, flop c-bet called by Minni's BB defend. Board Ah6s4cJs rainbow-ish (two spades), I hold 8s3d: complete air, no pair/draw beyond backdoor spade/flush block. However, as consistent aggressor in position vs passive opponent (Minni check-calls flop but history shows frequent folds to turn aggression: Hands 2,14,15,22). My button range perceived as Ax, Jx, sets, combo draws > her capped calling range (weak Ax like A5-A9, mid pairs 77-QQ, missed draws). Her check-check line screams weakness; checking back forfeits initiative/value and gives free river card to her equity. Pot 1100, deep effective stacks (~10bb+), bet ~50% pot (550) applies max pressure: high fold equity from history/exploits position/range advantage, denies her equity (e.g., flush draws, gutshots like 75,53), builds pot when ahead thin or reps strong hands ahead. Long-term +EV in 3-handed dynamic to bluff wide in these spots for stack growth.

I have 6♣5♠ on 6♠‑4♣‑A♥‑J♠ — a marginal middle pair with a very weak 5‑kicker. Elon (button) opened, c‑bet the flop and now bets the turn 550 into a 1650 pot. Calling 550 requires ~25% equity (550 / (1650+550) ≈ 25%). I only have 5 clean outs (two sixes + three fives) ≈ 11% to improve to two‑pair/trips on the river, and my current showdown equity versus a realistic button c‑bet/turn‑bet range (lots of A‑x, Jx, broadways, some pocket pairs) is well under the needed 25%. Raising OOP for fold equity is thin and risky. This is a clear fold — I’d rather preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with King-Trey suited. This is a standard opening hand in this position to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down pre-flop. I will raise to 3x the big blind, which has been my consistent sizing for opening raises.

Flash on the Button opened to 300 (3x). I have 6♣8♥ offsuit in the small blind — a weak, one-gap connector that plays poorly out of position. Calling 250 more to see a flop would commit chips into a 450 pot (pot after my call would be 700) and requires ~26% equity to break even immediately. 6-8o has significantly less equity vs a typical Button opening range and is often dominated; it’s also vulnerable to a cold-call or squeeze from the Big Blind. With a healthy 13k stack there’s no reason to spew chips in marginal multiway/OOP spots. Fold and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

9d5c is a trash hand: offsuit, gapped, low cards, no straight or flush potential. Defending BB OOP vs BTN raise is -EV; Button ranges are wide but postflop playability is poor. Standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots. Deep stacks don't change this.

I'm on the button with J♠5♥ (a marginal hand). In 3-handed play the button can open wide to steal the blinds and then play postflop in position. A raise to 300 (3x BB) is a standard, consistent sizing at this table that gives decent fold equity while keeping pot control if called. If I face a sizable 3-bet I will fold this hand; if called I'll play postflop aggressively on favorable textures (c-bet selective) and control the pot on dry boards.

Minni is an aggressive button raiser based on hand history, frequently opening wide from late position. KTo offsuit plays well against a wide BTN range (~35-45% hands), with ~43% equity typically, strong blockers to KK/AA/AK, and good postflop playability (straight potential, top pair potential). Facing a 3x open OOP in SB, calling keeps pot multiway potentially with BB, but 3-betting takes initiative, applies fold equity (Minni folds often to 3bets per history), isolates vs BB if they fold, and puts pressure. Deep stacks (117BB) support 3betting. Sizing to 3x the raise (900) is standard and balanced. Folding too tight OOP leaks EV in tournament.

I am in the Big Blind holding 6d 3d. Minni (Button) raised to 300, and Elon (Small Blind) 3-bet to 900. While my hand is suited, 63s is far too weak to continue against a 3-bet, especially when the original raiser still has the opportunity to 4-bet or call. Folding is the only sensible play here to preserve my stack.

I opened to 300 on the button with J♠5♥ to steal the blinds, but Elon (SB) 3‑bet to 900. Facing a sizable 3‑bet, J5o has poor equity and is often dominated by the SB’s 3‑bet range. Calling 600 for ~32% pot odds doesn’t justify committing chips with a hand that plays terribly postflop; 4‑betting as a bluff would be unnecessarily risky here. With stacks deep enough to make postflop decisions costly and many better spots to pick, I’ll fold and preserve my stack.

3-handed on the button with deep stacks (120+ BB). 72 suited is a playable hand from this position due to suited connector-like properties, potential for flush draws and straights, and strong postflop playability with position. Preflop, the blinds are loose/passive at times based on history (frequent limps and folds to raises). Raising steals the 150 pot often, and if called, I have position and implied odds. Limping is weak and invites multiway pots; folding is too tight 3-handed. Standard sizing 3x BB to 300.

I am in the Small Blind with Qc 2c. Elon has raised to 300 from the Button. While the hand is suited, Q2s is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive button raise. My stack is healthy, and there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand when I will be forced to play the rest of the hand out of position. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play here.

J♣2♠ is a very marginal, offsuit hand and I’m out of position facing a standard 3bb button open. However Elon's open is likely wide and the pot odds to defend are attractive (I must call 200 into a 450 pot — ≈30% equity needed). With deep stacks and heads‑up dynamics I’ll defend a bit wider than in full ring, but I will not 3‑bet this holding. I will call (total commit 300) to see a flop and proceed very cautiously OOP: continue only with a pair of J or 2, two pair/better, or strong draws; fold to heavy turn/river pressure on dry boards.

I hold J♣2♠ on a Ks Kh 4♦ flop — no pair, no meaningful draw, and I’m out of position. I called preflop for pot odds against a wide BTN open, but this flop is very bad for a float/raise: it heavily favors the raiser’s range (many Kx, AX, broadways, and some pocket pairs). With essentially no equity and deep stacks, I should not bluff-raise OOP or barrel out of position. Best line is to check and concede to any realistic bet — if Elon checks back I can see a free turn and re-evaluate; if he bets I’ll fold and preserve chips.

Flop is Ks Kh 4d, a very dry paired board with no flush draws completed and limited straight draws possible. My 72h has no pair, no draws, zero equity against a king or better, but as the preflop raiser, I have strong range advantage. Minni flatted pre from BB, which is often weak or speculative hands; her check suggests no king or strong hand. With position and deep stacks, a continuation bet is standard to take down the pot ~70% of the time against a calling station/loose passive player like Minni (history of flat calling but folding to aggression postflop in spots like hand 2,14). Sizing 60% pot (400 into 650) balances value (AK, QQ) and bluffs like this suited connector. Checking back risks giving free cards to draws and loses value when ahead.

I have J♣2♠ on a Ks Kh 4♦ flop — no pair, no meaningful draw and I’m out of position. Elon opened wide preflop and c‑bet 400 into a 1050 pot, but this board heavily favors his range (many Kx, AK, overpairs and occasional sets). Calling 400 into a 1450 pot would require ~27–28% equity; my realistic equity vs his betting range is far lower (single digits to low teens) and I’m frequently dominated. Raising as a bluff OOP with J2 is a poor use of chips (I don’t block many value combos and can be barreled off later streets). With deep stacks it’s better to preserve chips and wait for a playable spot — I will fold.

I am on the button with K7o. While not a premium hand, it is strong enough to open in a 3-handed game to put pressure on the blinds. However, given that both Minni and Elon have shown some aggression in previous hands, and I have a healthy stack, I will simply raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop.

Facing a button open to 300 in a 3-handed pot, T4o is too weak to 3-bet profitably but is worth defending for a small price. The opener’s range is wide in 3-way play, and calling 250 more (300 total committed) gives good pot odds to see a flop. I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with a raggy hand and poor kicker; a small reraise will often just get called and put me in uncomfortable spots postflop. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to heavy action if I miss, and play aggressively only if I pair my ten or pick up strong draws.

Q8o is a weak offsuit hand facing a button raise (3x, likely wide but aggressive range) and a small blind call (speculative or medium strength). Deep stacks (124BB effective), out of position multiway postflop play is difficult with poor equity realization, domination risks (vs Qx better, 8x), and no strong draws. Pot odds 700:200 (~22% equity needed) but actual equity lower vs ranges; standard fold to preserve stack for premium spots in 33/150 hands.

Check. With T♥4♣ on J♦5♠9♥ I have essentially nothing (no pair, no realistic draw) and I'm out of position to the preflop raiser (Button). I called preflop to see a flop but don't want to build the pot with air. Checking exercises pot control and preserves stack. Plan: fold to a sizable continuation bet (rough threshold ~50%+ pot, roughly ≥350) but call smaller c-bets (≲250) to see a cheap turn because Flash will c-bet fairly wide on dry boards. Only continue aggressively if I pair my ten or pick up a strong draw; otherwise avoid spewy bluffs OOP early in the tournament.

On a board of Jd 5s 9h, I have King-high. I was the pre-flop aggressor. Minni has checked to me. This board texture is relatively dry and favors my range as the button opener. A continuation bet here is likely to take down the pot immediately or set up a barrel on later streets if I pick up equity. I will bet around half the pot to maintain pressure.

I called preflop to see a flop but on J♦-5♠-9♥ with T♣4♥ I have essentially nothing (no pair, no realistic draw). I checked to control the pot and now face a 350 c-bet into a 1050 pot — the call would require ~25% equity. I have ~6 clean outs to pair by the river (≈24% to hit by river) and can easily be second-best to J/9/overpair holdings; plus I have no position and little fold equity. With marginal equity slightly below the pot odds and good stack preservation early in the tournament, folding is the +EV play.

J♦3♥ is a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with very poor postflop playability. In a 3-handed pot with deep stacks (~120–150bb) I don’t want to open or call into likely defense/3-bets from Elon (SB) and Flash (BB) — both have shown they will defend and raise. The pot is only 150, so calling 100 or making a tiny min-raise to 200 is low EV: a small raise will get called and leave me out of position or dominated; a call is just speculative with poor equity. Better to conserve chips and wait for a much stronger button spot. Folding is the correct, low-risk play here.

3d8c is a complete trash hand with no playability, suitedness, or high card strength. In the small blind facing the big blind, completing the blind gives poor equity against a wide defending range from Flash, who has shown willingness to call bets with marginal hands in past hands. The pot odds (150:50) require about 25% equity, but 83o realizes far less against any reasonable BB range. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this tournament phase, avoiding speculative calls that bleed chips over 150 hands.

84o is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Even on the button with a pot of 150 and only 100 to call (likely standard blinds with no prior action), the implied odds and postflop playability are terrible against two deep-stacked opponents (Flash SB ~153BB, Minni BB ~122BB). Raising for a steal risks a 3-bet from aggressive players like Minni (frequent raiser). Folding preserves my deep stack (~123BB) for better spots. History shows frequent preflop folds with similar weak hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Q7o. The action has folded to me. My stack is 15325, and Minni in the Big Blind has 12175. While Q7o is not a powerhouse hand, in a 3-handed tournament, blind vs. blind play allows for wider opening ranges to steal the blinds. However, Minni has shown some resistance and aggression in previous hands. Raising here is a standard play to try and take down the pot immediately, but against a potentially sticky big blind, it is also acceptable to fold to maintain a healthy chip lead. Given the current tournament status and my stack size, I will put pressure on the Big Blind with a standard raise.

I have Tc4s in the big blind facing a 300 SB open. The hand is weak and I'm out of position, but calling 200 into a 400 pot gives me ~33% pot odds and the SB is opening fairly wide, so defending is reasonable. A 3-bet OOP with this hand lacks blockers and is a poor play; folding surrenders too much equity given the pot odds. Plan: call to see a flop, proceed cautiously — fold to heavy aggression if I miss, continue if I hit a pair or a good draw.

I have flopped top pair (Queens) with a seven kicker on a relatively dry board of Qs-5h-6c. As the pre-flop aggressor in the Small Blind, I should lead out with a continuation bet to get value from weaker pairs like 5x, 6x, or middle pocket pairs, and to protect my hand against potential straight draws or overcards. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is a standard sizing here to maintain pressure.

Board Qs-5h-6c with Tc4s: I missed completely. Calling 300 into a 900 pot requires ~25% equity (300/(900+300)=25%). My realistic chance to pair a Ten or a Four by the river is only about 24% and I have essentially no backdoor flush and only tiny runner-runner straight possibilities. Flash has been opening and c-betting wide but a continuation bet here still connects with a large portion of his range (Qx, 6x, 5x, overcards) and I’m out of position — calling invites larger turns bets where I’ll have to fold. With marginal or negative immediate equity and no good plan to extract value, folding and preserving my ~12k stack for a better spot is the correct play.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of threes (3h 3c). In a three-handed game, any pocket pair has significant value. I will raise to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, or set mine if I get called. A standard raise to 3x the big blind is appropriate here.

I have QQ in the small blind facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, 250 to call). This is a premium hand and I want to avoid a multiway pot and extract value from worse hands/Ax. With deep stacks (~120BB) a standard 3-bet sizing of about 4x the open is appropriate: big enough to fold out speculative hands and get proper value, but not so large as to overcommit to a light 4-bet. I will raise to 1200 total. If called, I’ll proceed cautiously out of position and c-bet favorable boards; if I face a large 4-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and reads.

Facing a button raise to 300 from Flash and a small blind 3-bet to 1200 from Minni, with A3o in the big blind. A3o is a marginal ace offsuit with poor playability postflop, terrible kicker, and low equity against a typical 3-bet range which includes pairs JJ+, AKo/AQo, and some suited aces or bluffs but dominated here. Pot odds are about 1.45:1 effective (1100 to call into 1600), but implied odds are poor due to reverse domination risks and multiway potential if Flash calls. Opponents have been aggressive but Minni's SB 3-bet sizing suggests strength. Best play is to fold and preserve stack for better spots in this deepstack tournament.

I raised to 300 from the button with pocket threes (3h 3c). Minni has 3-bet from the small blind to 1200. This is a significant raise. While pocket pairs are valuable three-handed, 33 is the second-lowest pair and very vulnerable. Minni has been playing aggressively. If I call the extra 900, I am set-mining out of position (since Minni is SB but the action flow post-flop puts me in position, however, the pot-to-stack ratio is getting awkward). To call 900 more to win a total pot that will be 2500 requires hitting a set about 1 in 8 times. The implied odds might be there since our effective stacks are around 10k, but Minni's 3-bet range often dominates small pairs. However, folding immediately to a 3-bet with a pair in a short-handed game might be too tight. On the other hand, 4-betting 33 as a bluff is risky. Given the previous aggression from Minni, I will fold and wait for a better spot, as 33 is difficult to play post-flop when an overcard (which is almost certain) hits the board.

Button with As8d, both blinds to act. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call; stacks are deep (~120bb). With position and a blocking Ace, this is a good spot to open a wide stealing/raising range. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) — large enough to charge the blinds and reduce multiway callers while keeping postflop maneuverability. Plan: if I face a large 3-bet I’ll usually fold (re-evaluate vs a small 3-bet depending on player), but if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable A-high or dry boards, extract value when I hit top pair, and control pot size on draw-heavy textures.

3-handed, button open-raise (Minni) is extremely wide, likely 50%+ of hands. 4s6h is a suited connector with excellent playability: potential for straights, flushes, two-pair, etc. Deep stacks (122BB effective). Pot odds excellent: calling additional 250 into 450 (pre-BB action), ~1.8:1 direct but improves with implied odds OOP. SB defense range includes low suited connectors vs button steals. BB (Flash) may fold/call, but multiway ok for speculative hand. Folding too tight preflop; 3-bet not enough equity/protection OOP. Plan: call, play postflop aggressively if hit, fold to c-bet if miss unless draw.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 6s. The pot is 700 and it costs me 200 more to call. While the pot odds are 3.5:1, which might look tempting, 96-offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially against a raise from Minni who has been playing aggressively. Given the stack depths and my position, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot rather than potentially bleeding chips in a multi-way pot.

Top pair (6s) with a weak 4 kicker on a 6d-3h-Js flop, from a suited connector I defended wide preflop vs button raiser in 3-handed play. Heads-up OOP now (BB folded). Button range very wide: Ax, KQ, suited broadways, connectors, many miss or have dominated pairs/draws (e.g., Ax high, 45 gutshot). Backdoor flush draw adds equity. Deep stacks (~120BB). Pot 700; small bet builds pot, charges draws/worse (A-high, lower pairs), protects vs overcards realizing equity. History shows Minni c-bets often but checks sometimes; leading denies equity, takes initiative. Size 40% pot for value/thin value. Fold to raise if shows strength; call flat raise possible with playability.

Elon led out for 400 into a ~700 pot on a dry 6d-3h-Js board. I have A♠8♦ — the nut Ace as a blocker to A–J/A–x combos and a meaningless backdoor diamond. Versus Elon’s SB cold-call range his flop bets will include lots of Jx, some overcards, and many marginal hands; my hand has decent showdown value and blocks some of his best Jx combos. Raising to 800 as a pure bluff is feasible but thin: he can easily call with Jx or continue with better pairs and I’d be inflating the pot out of position on future streets if called. I’ll call 400 to use position, see a turn, and re-evaluate: if he checks the turn I can take it away; if he barrels heavy and I miss (no Ace/8/diamond) I can fold without committing a big chunk of my stack.

Turn brings the 4c, pairing my 4s and giving me two pair (6s and 4s) with a 6h backdoor heart potential. Strong hand heads-up OOP vs Minni's wide button calling range after my flop lead, which includes Jx (top pair), Ax-high, weaker pairs, and draws like 45/25/57 (now some have straights, but many missed). Pot 1500 (~15BB), deep effective stacks (~115BB). First to act: betting builds pot for value from 1-pair hands/draws that called flop, charges straight draws (e.g., 5x, now nutted but thin value from them unlikely), denies equity to floats/OESD. Check risks giving free card to overcards/Jx improving. Size 1000 (~2/3 pot) balances value/protection/inducement, good vs calling station tendencies observed in Minni's history (frequent calls pre/post, some folds to aggression). Fold/raise unlikely post-call.

Elon led out again and has shown continuing aggression after calling my preflop raise and c-bet on the flop. Pot is ~2,500 and he bet 1,000; calling 1,000 to win 3,500 requires ~28.6% equity. With A♠8♦ on the turn I only have ~6 clean outs (three Aces + three Eights) ≈ 13% to improve on the river, plus a small amount of showdown equity vs pure bluffs. His line (call preflop -> bet flop -> bet turn) is weighted toward Jx and other value hands that dominate me, so calling is -EV and raising as a bluff is too thin (he can/likely will call with many Jx or 3-bet with the strong hands). I’ll fold and conserve a healthy stack for a better spot.

Preflop on the button with Qc7s facing a big blind of 100 (pot 150, likely SB folded). Q7o is a profitable raise from the button due to position advantage against the BB, which allows wide stealing ranges. Q high blocks strong hands, and the hand has some straight potential and fold equity against an aggressive BB like Minni. Raising to 3x BB (300) is standard sizing for value and steals. Deep stacks support this speculative play to build the stack.

I am holding Ac 8c in the Small Blind. In a three-handed game, Ace-eight suited is a very strong hand, especially against a button open. Elon has been opening frequently from the button. Rather than just calling and playing the rest of the hand out of position, I will 3-bet to 1000. This puts pressure on Elon's wide button opening range and allows me to take control of the hand. If Minni or Elon has a monster, I can evaluate further, but A8s is a premium hand in this short-handed context.

Fold. 2♣6♠ (off-suit) is one of the weakest hands to defend with. I’m facing an open from the Button to 300 and a 3-bet from the SB to 1000 — both ranges are heavily ahead of my hand. The pot is 1400 and it costs me 900 to call (total commitment 1000), so I need ~39% equity just to break even; against two reasonably strong ranges my equity is far lower and playability postflop is terrible. No reason to 4‑bet or call and spew chips out of position. Preserve stack for a better spot — fold.

Preflop on the button with Qc7s after opening to 300. Flash 3-bets to 1000 from the small blind, which is a strong range including value hands like QQ+, AK, and some bluffs. Q7o has poor equity against a 3-bet range (dominated by Qx, low playability with gaps, offsuit). Calling 700 into 1400 pot OOP postflop is -EV due to position disadvantage and weak hand realization. 4-betting has too little fold equity and commits too much stack with a bluff catcher at best. Standard fold to the 3-bet to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Opponent tendencies show Flash can 3-bet light but still profitable to fold marginal opens here deepstacked.

I am on the button with Jack-Four offsuit. This is a weak starting hand in a three-handed game. While button aggression is generally encouraged, Minni and Elon have been active and willing to 3-bet or play back. Given the current stack sizes and the low value of my hand, folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips for better opportunities.

Heads-up from the small blind with Kd3h: the hand is marginal but has a top-card and reasonable blocker value. I don't want to limp and play a weak hand out of position for cheap, so I open to take the initiative and pick up the pot or see a cheap flop with fold equity. A standard, small open to 200 (min-raise) keeps the pot manageable while applying pressure to the big blind. Plan: if Elon folds I take the blinds; if he calls I will C-bet favorable flops (high cards, dry boards) and play cautiously OOP; if he 3-bets to a large size I'll fold this non-premium hand. Raising here is the +EV, exploitative heads-up play.

Pocket Jacks is a premium hand, top of our range. Minni's small raise to 200 (2x from SB) after Button folds is likely wide, including many hands we dominate like Ax, suited connectors, pairs below JJ. OOP in BB, we 3-bet to isolate, build the pot with equity advantage, and charge her off draws/medium strength. History shows Minni raises frequently but folds to 3-bets (e.g., Hand 9, 36). Raising to 900 (4.5x her raise) is standard sizing deepstacked, applying pressure while leaving room for postflop flexibility. Calling is too passive OOP; folding is never with JJ.

I opened to 200 from the small blind to seize the initiative, but Elon's large 3-bet to 900 shows strength and puts me to a tough decision OOP. Calling 700 to see a flop would give me only ~38.9% pot equity break-even (700 / (1100+700) ≈ 38.9%), and K♦3♥ has poor equity and is frequently dominated versus a typical 3‑bet range (strong Ks/KQs, broadways, pairs). A 4‑bet to the 1600 minimum would be an oversized bluff and unnecessarily commits a lot of chips with a weak hand and poor blockers. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left in this tournament, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is correct — fold.

Button with 7♠6♣ — marginal but playable because of position and the opportunity to steal the blinds. Pot is 150 and it’s only 100 to see a flop; I prefer taking the initiative and opening a wide range from the button to generate fold equity and isolate the blinds. A standard 3x-ish open to 300 (300 total committed) gives good pressure while keeping plenty of stack for postflop play. Plan: if called I’ll use positional advantage to c-bet favorable flops or check/realize equity when behind; if I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold this hand rather than get in with a dominated holding.

Ac7c is a strong suited Ace from the small blind facing a button raise. Minni's button opens are likely wide based on hand history, including light raises. We have excellent implied odds with nut flush potential, strong top pair/top kicker equity, and Ace blocker vs her AA range. Pot odds are favorable: calling an additional 250 into a 450 pot (effective ~1.8:1 direct, better with implied). Out of position but deep stacks allow post-flop playability multiway or heads-up. BB (Flash) may call/raise, but hand plays well. Folding is too tight for this spot; 3-betting is suboptimal as Ac7c isn't a great polarized 3-bet vs aggressive Minni. Standard call to realize equity and control pot size OOP.

I have Qh 8h in the Big Blind. Minni opened from the button and Elon called from the small blind. I only need to call 200 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 900. These are excellent pot odds (about 4.5 to 1) for a suited hand with some post-flop potential. Given the tournament context and my stack size, calling is the most efficient play here.

Flop of 9c-5s-8c is fantastic for Ac7c: nut club flush draw (9 outs) plus gutshot straight draw to the 6 (4 outs, ~3 clean). Combined ~40% equity multiway heads-up or better. Deep stacks (12k+ effective) and pot of 900 make this a prime semi-bluff spot OOP as first to act. Donk betting builds the pot, charges draws/pairs (e.g., Minni's wide button range hits medium pairs, weak flush draws, straight draws), generates fold equity from air, and protects against overcards/completions. Half-pot sizing (500) balances value from worse and thin value while keeping pot manageable OOP multiway vs aggressive opponents. Checking risks free cards to Flash/Minni or tough spot if checked through.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh 8h. The flop is 9c 5s 8c, giving me second pair with a Queen kicker. Elon (SB) has led out for 500 into a 900 pot. Minni (BTN) is still to act behind me. This is a very connected board. While I have a pair, I am out of position against one player and stuck between two. However, the pot odds are roughly 2.8 to 1. Given that I have middle pair and some backdoor straight possibilities, folding immediately seems too tight, but raising is unnecessary as I want to keep the pot manageable. I will call the 500 to see what Minni does and how the turn develops.

I have a made straight (5-6-7-8-9) on 9c-5s-8c with 7♠6♣. This is extremely strong on this texture — there are almost no hands that can currently beat me (only other players holding both a 6 and a 7 would tie). Elon’s flop bet and Flash’s call look like wide c-betting/call ranges from the blinds (pairs, single pair + combo draws, some connectors) so I expect lots of worse hands and some draws that will happily call a value raise. I want to build the pot and charge drawing hands while keeping worse pairs and 8x/9x hands in to pay me off, so I’ll make a straightforward value raise rather than just calling. I raise to 1,500 total (≈3x their bet) — big enough to extract value and price out pure floaters, but not so huge that I fold out most calling hands. If I get a large 3-bet shove I’ll evaluate based on sizing and opponent tendencies, but this is essentially the best made hand on the flop and I’m happy to commit for value on later streets.

Ac7c holds the nut club flush draw plus a gutshot straight draw (13-15 outs, ~45-50% equity heads-up vs typical raising ranges). Pot is 3400, calling 1000 OOP multiway offers decent odds (~3.4:1), but leaves us in a tough spot post-turn OOP vs two opponents, facing potential check-raises, free cards to non-clubs, or villain-led aggression. Minni (button, aggressive per history) raised flop after wide pre-flop open, likely 88+/98/95/T6+/KQc/JTc/AcXc etc. We block AA and dominate non-nut club draws. Flash (BB) flat-called our donk and Minni's raise, range capped somewhat (pairs/draws). Deep effective stacks (~9.5k with Minni). All-in raise (~12k total) applies max pressure: Minni commits 1500 already but faces 10k more into ~20k pot, fold equity high vs marginal draws/pairs; Flash unlikely to call massive overraise without premium. Takes it down often profitably; if called, realize massive equity all-in (often crushing). Calling/flat too passive OOP multiway; min-raise too small, invites further action. Consistent with flop aggression, maximizes EV long-term stack growth.

I am in a very difficult spot with middle pair (Eights) on a highly connected board (9-8-5). While I initially called Elon's 500 lead, the action has exploded behind me. Minni raised to 1,500, and Elon has now back-shoved for 12,150 total. Elon's range for this massive 3-bet shove on the flop is extremely strong, likely consisting of sets (99, 88, 55), two pair (98), or a dominant straight (76s). My 8h with a Queen kicker is almost certainly drawing thin or dead against two opponents showing this level of aggression. Calling would commit nearly 80% of my stack on a marginal holding. It is much wiser to fold and preserve my 14,000+ chip stack for a more favorable situation than to gamble it all on a weak middle pair.

I have the nut straight on this flop (5-6-7-8-9 with my 7♠6♣). Virtually nothing can beat me here—only an opponent holding both a 6 and a 7 can tie. Elon’s line (bet → call → massive reraise over my value raise) is polarizing and can represent either a bluff/overbet or a hand that actually loses to a straight (sets/overpairs/trips don’t beat a straight). The pot is 15,050 and I must call 9,575 (effectively all‑in). Given my near‑lock on the best hand and the favorable pot odds, folding would be a huge mistake. I can’t extract more by raising (it’s an all‑in decision), so I call for value and to deny him chance to fold worse holdings.

On the button with J9d suited, a premium speculative hand with straight and flush potential, excellent playability postflop. Effective stack of ~15.75BB, but position is ideal vs deep-stacked blinds. Pot unraised at 150, calling 100 is fine but raising takes initiative, isolates or steals blinds frequently based on history of preflop aggression/folds. Standard button open to 3x BB (300 total). Risks shove overkill with good equity vs calling ranges.

Elon is extremely short-stacked (approx. 13 BB) after a massive loss in the previous hand. His raise to 300 from the button is likely a desperate move or an attempt to commit his remaining chips. With Ad 6c 3-handed, I have a hand that is well ahead of a wide button-opening range. I will call the 300 to see a flop; this allows me to see how Minni (the big stack) reacts and keep the pot manageable while still having the opportunity to eliminate Elon if I connect with the board.

3♦2♦ is a speculative, suited wheel hand — not strong enough to 3-bet here but profitable to see a cheap flop given the pot and stacks. Pot is 700 and it’s 200 to call (total commitment will be 300), giving ~22% immediate pot odds and strong implied odds with deep stacks (Flash is deep; Elon is short and likely has a wide range). I’ll call to realize equity and play postflop; I avoid a raise which could invite a shove from Elon or isolate us out of position. Plan: see the flop and proceed cautiously — continue with strong draws or pairs, fold to heavy aggression if we miss.

The flop is 3s-3h-Qd, which is quite dry. I have Ace-high with a 6 kicker. Being first to act with two opponents, one of whom is extremely short-stacked (Elon), checking is the most prudent play. Elon may shove with any air or any piece of the board, and Minni, as the big stack, might try to push us around. By checking, I can see how the action develops and potentially reach a cheap showdown or catch a bluff if Elon commits his remaining chips with a weaker hand than Ace-high.

I’ve flopped a set: 3♦ plus the board 3♠-3♥ gives me trips — a very strong hand on this texture. Only hands that already beat me on the flop are Q-3 combos (very few combos), so I’m ahead of almost all of both opponents’ ranges (Elon opened wide preflop; Flash called and now checked). Pot is 900 and both stacks are relevant: Elon is short (1,275) and can shove; Flash is deep and can call large bets. I want to extract value from Qx, medium pairs and floats while charging any backdoor draws and not giving a free card that could pair the board. A value-focused sizing of ~2/3 pot accomplishes that: it keeps worse hands and draws paying to see more, and it invites a shove from Elon that I can call profitably. Plan: bet 600 to charge/price opponents, then reassess if I face a raise/shove (calling Elon's shove is reasonable; a huge raise from Flash is rare and will be evaluated but I’m unlikely to fold my set).

Short stack of ~12.75BB on the button with Jd9d facing a BB lead-bet of 600 into 1500 after SB check on a paired 3s3hQd board. Hand has 10-outs flush draw (~20% equity raw), jack overcard blocker, and backdoor potential. Preflop open was standard with suited connectors. Pot odds for call are poor (600/2100 ~22.6%, need ~28% equity), and leaves me with 675 (~1/3 pot) committed further. Minni's calling range preflop is wide (speculative hands from BB), and flop lead likely Qx, weak pairs, draws, or bluffs rather than 33. Raising all-in to 1275 maximizes fold equity (she risks 675 to win ~2775), while equity realized when called (often live vs non-nutted range). Folding kills dead money; check-fold impossible post-bet. Superior to flat-call due to stack size and position.

Elon is all-in with his raise to 1275, meaning there is no more betting possible from him. Minni, the big stack, raised to 600 and Elon then shoved. The board is 3s-3h-Qd. My Ace-high (Ad-6c) is currently beat by any Queen or any pocket pair. However, Elon's range for 13 BBs is very wide, and Minni's 600 lead could be a probe bet. If I call, I risk Minni raising again or calling and taking me to a turn where I have very little equity against her likely range (which should include Qx or mid-pairs). Given I have to call 975 more into a 2775 pot, the price is okay, but I am out of position against Minni who has shown aggression. More importantly, Elon is all-in, so I can't bluff him out. If I call, I am essentially playing a side pot with Minni or just trying to beat Elon's shove. With only Ace-high and a weak kicker on a board where someone likely has a Queen or a pair, and with a big stack behind me still to act, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

I have trips on the flop (3d with 3s-3h-Qd) — an extremely strong hand. I raised to 600 to charge draws and thin value; Elon shoved/raised to 1275 and is all-in (Flash folded). Only realistic hands that currently beat me on this board are Q3 combos (very few combos — roughly 3), quads are impossible given the cards. The pot is 2775 and I must call 675 (i.e. commit to 1275 total on this street); that's ~19.6% equity required to justify a call. My trips have well above that versus Elon's likely shove range (wide opener from the button), so this is a clear +EV call to extract value from Qx, pocket pairs and other worse hands. I will call Elon’s all-in.

In a heads-up situation, hand values change significantly, but Jack-Six offsuit remains a weak hand. I am on the button (Small Blind) and have already committed 50 chips. While I could raise to steal the blinds or call to see a flop, Minni has shown significant aggression in previous hands and holds a substantial chip lead. Folding here is a conservative play to avoid getting involved in a potentially expensive pot with a marginal hand, especially given the current stack dynamics.

I'm on the button (small blind) with pocket 3s — a clear premium in heads-up. Completing to 100 is cheap (call 50), but I want to play aggressively to extract value and isolate the big blind's very wide range while keeping positional advantage. A standard open to 300 (used often at this table) is a ~3x BB sizing: it gives fold equity, builds a pot I can play post-flop, and keeps my options flexible. If Flash calls I'll play post-flop in position and c-bet on many dry boards; if he 3-bets I will evaluate sizing and ranges (call smaller 3-bets for set-mining, fold or flat depending on the heat of the 3-bet and stack exposure). Raise to 300 is the best balance of value and discretion here.

In a heads-up situation, A6o is a solid hand to defend the Big Blind against a button open. Minni's 3x open is standard. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a chip leader who has shown aggression in previous hands.

I have top pair with an Ace kicker on a low, somewhat coordinated board (6c 5h 2c). In heads-up play, this is a very strong hand. Being first to act on the flop, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play to allow them to continue with their bluffs or wider range. If Minni bets, I plan to call or potentially check-raise depending on the sizing, as I want to protect my hand and extract value from draws or middle pairs.

I was the preflop aggressor and this flop (6c 5h 2c) is very dry and misses a large portion of Flash's calling range. I hold an underpair (pocket threes) with only a weak backdoor club possibility, so my goal is to take the pot down now or charge draws/overcards trying to see a free turn. A continuation bet is standard heads-up here — I choose a medium sizing (~1/2 pot = 300) to get fold equity against missed hands while keeping the pot manageable if I get called. If I face a large check-raise I will give up (my hand has poor protection); if called I will re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously.

I have top pair with an Ace kicker on a low board (6-5-2). In a heads-up pot against the Button's wide range, this is a very strong hand. Minni's half-pot bet is standard. I will call to keep the pot manageable and allow Minni to continue bluffing with overcards or draws on the turn. Raising here might only get action from hands that beat me (like sets or overpairs) or high-equity draws, while calling keeps her wide range in the pot.

On this turn card (2s), my top pair with an Ace kicker remains a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. The deuce is largely a brick, as it's unlikely Minni is betting a deuce on the flop. As the Big Blind, I will continue to check to the pre-flop and flop aggressor to allow her to continue her bluffs or bet with a wider range. This also keeps the pot size controlled while I have a medium-strength 'bluff-catcher' style hand that is currently ahead of most of her range. If she bets, I will call as planned in my previous turn reasoning to realize my equity and induce further action on the river.

I’m on the button with 3♥3♣. Preflop I opened and c‑bet the dry 6♣5♥2♣ flop for 300 and was called; pot is 1,200. The turn pairs the board with 2♠. That card gives trips only to hands containing a deuce and improves two‑pair combos (6x/5x), but the opponent’s flop‑call range is still weighted toward overcards, single middling pairs and float/call hands. Flash checked the turn, which is either pot control or a trap with a deuce. I’m ahead of a large portion of his range and want to extract value from worse pairs/overcards while keeping the pot manageable against the small but present set/trips combos. I’ll make a half‑pot value/denial bet of 600 to charge floats and deny free cards; if I face a substantial check‑raise I’ll fold (too often trips), and if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the river (size and runout dependent).

I have top pair with an Ace kicker. The deuce on the turn is unlikely to have helped Minni's button opening range unless she has specifically A2 or a small suited deuce. My hand is a very strong bluff-catcher/marginal value hand in this heads-up situation. Calling the half-pot bet is the most standard line to keep her bluffs in and control the size of the pot, as raising likely only gets called by hands that beat me (like an overpair or a set).

I have successfully improved my hand to a full house, sixes full of deuces, on the river (6h 6c 6s 2c 2s). This is the effective nuts, as only pocket deuces (quads) can beat me, which is highly unlikely given the pre-flop action. Throughout this hand, I have checked and called Minni's bets on the flop and turn. To maximize value, I will check again on the river. Minni has shown consistent aggression as the pre-flop raiser and has bet both previous post-flop streets. By checking, I am inducing her to fire a third barrel with her bluffs or value bet with a weaker hand like an overpair, a five, or a deuce. If she bets, I plan to respond with a significant check-raise to extract maximum value.

River: board 6c 5h 2c 2s 6s. My 3h3c gives me two pair (6s and 3s) — a medium-strength hand that is crushed by any 6 or 2 (full houses/quads) and loses to many pocket pairs/5x. Flash has called the flop and turn but checked the river, showing some weakness or pot-control. I want to extract thin value from missed/weak hands (floats, some broadways) while keeping the pot manageable against stronger hands. A small value bet (~1/3 pot) is optimal: it folds pure air and gets called by some worse hands, but doesn’t commit me to a big showdown vs a likely full house. If I face a check-raise I’ll fold (that line is almost always a full house). Bet 800 to maximize thin value and avoid catastrophic loss if raised.

I have improved to a full house, sixes full of deuces, which is the effective nuts in this situation (only pocket deuces beats me, which is highly improbable). Having checked the river to induce a bet from Minni, who has been aggressive throughout the hand, I must now raise for value. A raise to 2800 aims to extract maximum value from her likely overpairs, trip sixes with a worse kicker, or other value-betting hands she might hold in her button opening range.

I led the river for thin value with 3♥3♣ (board 6♣5♥2♣2♠6♠) — my hand is two pair (6s & 3s) and is strong vs air but crushed by any 6 or 2 (which make full houses/quads) and often behind many pocket pairs. Flash’s line (call flop, call turn, check then check‑raise my small 800 river bet to 2800) is highly polarized toward value here — most check‑raises on this river will be full houses or very strong two‑pair/overpair hands that beat me. Calling 2000 into the current pot doesn’t give correct pot‑odds versus that range, and a re‑raise would be gambling with the clearly worse hand. Per my earlier plan to fold to a river check‑raise in this spot, I fold.

In a heads-up situation, the Button (Small Blind) should open a wide range of hands to apply pressure and take advantage of position. While Ten-Deuce suited is a weak hand, it has some playability due to its flush potential. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to test Minni's response and attempt to take the pot pre-flop.

Facing a button open to 300; I’ve posted 100 so it’s 200 more to call. I hold T♥7♠ — a marginal but playable hand heads-up. The button’s opening range is wide, and the pot odds are attractive: call 200 into a 400 pot (600 after my call) — I need ~25% equity to break even. With deep stacks I have good implied odds to realize equity on tens/sevens/straight draws. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to 3-bet light (a small raise to 500 won’t fold many hands and leaves me OOP). Plan: call, see the flop, continue with top pair or strong draws, and be prepared to fold to heavy aggression on dry boards.

I have T♥7♠ on a K♦-9♥-7♣ flop — second pair with a mediocre kicker. Versus a button open in heads-up, villain’s range is wide and includes a lot of Kx but also many overcards and bluffs. I’m out of position, pot is 600 and stacks are deep, so I prefer pot control: check and let the button c-bet so I can call small/medium bets and fold to heavy aggression. Checking also keeps worse hands and bluffs in and lets me realize equity on later streets. Plan: check, call a standard continuation bet (~1/3–1/2 pot) and fold to a large raise; if villain checks back I’ll re-evaluate the turn.

As the pre-flop aggressor, the King-high flop of 7c-9h-Kd favors my range. Minni has checked, which is standard for the big blind after calling a raise. I will place a continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot, to take down the hand immediately or represent a strong King. I have a backdoor club draw with the Tc and 2c, giving me some equity if the turn brings another club.

I have T♥7♠ — second pair (sevens) with a ten kicker on a K♦-9♥-7♣ flop. Button’s range is wide heads-up and a 300 c-bet into a ~600 pot is a standard half‑pot continuation; it includes lots of Kx and 9x but also many bluffs/overcards. Calling 300 into a 900 pot gives ~25% break-even equity, and my hand fares well enough vs a wide c-bet range to continue. Out of position I prefer pot control: I’ll call to keep worse hands and bluffs in, realize equity on later streets, and fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve.

I have T♥7♠ — second pair (sevens) with a ten kicker on K♦‑9♥‑7♣‑3♠. Pot is 1,200 and villain c‑bet the flop (300) but checked the turn, which looks like either weakness/pot‑control or a float that failed. Against a wide heads‑up checking range I want a small, value/protection bet to fold out pure overcards and pick up the pot when they show weakness, while not bloating the pot OOP with a marginal holding. A ~1/3‑pot turn stab (~400) achieves that: it extracts value from worse pairs and some loose calls, deters giving a free river, and keeps my range sensible. If they fold I win the pot; if they call I’ll re‑evaluate river (check/call or small value bet); if they raise big I’ll assume Kx/strong and fold.

On the turn, Minni has led out with a small bet (donk bet) of 400 into a pot of 1200. I currently hold 10-high with no pair and no real draws other than a very remote backdoor flush chance which didn't improve on the turn. While the pot odds are 4:1 to call, I have almost zero equity in this hand and no showdown value. Minni's lead often indicates a hand that wants to define its value or a small pair/draw trying to take control. Since I have nothing, and a bluff raise into a player who just took initiative is risky, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with pocket Aces — best possible starting hand. Pot is 150 and I only need to put in 50 to complete, but I should raise to build the pot/isolate the lone opponent and charge his wide calling range. Flash has shown aggression and willingness to call/3-bet, so I size to a meaningful raise that keeps many worse hands (broadways, suited connectors, medium pairs) in the pot while leaving maneuvering room postflop. I’ll raise to 1,200 chips preflop. If he 3-bets big or jams I will strongly consider (and likely) call with AA; if he just calls I’ll proceed to extract value on most flops and control size on dangerous boards.

Minni has made an unusually large open-raise to 1200 (12x the big blind). While T6s is a playable hand in standard heads-up scenarios, calling such a significant raise out of position is mathematically unsound. I would need approximately 46% equity to justify a call, and T6s likely falls short against a range that opens for this size. Furthermore, playing a bloated pot out of position with a marginal hand increases the risk of a major stack loss. I will fold and wait for a more favorable opportunity with better pot odds.

I am on the button with Q7 suited, which is a strong opening hand in heads-up play. Raising to 300 is a standard move to take control of the pot and capitalize on my positional advantage against Minni in the big blind.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the button, I have K♠9♦ in the big blind. Heads-up K9o is worth defending — it has decent equity versus a wide button opening range and I get ~2:1 pot odds to call (put 200 more to win ~400). With both stacks deep (effective stack ~15.5k) I prefer to see a flop and realize equity rather than 3-betting out of position with a marginal hand and bloating the pot. I’ll defend the blind, play the flop cautiously, and fold to strong post‑flop/3‑bet aggression if I miss.

I defended the blind preflop with K♠9♦ against a wide button open to realize equity. The flop (7♥5♥J♥) completely misses my hand: no pair, no heart, and no real backdoor. Flash has position and will c-bet a large portion of their range on this wet, high-card flop. With virtually no equity and little fold equity, I should not invest more — check to control the pot and fold to a normal continuation bet. (I may call a very small probe to float, but the default plan is to check and give up to standard c-bets.)

On a monotone flop of hearts where I hold no hearts, my pair of sevens is vulnerable. However, as the pre-flop aggressor in a heads-up pot, a continuation bet is standard to maintain initiative and potentially fold out hands that have overcards or a single heart. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to test Minni's strength.

I defended the big blind preflop with K♠9♦ to realize equity, but the flop 7♥-5♥-J♥ absolutely misses my hand: no pair, no hearts, and no meaningful backdoor. Flash (in position) bets 300 into a 900 pot (≈1/3 pot). Calling 300 to win 1,200 requires ~25% equity. My only clean outs are 3 kings + 3 nines = 6 outs; the chance to hit at least one by the river is ≈24% (slightly below the 25% threshold), and many of those pairs will still be second-best versus Jx/heart/AX holdings. Out of position against a button who c-bets this wet, high-card board, calling to play multi-street is a marginal +EV at best and raising as a bluff is too thin without hearts or strong blockers. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for better spots.

I’m on the button (SB) with Ten of Spades (Ts) and Seven of Clubs (7c) in a heads-up spot. This is a playable hand and I want the initiative and positional advantage. Stacks are deep (23825 vs 16025) so I can comfortably play postflop. Raising to a standard ~3x BB (300) gives fold equity, narrows Flash’s defending range, and avoids inviting too many light 3-bets that a min-raise would. Plan: if called, play aggressively in position on favorable flops; if I face a big 3-bet I can fold and preserve the stack.

I am holding Ten-Five offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 3x from the button. While defending ranges in heads-up play are wide, Ten-Five offsuit is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. Given Minni's recent aggression and my current stack relative to hers, it is better to fold this marginal hand and wait for a more advantageous spot than to call and likely be forced to fold on most flops.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with a pocket pair of Threes (3c, 3h). In heads-up play, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand. I will put in a standard 3x raise to take control of the pot and see how Minni reacts. My stack is healthy enough to support this open, and I want to build a pot early with a hand that has set-mining potential or can win on low-board textures.

Facing a button open to 300 with 10♠4♥ in the big blind — a weak offsuit ten. The open is small and heads-up the button's range is wide, so I can defend without bloating the pot. I won't 3‑bet with T4o; calling 200 more (300 total including my 100 BB) gives ~25% breakeven equity and keeps pot control. Postflop I'll play cautiously out of position: check/fold to big continuation bets on dry boards, check-call small c-bets with backdoor/overcard potential, and only get aggressive if I pair the ten or hit a strong draw. If Flash 3‑bets preflop I will fold.

I flopped top pair (T♠) with a weak kicker (4♥) out of position vs a wide button opening range. The board (T♣ 2♣ 6♦) is fairly dry with some club combinations to worry about but few strong draws, so betting for value and protection is correct. A half-pot bet (~300 into 600) will get called by worse tens, underpairs and some broadways/clubs while charging club combos and denying free cards. If Villain raises big I’ll heavily re-evaluate (likely fold to a large 3‑bet, call a small raise to see the turn); if called I’ll plan my turn strategy based on the card (value-bet safe cards, check/float on scary runouts).

I have a pocket pair of Threes on a board of Ten, Deuce, Six. Minni has led into me for 300 into a pot of 600. While I only have a small pair, the board is relatively dry and disconnected. My hand currently beats all of her bluffs and many of her drawing hands (like 54 or random overcards). Given the pot odds (300 to call for a 1200 pot), I have a clear call to see if the turn improves my hand to a set or if she slows down. Folding is too weak here, and raising might be overplaying the hand since I'm behind any Ten or a better pocket pair.

I have T♠4♥ on Tc-2c-6d-7h — top pair with a weak kicker. I led the flop for 300 and was called; Flash’s calling range is wide (worse tens, underpairs, broadways, some clubs). The 7♥ is a relatively safe turn card (few hands improve besides pocket 7s or unlikely two-pair combos), so betting for value/protection is correct. I want to charge worse tens and underpairs and deny free cards to backdoor draws while keeping the price reasonable if I face resistance. A ~2/3-pot bet (~800 into 1200) achieves that. Plan: if called, re-evaluate the river; if I face a large check‑raise or big raise, I’ll heavily respect that line (likely fold to strong aggression as it indicates sets/trips).

On the turn, the board is 10c-2c-6d-7h. Minni led on the flop and is now firing a second barrel of 800 into a 1200 pot. My pocket threes are currently an underpair and only beat pure bluffs or extremely low draws. Given Minni's continued aggression and the sizing of the bet (two-thirds pot), it is highly likely she has at least a Ten or a better pair. Calling here would leave me in a very difficult position on the river with almost no outs to improve (only two threes remain in the deck). I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with Q7o — marginal but standard open in heads-up. I have position and a big effective stack, so I want to seize initiative and apply pressure to the big blind. I will min-raise to 200 total to try to steal the blind and keep the pot manageable. Postflop plan: continuation-bet on dry/favorable boards, control the pot or fold to strong resistance on heavy Ace/King/connected boards; fold to a large 3-bet (e.g. >600–800) and re-evaluate against smaller 3-bets.

I am in the Big Blind with 2h 3h. Minni has raised to 200, which is a min-raise. I need to call 100 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 400. This gives me 3:1 odds. While 23s is the lowest suited connector, in a heads-up situation with these pot odds, it is worth seeing a flop to look for potential draws or two pair. I will call and play cautiously post-flop.

I have bottom pair (threes) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a four) on a fairly low, connected board. In heads-up play, bottom pair with additional equity is worth defending. I will check to see how Minni proceeds, intending to call a standard continuation bet to see if I can improve on the turn.

Heads-up on the button; I opened to 200 pre and got called, pot is 400. Flop 6d-3c-5d with my Qd7c: I have a clean overcard (Q), a backdoor diamond possibility (runner-runner flush) and 4 outs to a straight (a 4 completes 3-4-5-6-7). Opponent’s calling range is wide so many hands missed this board. This is a good C-bet spot to take the initiative, get folds from pure air and small overcards, and realize equity when called. I don’t want to bloat the pot with only overcard/backdoor equity, so I’ll bet ~60% pot to apply pressure while keeping options flexible. If I face a large raise I’ll fold; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue only when I improve or get a favorable action.

On this low, connected board (6-3-5), my bottom pair of threes and gutshot straight draw (any four) represent significant equity in a heads-up pot. Minni's continuation bet of 250 into 400 is a standard size, and I am getting roughly 2.6:1 odds to call. Given that this flop likely misses much of a standard pre-flop opening range (high cards like Ax, Kx, or Qx), I may currently have the best hand, and even if I don't, I have five clean outs to improve to trips or a straight. Folding is too weak, and raising is unnecessary given my hand's value as a drawing hand with some showdown potential. I will call to see the turn.

On the turn, the Queen of Hearts is an overcard to the board. I still hold bottom pair (threes) and a gutshot straight draw. As the first to act post-flop in the big blind, I will check to see how Minni reacts to the overcard. Betting here doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to fold out better hands, and checking allows me to control the pot size while keeping my options open to see a river card for my straight draw or potentially reach a cheap showdown.

Turn pairs my queen — I now have top pair (Qd7x) with a mediocre kicker. Flash called my flop c-bet, so his range is wide: missed overcards, weak pairs (6/5/3), some straight draws (4/2), and occasional diamond floaters. Checked to me, so betting here accomplishes two things: extract value from worse pairs and Qx hands, and charge the straight/draw combinations. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal kicker, nor invite a large check-raise, so I’ll use a controlled, value/protection size ~1/2 pot. Bet 450 into 900 — big enough to get calls from weaker pairs and draws, small enough to fold to a large check-raise. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the river; I’ll fold to a sizable turn check-raise.

On the turn, the Queen of Hearts represents a significant overcard to the board and is well within Minni's pre-flop raising range. I currently hold bottom pair (threes) with a weak kicker and a gutshot straight draw (needing a four). Minni's bet of 450 into a 900 pot (offering 3:1 odds) is substantial given that my showdown value is minimal and my draw is thin (approximately 8.7% for the straight and 4.3% for trips). While I have some implied odds if the straight hits, calling another bet with such a marginal hand and an unfavorable board texture is likely negative EV in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

I am on the button with Ten-Four offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand. Against Minni, who has been showing significant aggression and has a larger stack, I should play conservatively and wait for better opportunities rather than entering the pot with marginal holdings.

Heads-up on the button with J9o — a hand that plays very well in position. I want to seize initiative and apply pressure rather than limp and give the BB free equity. A standard 2x BB open to 200 (total commitment) achieves good fold equity, keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks, and lets me play postflop with position if called. Flash has shown varied preflop aggression but also folds to pressure, so min-raising is the right balance between value and risk. I can fold to a large 3-bet, or proceed postflop aggressively if called.

I have a pocket pair of threes in the Big Blind. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the Small Blind. With a pocket pair, I have good value to at least see a flop, especially since the price to call is only 100 more chips. This gives me excellent pot odds to set-mine or evaluate the board post-flop. I will call the raise.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket threes. The flop has come Ace-King-Six, which is a very unfavorable board for a small pocket pair, especially since my opponent raised pre-flop and likely has many high cards in their range. My best course of action is to check and see how the opponent reacts; I have no reason to bet into a board that hits a pre-flop raiser's range so well.

Heads-up on the button with J♣9♦ (J9o) on A♠ K♦ 6♥ — I missed the flop but have position and villain checked from the BB, which shows weakness or a wide checking range. In heads-up play I should be c-betting a wide range here to pick up the pot and exploit the BB’s tendency to check-fold many unmade hands. I’ll use a small sizing (~1/3 pot) to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot manageable given very deep stacks. Bet 150 chips: if Flash folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll use position to re-evaluate on the turn (give up to significant aggression, check back on obvious A/K turns, look to continue as a bluff only on safe turns or when I pick up equity). Fold to a sizable raise — this is a bluff-seeking spot, not a commitment with a marginal hand.

I have pocket threes on a board featuring an Ace and a King. This board hits the pre-flop raiser's range significantly better than mine. Minni has followed up with a continuation bet. While the bet size is relatively small (150 into 400), my hand has very little equity and almost no chance of improving to the best hand unless I hit one of my two remaining outs. Calling here would likely lead to facing more bets on later streets where I would be forced to fold anyway. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

I am on the button with QTs, a strong broadway hand. In a heads-up situation against Minni, this is well above my average starting hand range. Raising is standard here to take initiative and build a pot against the Big Blind. Given the current stack sizes and Minni's aggressive play in previous hands, a standard 3x open is appropriate.

Heads-up in the big blind with A♠J♣ against a button open to 300. This is a strong hand that dominates a lot of the opener's range, and Flash has shown frequent preflop aggression — I want to be proactive. I 3-bet to 1200 total (well above the 500 minimum) to get fold equity, build a pot when I'm ahead, and charge weaker aces/major broadways while reducing SPR. If called I'll play postflop based on texture (c-bet favorable boards, control size on coordinated boards). If met with a large 4-bet shove I will re-evaluate (likely fold to polarized all-in sizes; consider calling smaller 4-bets depending on pot odds). Overall, aggression here maximizes EV heads-up with this holding.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with QTo. Minni has 3-bet my open to 1200. While QTo is a solid hand in heads-up play, Minni has shown significant aggression recently (Hand #40, #45, #51). A 3-bet of this size (4x my open) from the Big Blind often represents a polarized range or a very strong linear range.
