Calling out of position (or in this case, being the one facing the 3-bet) with a hand that is easily dominated like QTo can lead to difficult post-flop situations, especially against a larger stack. However, in heads-up play, QTo is often too strong to fold to a single 3-bet. I will call to see a flop and utilize my position for the rest of the hand.

I 3‑bet preflop with A♠J♣ and took the initiative; on K♦ 6♣ J♦ I have top pair with the best possible Ace kicker vs a lot of the caller’s range. The King on board is dangerous (beats me) and there are two diamonds, but as the preflop aggressor this is a good spot to continue for value and protection. I want to charge worse Jx, QJ, some pocket pairs and hands with one diamond, and fold out some overcards; a ~2/3‑pot c‑bet accomplishes that without bloating the pot unnecessarily. Plan: bet 1,600 into 2,400. If called I’ll re‑evaluate the turn (pot‑control on blanks, value/defend on safe bricks); if I face a large check‑raise or shove I’ll give credit to a polarized strong range (Kx/sets) and fold most of the time.

I have an open-ended straight draw (OESD) with my Tc Qs on a Kd-6c-Jd board. Minni's 3-bet pre-flop and lead on the flop suggest a strong range, likely including Kx, Jx, or high pocket pairs. The pot is currently 4000 and I need to call 1600, giving me pot odds of 2.5:1 (approximately 28.5%). My probability of hitting the straight on the turn is about 17%. While I don't have the immediate pot odds to call based solely on the next card, my position and the high implied odds—if I hit my straight against her likely top-pair range—make a call profitable. I will call to see the turn and evaluate.

I have Jc As on Kd 6c Jd 8h — top pair with the best possible Ace kicker. I was the preflop 3‑bettor and c‑bet the flop; Flash called, so his range is fairly wide (Kx, worse Jx, pocket pairs, some backdoor diamond holdings and floats). The 8♥ is a fairly harmless turn — it doesn’t complete the diamond draw and improves few of his obvious value hands — so this is a good spot to take value and deny equity to backdoor combos. I’ll make a sizing that extracts from worse jacks and medium pairs without over‑committing if I’m behind to Kx or a set: bet roughly half the pot. If I face a large shove or a polarized check‑raise I’ll give credit to Kx/sets and fold most non‑Ace jacks; if called I’ll re‑evaluate the river and adjust (value bet safe cards, pot‑control vs obvious killers).

I have an open-ended straight draw (any Ace or any Nine) on a board of Kd-6c-Jd-8h. Minni has shown significant aggression by 3-betting pre-flop, betting the flop, and now firing a large 3000 bet on the turn. To call, I need to commit 3000 chips into a total pot that would become 11600, giving me pot odds of approximately 25.8%. My probability of hitting one of my 8 outs on the river is about 17.4%. This means I would need significant implied odds to make the call profitable. However, the presence of two diamonds on the board (Kd and Jd) makes two of my outs (Ad and 9d) potentially dangerous as they could complete a flush for Minni. Given the high cost of the call and the fact that my outs might not even be clean, I will fold and preserve my remaining 11,925 chips.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with A♦Q♦ — one of the best non-pair hands preflop. I'm in position postflop and deep-stacked relative to the blinds, so I want to take the initiative, build a pot with a strong value hand and get fold equity against weaker broadways and speculative hands. Limping/calling 50 would give Flash cheap odds to see a flop and invite him to take the lead; the minimum raise to 200 is too small and often just keeps dominated hands and 3-bet bluffs in. I raise to 300 total (≈3x the BB) — a standard heads-up sizing that balances extracting value and maintaining fold equity. Postflop plan: if called, continue aggressively on many favorable flops (ace/queen-high, dry boards) and re-evaluate vs larger resistance; if Flash 3-bets heavily, I will assess stack-to-pot ratio and range dynamics (call or 4-bet as appropriate vs his tendencies).

I am holding 2c 4s in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 3 big blinds from the Button/Small Blind. While I am getting 2:1 on a call, 4-high offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker and plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot to defend.

I am on the button with 7h8h, a premium suited connector that plays very well in heads-up play. With a stack of over 117bb and Minni having a significant chip lead, I want to see a flop while maintaining control. Raising to 300 is a standard open-raise size to build the pot and apply pressure on the big blind.

Heads-up against the button's open, 4♣3♣ is a clear defend from the big blind — good postflop playability and suited connectors realize equity and can win big pots when they hit. The raise to 300 is small relative to both stacks, so calling 200 more to see a flop is cheap and keeps the pot manageable while keeping the opponent's wide opening range in. A tiny 3-bet to 500 would be ineffective as a bluff (won't fold enough of his range) and would commit us to a bigger OOP pot with a marginal hand. Folding is too tight in heads-up. I'll call to 300 total, plan to play the flop cautiously and proceed aggressively only if we hit or pick up strong equity.

I have 4c3c on 3s Kh 3h — I’ve flopped trips (a very strong, near-nut hand; only the lone remaining 3 (3d) can beat me). Heads-up versus the button’s wide opening range this is almost always the best hand, so I should be betting for value. A bet of 400 (~2/3 of the 600 pot) balances extraction and deception: big enough to charge Kx, mid pocket pairs and worse Ax hands, but small enough to keep many of those hands in the pot. Checking to trap risks giving a free card or letting the opponent realize equity cheaply; an over-large bet could fold out too many thin-value hands. If called I’ll continue to extract value on later streets and re-evaluate if they raise (which would indicate either the unlikely 3d or an ambitious bluff).

I am heads-up and holding 8h7h on a board of 3sKh3h. I have a backdoor straight draw and a flush draw. Minni has led out (donk bet) for 400 into a 600 pot. This lead often indicates either a King, a protected 3, or a bluff/draw. Given my flush draw and the fact that I am in position, I have sufficient equity to continue. I will call the 400 bet to see a turn card, keeping the pot manageable while I look to improve my hand or potentially outplay my opponent on later streets if they show weakness.

I have trips (3c + board 3s/3h) on 3s Kh 3h 2h — a very strong hand heads‑up. The only current hands that realistically beat me on the turn are rare: pocket deuces or pocket kings (full houses) or a made heart flush (two hearts in villain’s hand). Flash opened preflop and just called my flop bet, so his range is wide and contains plenty of Kx and some heart combos that will call a reasonable bet. I want to both extract value from top pairs/medium pairs and charge heart draws, while not folding out too many worse hands; a ~2/3‑pot turn bet accomplishes that. I’ll bet 1,000 into the 1,400 pot to get value and protect — if he check‑raises large I’ll re‑evaluate (that will usually indicate a very narrow, strong range), but for now build the pot with my near‑nut hand.

On the turn, the 2h completes my flush (8-high). The board is paired (3s-Kh-3h-2h), so there is a slight risk of a full house or a higher flush, but an 8-high flush is extremely strong in a heads-up match. Minni bet 1000 into a 1400 pot. By calling rather than raising, I keep her range wide and allow her to continue bluffing or value-betting a King on the river. Raising now might only get called by better hands (higher flushes or boats) and would likely force her to fold weaker Kings or bluffs that I can still extract value from on the river. I will call to maintain pot control and evaluate the river action.

I have 3♣4♣ on 3♠ K♥ 3♥ 2♥ 4♦ — that’s a full house (threes full of fours), essentially the near‑nut river hand. Villain’s line (open preflop, call flop, call turn) is wide and sticky: most of his range is Kx, medium pairs and broadways. Very few hands actually beat me — pocket kings (3 combos) and pocket 4s (1 combo) — and there are only a couple of tie combos (3♦+4x). Against that distribution I should be betting for value rather than checking. A pot‑sized value bet (~3400) will get called by worse hands (Kx, single pairs, some two‑pair/fuller but inferior combos) while keeping raises from the very small number of beating combos manageable. If he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (that will usually indicate one of those few beating combos).

I have an 8-high flush on a paired board (3s-Kh-3h-2h-4d). Minni has led out on every street since the flop, and her river bet of 3400 is a full pot-sized bet. This line is very strong, indicating either a King, trips, a flush, or a full house. While an 8-high flush is a very strong hand in heads-up play, the paired board and the presence of several higher heart cards (A, Q, J, T, 9) mean I am not holding the nuts. A raise would likely only be called by hands that beat me (better flushes or full houses) and would fold out the hands I currently beat (trips or a King). Given the pot odds and the strength of my hand, folding is too tight, but calling allows me to reach a showdown and see where I stand without over-committing against a potentially superior hand.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a very wide range. 6h7d is a playable, positionally strong hand with good flop playability and straight/flush potential. Flash is short (6.6k) and has shown mixed preflop aggression, so a standard 2xBB open (raise to 200 total) seizes the initiative, builds the pot when we're likely ahead, and preserves post-flop maneuvering in position. Plan: c-bet favorable flops, use position to apply pressure, and re-evaluate versus a big 3-bet or shove (I can call shoves often given his likely wide range).

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 5h, a weak hand. The stack sizes are significantly skewed with Minni holding 33,075 chips against my 6,625. Minni has raised to 200, which is a min-raise from the button (Small Blind). To call, I only need to put in 100 more chips into a pot that will be 400. This provides 4:1 pot odds. However, 95 offsuit plays very poorly post-flop, and I am out of position. Against a massive chip leader who has been aggressive, I should avoid bloating the pot with marginal holdings. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my remaining 66 big blinds.

I am on the button (SB) with 3s 6d. This is a very weak hand. Given my stack size of 6,575 compared to Minni's 33,275, I need to be cautious about playing marginal hands out of position or with low equity. Although it's only 50 to call, the hand has poor playability and I've seen Minni be aggressive in previous hands. Folding is the standard move with trash hands like 63o.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a wide range — Q7o is marginal but perfectly fine to raise from the SB/button. Raising to the minimum (200 total) preserves my positional advantage and creates fold equity vs Flash's BB while keeping the pot manageable given my very deep stack and his ~64bb stack. Calling 50 is too passive (gives up initiative and allows cheap flops), so I raise to take control; plan is to C-bet favorable flops and apply pressure in position. If Flash 3-bets big I will usually fold this hand; if he calls I'll play postflop aggressively with position.

In a heads-up situation, defending the big blind against a minimum raise (2x) with a hand like Q5o is standard. The pot odds are 3:1, requiring me to call only 100 more into a 200 pot. While Q5o is a weak hand, it has enough equity to see a flop, although I must play cautiously out of position against a significant chip leader.

I am out of position as the big blind in a heads-up pot. I have Queen-high on a board of Jack-Four-Eight. This board does not connect with my hand at all, and I have no draws. My standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. I have very little equity and will likely fold to a continuation bet.

Heads‑up on the button I opened a wide range preflop and the flop Js 4s 8h is relatively dry and favors the preflop raiser. I hold Q7o — an overcard to the board and I have position. Flash checked, so I want to use my initiative to fold out lots of his missed hands and get value from weaker Jx or turn cards. A half‑pot c‑bet gives good fold equity while keeping the pot manageable against his ~63bb stack. Plan: bet ~50% pot (200). If he check‑raises large I’ll fold Q7o (too little equity); if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and barrel selectively when I improve or the turn is favorable.

I am in the big blind facing a continuation bet of 200 into a 400 pot. The board is Js 4s 8h. I have Queen-high with no draws and no pair. My hand has very little equity against Minni's range, especially since she has been an aggressive chip leader. There is no reason to continue with Queen-five offsuit on this texture; I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have been dealt pocket eights, a strong pocket pair in a heads-up situation. My stack is relatively short (63.25 BBs) compared to Minni (335.25 BBs). I need to play aggressively but cautiously. Pocket eights are well ahead of an average heads-up range. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand from the button.

Heads-up in the big blind with Ad Tc vs a button open to 300. This is a strong hand heads-up and I want to take the initiative rather than playing OOP for a flat call. Flash has shown he will sometimes fold to aggression; a 3-bet will get value from worse aces/Kx/Tx and fold out small pairs and speculative hands. I’ll make a sizeable but not committing 3-bet to apply pressure while keeping postflop maneuverability against his 6,075 stack. Plan: raise to take the lead preflop; c-bet favorable flops and re-evaluate if he 4-bets or jams.

I have pocket eights (8h 8s) on the button in a heads-up match. I initially raised to 300, and Minni has 3-bet me to 1200. My stack is 6075 (approx 60 BBs). While pocket eights are a strong hand, a 4x 3-bet from a dominant chip leader like Minni is significant. However, in heads-up play, pocket eights are often ahead of a 3-betting range. I have three main options: call, 4-bet, or fold. Folding is too weak. Calling allows me to play a pot in position and see a flop, but it may be difficult to navigate if overcards come (which they usually do). A 4-bet would likely commit a significant portion of my stack. Given the current stack sizes and the need to defend against aggressive 3-bets, I will call to see a flop and evaluate, keeping the pot manageable while retaining position.

I 3‑bet preflop to take the initiative, and this flop (Qh Jc 6s) is a very reasonable continuation-bet spot — it misses a big chunk of Flash’s calling range (small pairs, many broadways) and I have relevant equity (Ace‑high plus a gutshot to the King). Betting keeps the initiative, folds out many marginal hands, and charges draws. I’ll make a half‑pot c‑bet (1,200 into the 2,400 pot) — large enough to get fold equity against his wide flatting range but small enough to keep options on the turn against his short stack. If he folds I win; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (K improves me a lot; a big raise/shove will usually indicate very strong made hands and I’ll fold if the line is polar).

On a flop of Qh Jc 6s, my pocket eights are now essentially a weak bluff-catcher. There are two overcards (Queen and Jack) that are very much in Minni's 3-betting range. Minni has led out with a 1/3 pot bet (1200 into 2400). Given the board texture and the pre-flop action, it is highly likely Minni has at least one overcard or a pair better than eights. While I have some backdoor straight potential, continuing against a large stack who has shown significant aggression is risky with a mid-pair on a coordinated board. I will fold to preserve my remaining 51.75 BBs and look for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with KcTd — this is a strong, playable hand (top-card strength, good K blocker and decent connectivity). I want the initiative and to isolate the big blind rather than just calling 50 and letting them see a cheap flop. A 3x BB sizing is standard heads-up and balances value and fold equity against Flash (stack ~50BB). Raise to 300 total: big enough to deny free cards to weak holdings and define ranges, but not so large that I overcommit out of position postflop if they 3-bet. If Flash 3-bets, I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to very large 3-bets/shoves depending on range; call or continue vs smaller 3-bets to play position).

I am currently in the Big Blind with Ten-Three of Hearts (T3s). Minni, who holds a massive chip lead and has been playing very aggressively, has raised to 300 (a 3x open). While T3s is suited, it is a very weak hand to defend with out of position, especially given our current stack depth of approximately 50 big blinds against a player who is consistently applying pressure. Calling here would likely lead to difficult post-flop situations where I am either dominated or forced to fold to further aggression. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with a suited connector (4s 6s). Although my stack is significantly smaller than Minni's, I have the advantage of position. I will put in a standard open-raise to 300 to maintain pressure and try to take down the blinds or play a pot in position. I have been opening for 300 consistently, and I will continue this pattern here.

Flash opened to 300 from the button; pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call (300 total including my posted BB). Qc2 is a marginal offsuit hand but heads-up the button’s opening range is wide and this hand has reasonable showdown value. I’m out of position postflop and Flash’s stack (~4,775, ~48bb) makes 3-betting with a speculative, dominated hand unnecessary — I don’t want to inflate the pot or face a shove with a weak kicker. I’ll defend the big blind and see a flop cheaply; if I miss I’ll fold to heavy continuation aggression, but if I hit a queen or a strong draw I’ll continue for value or apply pressure.

I called preflop with Qc2d to see a flop against a wide button opening range. The board Kd Kh 6c is paired and my queen has modest showdown value versus many non-K hands; Flash checking the flop shows weakness or pot control. Rather than check behind and give a free card, I want to apply pressure and take the pot now — a half‑pot bet folds out worse broadways/Ax and small pairs while keeping the pot manageable if called. I will bet 300; if he raises heavily I’ll assign him a King/strong hand and fold, if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn.

I have bottom pair (Sixes) on a paired board (Kings). Minni's lead of 300 into the pre-flop aggressor is a 'donk bet' that could signify a range of hands, including a King, a small pocket pair, or a complete bluff. Given my relatively short stack compared to Minni, I want to keep the pot manageable while still realizing my equity. Calling is the most prudent play here; it allows me to see how the turn develops without over-committing. Raising would likely only be called by hands that have me beat, and folding is too weak given that I have a pair.

I c-bet the flop as a bluff/pressure play but Flash called, which narrows his range to many hands that beat or have decent equity versus my Q2 (Kx, pocket 6s/full house possibilities, and reasonable pairs/Ax). He checked the turn, which could be pot control with a strong hand or a trap, and it’s unlikely a second small bluff will fold out the hands that already called the flop. With a marginal hand and a large SPR (his stack is deep enough to call a significant turn bet), the least-risk, highest-expected-value line is to check and keep the pot small — I preserve chips and can fold to serious aggression on the river or re-evaluate if a harmless card arrives. I’ll check behind and go to the river for a cheaper showdown rather than barrel more with a weak kicker.

I have a pair of sixes on a paired King board. Minni donk-bet the flop and has now checked the turn. This check often indicates a marginal hand or a surrender. Betting here likely only gets called by hands that beat me (like a better pocket pair or a King) and folds out hands I already beat. By checking behind, I control the pot size and move towards a cheap showdown with my modest pair, which is a prudent strategy given my stack size relative to Minni's.

Flash called my flop c-bet and then checked the turn, which reads like pot-control with Kx or a medium pair rather than a wide bluffing range. My hand (Qc2d) is only queen-high and the river 9h doesn’t improve me. A river bet would be a thin bluff with limited fold equity (he already showed willingness to call the flop) and a thin value bet (few worse hands will call). With negligible river equity and no bet to face, the least-risk, highest-EV line is to check and take the cheap showdown — preserve chips and fold if he leads out instead of bloating the pot with a weak kicker.

I have a pair of sixes on a board with a pair of Kings. Minni donk-bet the flop but has checked both the turn and the river. This sequence typically suggests a hand with some showdown value that isn't strong enough to bet for value, or a missed draw. My pair of sixes has decent showdown value in this spot. Betting is unlikely to get called by a worse hand, and checking ensures I reach showdown without risking more chips against a slow-played monster or a slightly better pair. I will check behind.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with Q♥5♠ — a playable hand that should be opened to take the initiative and steal the BB. Flash has ~5,575 (~55 BB), so I don't want to commit too much with a marginal hand but also want a standard aggressive sizing. I'll raise to 300 total (about 3x the BB / a common table raise) to apply pressure, isolate the shorter stack, and keep postflop options. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet/all-in; call small-ish 3-bets only if odds justify; play cautiously postflop (c-bet good Q/low/draw flops, check/fold on unfavorable textures).

In Hand #61, I am in the Big Blind with T7o. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. My current stack is 5575, while Minni has a significant lead with 34025. This is a heads-up situation. T7o is a marginal hand that doesn't play particularly well against a standard opening range from a chip leader. Given the stack sizes and the pressure Minni has been applying in previous hands, I should avoid playing weak hands out of position. Folding here preserves my stack and allows me to wait for a better spot to commit chips.

QJo is a very strong hand in heads-up play, typically ranking in the top 15-20% of starting hands. As the button (Small Blind), I have positional advantage for the rest of the hand. While Minni has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively, I need to continue playing fundamentally sound poker. A standard 3x raise to 300 is my typical opening size and allows me to take control of the betting lead.

Facing a standard SB open to 300 heads-up. Q♠5♦ is a marginal but defensible hand against a very wide button opening; the pot is 400 and I only need to call 200 (pot odds ~2:1, need ~33% equity). I don’t want to 3‑bet out of position with this hand, so I’ll call to see a flop and evaluate — continue on a Q/5 or strong draw, fold to heavy aggression if I miss. Amount 300 is my total contribution (100 BB already + 200 call).

I have Q♠5♦ on K♥ T♣ 5♣ — second pair with a decent queen kicker. Heads-up versus a wide SB opening range this hand is often ahead of many unpaired broadways and small pairs, and the board has two clubs so there are club draws to charge. I want to take the initiative and extract value from worse hands while denying cheap equity to draws, but keep the pot controlled given my hand’s vulnerability and the effective stacks. Bet a small sizing (~1/3 pot) to accomplish that; if Flash raises strongly I’ll respect the aggression and fold/re-evaluate accordingly.

I have an open-ended straight draw (OESD) with my Queen-Jack on a King-Ten-Five board, needing an Ace or a Nine to complete my hand. Minni has led out with a small bet of 200 into a 600 pot (1/3 pot size). Given my position and the fact that I have about 32% equity to hit my straight by the river, calling is the most profitable and standard play here. It allows me to see the turn card cheaply while maintaining the ability to take the pot away later if I miss or value bet if I hit, all while keeping the pot size manageable against a larger stack.

I continuation‑bet the turn to keep the initiative, charge club draws and extract value from worse pairs and Broadway hands. The 4♠ is a blank — it doesn’t complete the two‑club draws that called the flop and it doesn’t dramatically change my relative equities. With Q♠5♦ I have second pair with a decent kicker but the hand is vulnerable to Kx/Tx/sets, so I want a small sizing to deny equity and keep the pot controlled against the single active opponent (Flash, ~5k stack). A bet of ~1/3 pot forces clubs and many underpairs to pay to see a river while still allowing me to fold to significant turn aggression. If Flash raises me large I will respect that line and fold/re‑evaluate; if he calls I’ll re‑assess on the river and proceed cautiously.

On this turn, the 4s is a complete brick that doesn't change the board texture. I still have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: Aces and Nines). Minni has bet 350 into a pot of 1000, giving me roughly 3.8:1 odds. While my immediate equity to hit the straight on the river is about 17-18%, the presence of implied odds (if I hit, I can potentially get paid off) and my positional advantage make this a clear call. I am not looking to raise here as a semi-bluff because Minni's small sizing and massive stack suggest they are unlikely to fold many hands they've chosen to lead with twice.

I have Q♠5♦ — second pair (pair of 5s) on K♥ T♣ 5♣ 4♠ 7♣. The river 7♣ completes the possibility of a club flush for any opponent holding two clubs, and Flash has shown a wide opening and called both flop and turn so his range contains Kx/Tx/pairs and two‑club combos. With the pot only 1,700 and Flash a short stack (4,725), a bet here risks inducing a shove that I should usually fold to with second pair. I want to keep the pot controlled and realize my showdown equity while allowing missed draws to bluff — check and call small bets, fold to a large jam. Action: check (amount 0).

On this river, the 7 of clubs completes a possible flush. I have completely missed my open-ended straight draw and am left with only Queen-high. While I do hold the Jack of clubs as a minor blocker, Minni's check doesn't necessarily mean she is weak; given her massive stack and previous aggression, she might be planning a check-call with a marginal hand like a Ten or a weak King, or she may have just given up on a draw herself. Betting as a bluff into a player with a huge chip lead who has shown stickiness is risky. Since I have no showdown value, I will check and hope to see a missed draw from her, though I expect to lose the pot most of the time. Preserving my remaining stack of 4,725 is more important than a low-probability bluff in this specific tournament context.

I’m on the button (small blind) with 4♦6♥ — a weak, unsuited holding. Heads-up I have the positional advantage postflop, and the to-call is only 50 into a 150 pot, so completing to 100 is cheap and keeps my range wide. Raising with this hand would bloat the pot with marginal equity and invite difficult postflop decisions; folding 50 is too passive given the favorable pot odds. Plan: complete to see a flop in position; continue only if I hit a pair or a meaningful draw, otherwise check and give up to significant pressure. If Flash 3-bets to a large size preflop I’ll fold.

In Hand #63, I am in the Big Blind with 7s 8h. The SB (Minni) has just limped in (called the remaining 50). The pot is 200, and I have the option to check and see a free flop or raise. Given our stack sizes (I have about 46 BBs, while Minni is the massive chip leader with over 350 BBs), there is no need to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand like 78o. Checking allows me to realize my equity cheaply and see if I can connect with the board.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 8h. The flop is Ac Jd 5c, and I have no pair or draw. Since I am out of position against the chip leader who limped pre-flop, I will check to see how the action unfolds and avoid bloating the pot with a weak hand.

Heads-up on a dry A♣ J♦ 5♣ flop and I'm in position with 4♦6♥ — no pair, no club, only weak backdoor possibilities. Flash checked, so a small continuation bet should pick up the pot often against missed ranges (Kx, Qx, small pairs, and many bluffs). I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll make a half-pot bluff to buy the pot now and preserve stack depth. Plan: bet ~50% pot (100). If he calls, I’ll give up to significant turn aggression unless I pair or hit a meaningful draw; fold to a big raise.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7s 8h. The board is Ac Jd 5c, and I have no pair, no significant draw, and only 8-high. Minni, the chip leader, has bet 100 into a pot of 200 after limping pre-flop. While the price to call is small, I have zero equity on this texture and am out of position. Continuing here would be a waste of chips. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my remaining stack.

I am on the button with K7o. In a heads-up situation, a King-high hand is well above the average starting hand. Given my stack size of 4575 (about 46 big blinds) relative to Minni's 35275, I need to look for spots to accumulate chips without being overly reckless. Raising from the button is a standard aggressive play to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately. A standard 3x raise is 300.

Heads-up facing a 3x open to 300 from the button. J♣8♦ is a perfectly serviceable defending hand in the big blind against a wide opening range — it has decent showdown equity and playability post-flop. Opponent has ~43BB so they open fairly wide but can still call/4-bet; I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand by 3-betting and running into a call or shove. Calling keeps the pot reasonable (300 total), realizes equity, and lets me proceed based on the flop texture — I’ll check/control on dry boards, call small c-bets to see turn, and play aggressively if I hit or pick up initiative.

Flop is 2♣-6♣-T♠ and I hold J♣-8♦ — no pair, one overcard (J) and only a backdoor club. Opp checked to me after I defended preflop. This is a fairly dry texture and my hand is marginal; I want to keep the pot small and realize equity rather than bluff into a wide but potentially sticky range or induce a shove from a shorter-stacked opponent. I’ll check to control the pot, plan to call a modest c-bet to see a turn (folding to very large aggression), and only start building the pot if I pair the J or pick up clear initiative on a later street.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and have been checked to on a relatively disconnected board (T-6-2 with a flush draw). While I only have King-high, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet to take down the pot immediately, as Minni will miss this flop a large percentage of the time. A half-pot bet of 300 is sufficient to put pressure on her air hands while keeping my risk controlled given my shorter stack.

I defended preflop with J♣8♦ intending to check and keep the pot under control. On 2♣-6♣-T♠ I have only backdoor club equity and a J overcard — no made hand. Flash's 300 is a small c-bet into this pot, so calling gives good pot odds to realize equity and lets me re-evaluate on the turn. Raising here would be a thin semibluff that unnecessarily inflates the pot against a fairly wide but sticky opening range and could put me in a marginal spot vs a shorter-stacked opponent. I’ll call the 300, plan to take the lead if I pair the J or pick up the club on the turn, and fold to large, committed aggression on a blank turn.

Pot is 1,200 and there is no bet to call. I hold J♣8♦ on 2♣-6♣-T♠-5♠: no pair, only a J overcard and a very weak backdoor club (need both turn+river clubs). Opponent c-bet small on the flop then checked the turn, showing a fair amount of weakness or a willingness to control pot size. With only marginal equity and a shorter-stacked opponent (≈4,025) who can still shove, I don’t want to inflate the pot and get committed with a likely second-best hand. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize equity, and keeps options open: call a small/medium bet on later streets, fold to heavy aggression, or start building pot only if I pair the J or hit a club on the river. I’ll check back the turn.

On the turn, the board is 2c-6c-Ts-5s. I have King-high and a very weak straight possibility with my 7. Minni has checked to me for a second time after calling my flop continuation bet. While I could continue the aggression, checking back here allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity. King-high still has some showdown value against her draws (like clubs), and I am the shorter stack, so I want to avoid bloating the pot without a made hand or a strong draw.

I have J♣8♦ on 2♣-6♣-T♠-5♠-2♠ — I never made a pair and my only real show-down equity is a Jack-high. Flash c-bet small on the flop, then checked turn and checked the river: that line shows a lot of medium-strength and missed hands (or a slow-played deuce/trips). Betting here could fold out pure air (A/K/Q highs) but will be called by any T/6/5 or a 2, and can be punished by a raise; with the pot at 1,200 a half-pot bluff still requires an uncomfortably high fold frequency (~67%) to be +EV if called only by better hands. Flash still has ~4k behind and has shown willingness to call/commit with medium hands in other spots, so I don’t trust sufficient fold equity. I’m not getting value with J-high and I don’t want to spew chips on a thin bluff, so I’ll check to see showdown (and fold to any river shove if he leads out).

On the river, the board is 2c-6c-Ts-5s-2s. Minni has checked to me for the third time (after calling the flop). With King-high, I have reasonable showdown value in a heads-up pot against her potential missed draws (like clubs). Betting here would likely only be called by hands that beat me (a Ten, a six, or a five), and I am unlikely to fold out a better hand with a small bet given her large stack. Checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity and avoid losing more chips with a relatively weak hand.

I have AdAh (best possible preflop holding) heads-up on the button with position and a deep effective stack (Flash ~5,125). Pot is 150 and it costs 50 to just call. I want to build a pot and charge all of Flash’s calling range (pairs, broadways, suited connectors) while still leaving room to call a shove if he jams. A small raise (min-raise) gives him good odds to see a flop and doesn’t extract value; an overly huge raise is unnecessary. I’ll raise to a size that (1) gets value, (2) makes speculative hands pay, and (3) still keeps shove/call decisions reasonable. Raise to 1,200 total chips preflop. If he shoves, I’ll call; if he calls, I play AA in position for maximum postflop value.

Minni has opened with a very large raise of 12x (1200 chips), which is consistent with her aggressive play in previous hands (e.g., Hand #58, #52, #45). Calling 1100 more with Ten-Six suited would commit more than 20% of my remaining stack pre-flop. While the hand has some potential, T6s is too weak to defend against such a polarisingly large sizing out of position, especially when trying to preserve my stack to find a higher-equity spot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with J8offsuit, which is a marginal hand but generally strong enough to open in a heads-up situation or call a small raise. In this case, I am the small blind and it's 50 to call or I can raise. Given Minni's recent aggressive play and the stack disparity (I have about 50 BBs, Minni has nearly 350 BBs), I should play cautiously. However, J8o is well within a standard heads-up opening range. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand pre-flop.

I’m in the big blind facing a button open to 300 from a relatively short-stacked opponent (Flash ~4,825). K♠8♠ is a reasonable heads-up defending hand: top-card value plus suitedness and decent postflop playability. Calling 200 keeps the pot small and lets me realize equity; 3-betting out of position with a marginal hand risks inflating the pot unnecessarily and may induce an all-in or commit me with a dominated holding. Plan: call to see the flop — if I hit a king or a strong spade draw I’ll apply pressure; otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to heavy action.

I missed the flop — K♠8♠ has no diamond backdoor and only king-high (runner-runner spades are unrealistic). The board (2♦4♦2♣) favors pocket pairs and diamond draws from the opener, so betting out risks inflating the pot and getting shoved on by a made hand. Stick to the preflop plan: control the pot and keep ranges wide by checking. I’ll check and fold to a large shove, but will call smaller probes to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn (look to apply pressure only if I pair the K or pick up a spade).

On this flop of 2d-4d-2c, the board is paired and relatively dry, though there is a diamond flush draw. My J8-high doesn't have much showdown value yet, but it's ahead of many of Minni's random missed hands. Since Minni checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor, I'll put out a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to try and take it down now or set up a play on later streets. This board texture often misses a calling range, and a bet here puts pressure on her non-pair hands.

Call 300. Flash’s flop bet is small (~300 into 900, ~1/3 pot) and he’s a relatively short-stacked, wide-button opener. K♠8♠ has almost no showdown value on 2♦-4♦-2♣ (no diamonds, only backdoor spade/few outs), so raising here risks getting shoved or called by many better hands (pocket deuces, small pairs, diamond draws). Calling keeps the pot controlled, lets me realize equity and see a cheap turn where I can continue only if I improve (pair a K or pick up a spade) or if he shows weakness. I will fold to a large shove but will re-evaluate on the turn if the board or action changes in my favor.

I check. K♠8♠ missed the board — no diamonds and no real backdoor spade draw — so I have basically just a king-high with very little showdown value on 2♦-4♦-2♣-J♥. Flash is short-stacked and opened wide preflop and fired a small flop bet that I called; his range here includes trips (2x), single pairs, and diamond draws. Betting now will either be called by better hands or induce a shove from a short stack that I should fold to, so checking keeps the pot under control. Plan: check and fold to a large shove; if he checks back I get to see a cheap river and can bluff or value-bet only if I improve or pick up a clear read.

I have improved to top pair with the Jack on the turn. The board is 2-4-2-J with two diamonds. Minni checked to me again after calling my flop continuation bet. Given the heads-up nature of the game, top pair is a premium hand here. I will bet 800 into the 1200 pot for value and to charge any diamond flush draws or straight draws she might be holding.

Fold. The board (2♦ 4♦ 2♣ J♥) and action (button open, small flop c-bet, turn bet 800 into a 2000 pot) are consistent with a wide value range plus many hands that already beat lone king-high (pocket deuces, small pairs turned into value bets, or diamond draws). I have K♠8♠ with no diamonds and essentially zero fold-equity or meaningful draw: only 6 clean river outs (3 kings + 3 eights) ≈ 13% equity. Facing a required equity of ~28.6% to justify a call (800 into a 2000 pot), calling is -EV. Raising to 1600 is also unattractive — it either folds out bluffs infrequently or induces an all-in from the short stack when I’m behind. Preserve the healthy stack and fold.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 2♦6♣ — a weak holding but perfectly fine to open in heads-up. Calling 50 is passive and leaves me out of position; a tiny min-raise (to 200) just invites more calls/3-bets. I’ll open to a standard 3x size (raise to 300 total). That size gives good fold equity against Flash’s likely defending range (he’s shown mixed passivity), keeps the initiative, and is consistent with my previous open sizing so I don’t reveal the hand. If he folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable flops and avoid bloating the pot with trash. If he 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate and fold this weak holding.

I am in the Big Blind with 4c 7d. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised to 300. Given the current stack sizes and the hand's weakness, I should not be defending with 7-4 offsuit, especially when I'm out of position against a player who has shown aggression throughout the match. I will fold and wait for a better spot to try and rebuild my stack.

In Hand #68, I am on the Button (Small Blind) with King of Hearts and Four of Diamonds. My stack is significantly smaller than Minni's (5,575 vs 34,275), meaning I need to find good spots to accumulate chips without overcommitting with marginal hands. K4o is a mediocre hand, but heads-up it has reasonable value for a blind-stealing attempt or to see a flop. Given that Minni has been aggressive, I will raise to 300 to exert some pressure and take control of the hand, rather than just limping or folding.

Heads-up vs the button (SB) who opened to 300. I have A♣Q♥ in the big blind — a very strong hand heads-up and ahead of a wide SB opening range. Villain is short (~5,325) relative to my stack, so I want to seize initiative and isolate while leaving room if they shove. A standard 3-bet accomplishes that: it gets value from worse aces and pocket pairs, generates fold equity, and lets me play a pot with position if called. I’ll raise to 1,200 total (about 4x the open). If called I’ll c-bet favorable boards; if villain jams I’ll be prepared to call given AQo’s equity vs a wide shove range.

I am heads-up and currently hold King of Hearts and Four of Diamonds. My stack is quite shallow (about 53 BBs) compared to Minni's massive stack. After my initial raise to 300, Minni has 3-bet to 1,200. K4o is a very weak hand to continue against a 3-bet from an aggressive opponent who has shown the ability to punish shorter stacks. Calling 900 more out of position (or even in position, but facing a strong range) with a marginal King and a poor kicker is not a winning strategy here. I will fold and wait for a better spot to double up.

I hold 3♠7♦ — a very weak, disconnected offsuit hand. I’m the small blind/button and can fold, call 50 to see a flop, or raise to 200. Although position helps, this hand has poor equity and terrible post‑flop playability versus a normal big‑blind defense range. Raising to 200 is overly spewy and calling 50 is a marginal loss-making play long term. With a large healthy stack (34,625) and an unknown but willing opponent, the +EV play is to conserve chips and fold this marginal holding (forfeiting the 50 SB).

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. My stack is relatively short at 5325 chips (about 53 big blinds) compared to Minni's 34525. Raising is the standard play here to build the pot and take control of the hand. I will raise to 300.

Facing a button open to 300 while I’m in the big blind with T9o heads-up. Pot is 400 and I must call 200. Flash’s effective stack is ~5075 while I have 34,525 — I can apply pressure without committing my tournament. T9o is a strong heads-up hand (good connectivity and decent equity vs a wide SB opener). Calling keeps me out of position and gives the button free initiative postflop. A modest 3-bet to 1500 (total) (i.e. add 1200 more) achieves three things: (1) generates fold equity and will pick up the pot often against weaker holdings, (2) narrows Flash’s continuing range to hands I play well against postflop, and (3) is small relative to my stack so it avoids unnecessary variance if he decides to shove. If Flash shoves, I’m happy to call given his wide opening range. Raise to 1500 to take the initiative and maximize expected value in this spot.

Ace-Jack offsuit is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. Although Minni's 3-bet is quite large (5x my open), I have position and a hand that is well ahead of a typical 3-betting range in this format. Folding would be too conservative. While my stack is relatively short (around 50BB), calling allows me to evaluate the flop in a high-equity situation with an SPR of about 1.3, making it likely I will commit my stack if I hit any piece of the board or even on dry boards where I remain ahead of her bluffs.

I’m first to act on Qs-Jc-6d with Td9h — I have an open-ended straight draw (Ks or 8s complete) = 8 clean outs (~31% to hit by river) plus a tiny backdoor diamond. Pot is 3,000 and Flash’s effective stack is only ~3,875. C-betting as a semi-bluff here makes sense: it takes the initiative, folds out a lot of unpaired broadways and weaker holdings that called my 3-bet, and builds the pot when I hit. I choose a half-pot bet (1,500): large enough to generate meaningful fold equity against his calling range but small enough that if he jams I can comfortably call given my stack. Plan: bet 1,500 to pressure, and if he shoves I will call because my draw equity + stack depth justify it.

I have middle pair (Jacks) with an Ace kicker on a board of Q-J-6. The pot is currently 4500 and Minni has bet 1500, leaving me with 3875 chips behind. My remaining stack is quite small relative to the pot (SPR of less than 1). Given that I have a piece of the board and the top kicker for that pair, folding is not an option in a heads-up match. Calling is possible, but given the texture of the board and my stack size, pushing all-in is the most effective way to realize my equity and potentially force a fold from hands like lower pairs or straight draws (K-10, 10-9). I will commit my remaining chips.

Board Q♠-J♣-6♦ with T♦9♥ gives me an open-ended straight draw (Ks or 8 completes) — ~8 clean outs, ~31% to hit by the river. The pot is 8,375 and it costs me 2,375 to call; that means I need ~22% equity to break even (2,375 / (8,375+2,375) ≈ 22%), so my draw has sufficient immediate pot odds. Flash’s check-raise polarizes to strong made hands and some bluffs, but his effective stack is small and raising here would commit more chips without substantially improving fold equity. I’ll call to realize my equity (and because I’m prepared to call a shove given the percentages), then re-evaluate on the turn — fold to extreme aggression if I miss, or press/fish for value if I hit.

Heads-up on the button with 3c9c: marginal but playable and suited. I want to take the initiative and use post‑flop position rather than just completing. Opening a standard sized raise (about 3x the big blind) gives me fold equity versus many of BB’s wide defending hands, keeps the pot manageable if called, and preserves lots of maneuvering room with our deep stack. Plan: if called, play position — c-bet dry, realize equity on club/connected flops, and control the pot or fold to heavy aggression on bad boards. If Flash 3‑bets big I’ll usually fold this hand; I may consider a call to small 3‑bets depending on sizing. Raise to 300 total (I’ve already posted 50 SB, so this is a normal heads‑up open).

I am in the Big Blind with J6s, which is a suited hand with some playability. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 400), this is a standard defend. J6s is strong enough to see a flop against a wide button-opening range, even though I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

On this flop of Q-7-4 rainbow, I have missed completely with my Jack-Six of spades. I have no pair, no draw, and only two overcards to the lower board cards. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, has a range advantage on this queen-high board. Since I am out of position and have no real equity, checking is the standard and most prudent play to see how Minni reacts.

I was the preflop aggressor in heads-up and this flop (7c Qh 4s) is very dry. Flash checked, so a continuation bet will fold many unpaired hands and take advantage of my positional initiative. I have backdoor club equity with 3c9c and some blockers, so a small-to-medium c-bet preserves fold equity while keeping the pot manageable. Pot is 600 — betting ~50% (300) puts pressure on a wide BB calling range without committing too much. Plan: if Flash folds, take the pot; if called, proceed in position (barrel when I pick up equity or on safe turn cards, check/control vs heavy resistance). If Flash raises large I will release; I may call a very small raise depending on sizing and implied odds.

On this dry Queen-high board, my Jack-Six of spades has missed completely. I have no pair, no straight draw, and only a very weak backdoor flush draw. Minni, as the pre-flop aggressor, has a range advantage here. I am out of position and calling with Jack-high in hopes of hitting a Jack or a Six is not profitable against a persistent opponent. Given the stack sizes and the lack of equity, the most disciplined play is to fold and look for a better spot.

I have been dealt pocket Aces (Ad Ah), the strongest starting hand in No-Limit Texas Hold'em. My stack is 10,400 compared to Minni's 29,450. I need to build a pot to maximize value and try to double up. Raising to 300 is a standard size for me this game and should entice a call or a re-raise from Minni, who has been playing aggressively.

Facing a 300 open from the button (SB). I hold 8♠9♣ — a playable, connected hand heads-up with decent equity vs a wide SB opening range. Stacks are deep but I’m out of position postflop, so I prefer to keep the pot controllable and realize equity rather than 3-bet a marginal offsuit hand. Calling 200 more (300 total) gives ~3:1 pot odds and lets me see a flop to continue or fold to aggression; I’ll defend here and evaluate on the flop.

Flop: 7d 9h Qs. I hold 9c 8s — second pair with a mediocre 8-kicker and only a backdoor straight possibility. I'm out of position versus the preflop raiser (button) who opens a very wide range heads‑up and will have lots of Qx and overcards here. My priority is pot control and information: I want to avoid bloating the pot OOP with a marginal made hand that gets crushed by many of the raiser’s value combos. Checking lets them bluff or barrel with worse/air and keeps weaker hands in to pay me off; if they bet I can call reasonable sizes and re-evaluate on the turn, folding to a large raise. If they check back I’ll often take a stab on the turn or continue with a value bet later. For these reasons I check now.

I have pocket Aces on a board of 7d-9h-Qs. While the board has some connectivity for straights (like T8 or J10), I currently have the best hand most of the time. I'll bet 350 to extract value from any Queens, Nines, or straight draws in Minni's range and to protect my hand from giving a free card.

Flop (7d 9h Qs): I have second pair (9c) with a weak 8-kicker. Button opened very wide preflop and c-bet small (350 into 950), which is a standard probe that contains a lot of bluffs, floats and thin value hands. Calling 350 keeps the pot controlled out of position, gives me good pot odds (~3.7:1) to realize equity and to get value from worse pairs and bluffs, while avoiding bloating the pot with a marginal hand by raising. My plan: call and re-evaluate on the turn — call small/medium continuation bets, fold to a large raise that polarizes the opponent to strong Qx/sets, and extract value if the turn improves my relative strength.

I have 9♣8♠ on 7♦ 9♥ Q♠ T♦ — second pair with a weak 8‑kicker plus an open‑ended straight draw (J or 6, and two remaining 9s for trips) — roughly 10 outs (~22% to improve on the river). I’m out of position, the pot is 1,300 and Villain (Flash) has a much shorter stack (~9,800). Villain opened wide preflop and made a small flop c-bet, so his range contains a lot of bluffs/float hands but also Qx and some T/x. With a marginal made hand + draw I want pot control and information rather than bloating the pot OOP. Checking keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, lets me realize my equity, and forces Villain to take the next step — I can call small/medium barrels, fold to large polarized raises, and extract value on the river if I improve or Villain shows weakness. Therefore I check the turn and re-evaluate on Villain’s action.

The 10 of diamonds is a highly coordinated card that completes several potential straights (KJ, J8) and significantly improves many hands in Minni's calling range like Q10, J10, or 89. While I still have an overpair and the nut flush draw (Ad), the board is now very dangerous. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, avoid a potential check-raise on this wet board, and ensure I see the river to potentially realize my flush equity or get to showdown cheaply if my Aces are still the best hand.

I have 9c8s on 7d-9h-Qs-Td-6h — the river 6 completes a 6-7-8-9-10 straight for me (my best five). That hand is very strong heads-up; the only realistic way I lose is to specific higher-straight combos that include both a J and an 8 (J8 combos). Flash’s line (preflop raise, small flop c-bet, check on turn, check on river) looks like a wide, fairly weak-to-medium holding range (Qx, Tx, one-pair hands, missed draws, floats). With the pot only 1,300 and Villain having ~9,800 behind, the best play is to extract value with a small-to-medium river bet that gets called by worse pairs and Qx/Tx hands rather than checking and letting showdown go for free. A half-to-2/3 pot sizing keeps worse hands in and minimizes the chance of folding out calls; it also prices Villain into thin calls while still protecting against an unlikely raise. I will bet 700 chips (≈54% pot) to get value from Qx/Tx/pairs and to deny free shows to missed draws. If Villain raises large or shoves, I’ll re-evaluate — but given the line, a large raise would put them on a polarized range (likely a very narrow combo like J8 or a bluff), and my straight is good vs almost everything else.

The board runout of 7d-9h-Qs-Td-6h is extremely coordinated. The Ten on the turn completed straight possibilities like KJ and J8. The river 6h doesn't change much except making a 98 or 87 straight less likely to be the winner compared to the J8 or KJ. Minni's lead of 700 into a 1300 pot (making it a 2000 pot) is a common value bet size. While pocket Aces are a strong starting hand, on this board they are merely one pair. Minni's range for calling pre-flop and on the flop includes many hands that improved significantly on this board, such as Q-10, J-10, 8-9, or even slow-played sets. However, Minni could also be betting a worse Queen or turning a hand like 9x into a bluff given my check on the turn. Given the odds (700 to win 2000), I only need to be right about 26% of the time. While I am likely beaten, folding Aces for one bet in a heads-up match after showing weakness on the turn might be too exploitable. I will call to see the showdown, as my hand still beats all bluffs and some thin value bets like QJ or Q8.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with K♣? wait K♥3♦ — a marginal hand but in heads-up play it’s perfectly reasonable to open. Pot is 150 and I only need to put in 50 to see a flop, but limp/pass lets BB see a cheap flop; open-raising takes the initiative, builds fold equity, and leverages my big stack vs Flash’s shorter 9,000 stack. I’ll make a standard 2BB open (min raise to 200). This sizing is small enough to keep pots controllable but high enough to often pick up the blinds or isolate. If Flash jams or makes a large 3-bet I’ll give K3o respect and fold; if he calls I’ll play postflop in position and c-bet most favorable flops.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 4s, which is a suited hand with some playability. Minni has raised to 200, making it 100 more for me to call into a pot of 300. This gives me 3:1 odds, which is generally enough to defend the big blind against a small min-raise, especially with suited cards. Given my stack size relative to Minni's, I need to be cautious, but calling here is standard to see a flop and potentially realize equity with a flush draw, straight draw, or even a pair.

I have flopped trip nines on a board of Kd-9d-9c. Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor, I will check to her to allow her to continue with a range that likely includes many Kings or diamond draws. Checking protects my range and allows me to either trap or check-call to keep the pot manageable while holding a very strong hand.

Flop: Kd 9d 9c; I hold Kh 3d — top pair but with a weak 3-kicker and a backdoor diamond. Flash checked; heads‑up that check is often weakness or a trapping line with 9x. I want to extract value from worse Kx and pocket pairs and charge any backdoor/overcard draws while keeping the pot controlled against a possible 9. A half‑pot c‑bet (~200 into 400) gives value, protection and fold equity without bloating the pot against Flash’s 8.9k effective stack. If he check‑raises big or jams I’ll give weight to trips/strong two‑pair and fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously.

I have flopped trip nines with a four kicker. Minni has bet 200 into a 400 pot. At this stage, calling is the most strategic play to keep her range wide. If I raise now, I likely fold out her bluffs and only get called by hands that have me beat (like K-9 or a better nine) or strong draws. By calling, I allow her to continue betting with air or a King on the turn, maximizing my potential value while keeping the pot manageable given my stack size relative to hers.

I have trip nines with a four kicker on a board of Kd-9d-9c-Jd. While trip nines are very strong, the turn card is quite coordinated, completing a potential diamond flush and adding straight possibilities (like QT). I am out of position against Minni, who has a significantly larger stack. Checking here is the best play to control the pot size and observe her reaction. If she checks back, I can evaluate on the river; if she bets, I can decide whether to call based on the bet sizing and the likelihood that she has a flush or a better nine.

Board: 9♦ K♦ 9♣ J♦. I hold K♥3♦ — top pair but a very weak kicker and now a three-diamond board. On the flop I c-bet 200 and was called, which is consistent with a range of Kx (often better kickers), pocket 9s/trips, one-card diamond draws and medium pairs. The turn J♦ makes that range worse for me: trips (9x) and any two-card diamond combos now have huge equity or are already beating me, and many Kx still dominate my weak kicker.

Betting here builds the pot and gives strong hands (trips/flush) profitable raises/jams; I don’t want to be forced into a big fold or call against a likely better holding. With a big stack advantage I can control the size of the pot and see a safe river. I’ll check to keep the pot down, evaluate Flash’s river action, and then call small river bets for thin value or fold to large raises (since I’m often behind to the continuing range).

On the river 2h, the board is Kd-9d-9c-Jd-2h. I have trip nines with a four kicker. Minni checked back the turn, which suggests she might have a medium-strength hand like a King, a weak Nine, or a missed draw. While trip nines are strong, my kicker (4) is very weak, and any other Nine (A9, Q9, T9, etc.) or any diamond flush beats me. By checking, I can potentially induce a bluff from her missed draws or get to a cheap showdown against her Kings. If I bet, I risk being raised by a better Nine or a flush, putting me in a difficult spot given my stack size. I will check and evaluate her response.

I have K♥3♦ on a 9♦ K♦ 9♣ J♦ 2♥ board — top pair but a very weak kicker and three diamonds on board. Flash’s line (check-call flop, check turn, check river) is consistent with several hands that beat me now: trips (9x), KJ/two‑pair, or any diamond that made a flush on the turn. It’s also consistent with missed diamond draws or medium Kx, but there’s enough combination of hands that dominate me that I don’t want to bloat the pot. The pot is only 800 and my stack is much deeper, so the lowest-variance +EV play is to check and take the cheap showdown. If Flash instead bets a small amount I can call for thin value; if he jams or makes a large raise I’ll fold my weak kicker. For now: check to showdown.

I am on the button with King-Jack offsuit (KJo), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy dictates a raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. Given the stack sizes and the 50/100 blinds, a 3x raise to 300 is appropriate.

Facing a 300 button open from SB; I’m in the BB with 5♥7♦. I’ve already posted 100, need 200 more to call and the pot is 400. Calling 200 into 400 gives ~3:1 pot odds (break-even ~25% equity). Heads-up the button’s open is wide, so this hand has enough raw equity to justify a call rather than folding. I’m out of position postflop, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a light 3-bet/raise (min-raise to 500 is available), especially with such a marginal hand. I’ll call to 300 total to see a flop; plan is to proceed only with clear pair/draw equity or fold to strong continued aggression. Preserve my big stack and avoid unnecessary multi-street wars with air.

Check. On Js Qd Ad with 7♦5♥ I have no pair and no meaningful draw (only remote runner-runner diamond possibilities). Opponent opened wide from the button so will c-bet often and his range contains many A/Q/J and diamond hands that dominate me. Betting out OOP with pure air just bloat the pot and hands I can’t beat; checking controls the pot and preserves my large stack. Plan: if he checks back I take a free turn; if he c-bets a very small size (≈≤1/3 pot, ~200) I may call to see the turn for my slim equity, but I will fold to larger c-bets (≈1/2 pot or more) or any shove.

I have middle pair (Jacks) with a gutshot straight draw to the nuts on a very heavy board (Js Qd Ad). While I have showdown value, the board hits many of the hands Minni would call a pre-flop raise with (Ax, Qx, and better Jacks). Checking back allows me to control the pot size and see a turn card for free, keeping the pot manageable while I have significant equity to improve.

Turn pairs my 7 (I have 7♦5♥) — I now have a middle pair on J♠ Q♦ A♦ 7♣. Both players checked the flop, which makes it more likely the opener missed or is pot-controlling rather than holding a confident A/Q/J (those hands tend to c‑bet). Heads‑up I’m ahead of a good chunk of his checking range (Kx, Tx, Jx, pure air/bluffs). I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand, but I should bet for thin value and to deny free cards to broadway/straight/diamond combos. Bet about half the pot (300 into 600) — small enough to keep the pot manageable and fold to big aggression, large enough to get called by worse. If he raises big, I’ll strongly consider folding; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river.

I have middle pair (Jacks) and a gutshot straight draw to the nuts on a broadway-heavy board. After the flop checked through, Minni's 300 bet into the 600 pot represents a polarized range of either a slow-played strong hand, a marginal made hand like a Queen or a Seven, or a draw. Given I am getting 3:1 odds to call and have multiple ways to improve on the river (hitting a Ten for the straight, or potentially a King or Jack), calling is the most profitable play. Raising would likely only be called by better hands, and folding would be over-folding given my pair and gutshot equity.

Both checked the flop, I bet 300 on the turn (half pot) and Flash called. The river K completes a very high Broadway board J–Q–A–7–K. I only have a single pair of 7s — showdown-worthy versus bluffs and very small pairs, but dominated by a large portion of Flash’s calling range (Ax/Qx/Jx and any Kx that now beats me). Given his line (preflop raise, check on flop, call turn) he likely has many hands that will either check-back or call a small bet on the river rather than fold. Betting here would rarely fold out better hands and would get called by most hands that beat me, so it’s a negative‑EV bluff/value spot. Pot control and taking this to showdown is the right play. I check and go to showdown rather than bloating the pot or risking a costly bluff.

On the river, I have improved to two pair (Kings and Jacks) with an Ace kicker. However, the board is extremely coordinated (Js-Qd-Ad-7c-Kh). Any Ten makes a straight, and any Ace in Minni's hand (which is very much in her calling range pre-flop and her betting range on the turn) makes a higher two pair (Aces and Kings, Queens, or Jacks). When Minni checks the river after betting the turn, she is likely either giving up on a bluff, pot-controlling with a marginal hand like a Queen, or potentially trapping with a straight or an Ace. Given that a bet from me is unlikely to be called by a worse hand and could potentially face a check-raise from a superior hand, checking back to realize my showdown value is the most sensible play.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with Js3h. Heads-up I should be opening very wide — J3o is a playable steal attempt because I have position post-flop and can apply pressure. Flash (BB) has shown an aggressive but sometimes fold-prone style; a standard 2xBB open (raise to 200 total) gives me fold equity while keeping the raise size economical vs his 10k stack. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (preserve stack with a marginal holding), call small 3-bets that are reasonable and play postflop using position — c-bet dry, check/float on coordinated boards, value-bet if I pair my J. Calling 50 would concede initiative; folding is too tight. I raise to seize initiative and maximize long-term EV.

I am in the Big Blind holding J3o. Minni has raised from the Small Blind/Button to 200, making it 100 more for me to call. The pot is currently 300. Given the pot odds (3:1) and the fact that we are heads-up, J3o is a weak hand but sometimes defensible against a small raise. However, Minni has been quite aggressive and currently holds a significant chip lead (nearly 3:1). Looking at my stack of 10,000, I want to avoid bleeding chips with marginal hands out of position. J3o performs poorly post-flop, especially against a wide but strong range from a button raiser. While calling is an option, folding is the safer, more disciplined play here to wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

In this heads-up scenario, I am on the button with King-Deuce offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in heads-up play, but I am significantly outstacked by Minni (9,950 vs 29,900). While I could fold to conserve my stack, raising to steal the blinds or see a flop with a King-high hand is standard. I'll put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to maintain aggression and put pressure on the big blind.

Heads-up vs Flash (button/SB) who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with Js8c. Pot is 400 and it’s 200 more to call (I already have 100 in). Flash has a wide opening tendency at this size, so defending a wide range is correct. Js8c is marginal but playable heads-up — it has a Jack blocker and reasonable showdown potential to hit top pair or backdoor draws. Out of position, I want to keep the pot controlled and see a flop rather than build a bigger pot with a marginal hand. Plan: call to see a flop, fold to a large 3-bet preflop, and evaluate postflop (fold vs heavy C-bets on blanks, extract value/raise when I hit).

Heads-up on a relatively dry 9♦ T♠ 6♥ flop and I hold J♠8♣ — one overcard and some backdoor improvement potential but no pair or strong draw. I’m out of position so I should prioritize pot control and realize equity rather than bluffing into a wide but sticky opener. Flash opened wide preflop, so he will c-bet often, which makes a check-first line + calling small bets + folding to large denial bets the most +EV line. Plan: check and evaluate. If Flash checks back we keep the pot small and can continue on a turn pair or good card; if he bets a small-to-medium c-bet (~≤½ pot) I’ll call to see the turn and fold to heavy turn pressure unless I improve; if he fires a large overbet I’ll fold since J8 has poor equity against a polarized bet. If I pair my Jack or pick up a backdoor straight/club on the turn I will become aggressive for value.

On a mid-connected board like 9-10-6, the big blind's calling range has many hits. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I should maintain my initiative with a continuation bet. My King-high has some equity, and a half-pot bet (300) can often fold out weaker air or hands that missed entirely. If Minni calls or raises, I'll likely check down or fold on later streets unless I improve to a King.

Heads-up out of position on a relatively dry 9♦ T♠ 6♥ flop with J♠8♣. Flash is a wide opener and will c-bet often; the bet size (300 into the 600 pre‑bet pot) is about a half‑pot sizing. Calling 300 gets me to a 1:3 risk/reward (300 to win 1,200) — ~25% pot odds. My hand has realistic river equity (pair or straight draws: J/8/7/Q outs and some backdoor possibilities) — roughly ~24% chance to improve by river and additional fold equity against some of his weaker holdings. Out of position I want to keep the pot controlled and realize equity rather than turning a marginal hand into a big bluff/raise spot. Raising OOP here with J8 is too thin (it invites a shove or a shove‑call by lots of hands I’m behind), and folding misses too much equity against a wide range. Plan: call the half‑pot c‑bet, see a turn, and fold to heavy turn overpressure unless I improve; if I pair or make the straight I’ll shift to value/raise. If Flash checks back I’ll continue controlling the pot and evaluate on the river.

Board: 9♦ T♠ 6♥ 6♦. I hold J♠8♣, pot is 1,200, effective stacks: I 29,400 / Flash 9,400. Flash is a wide opener who c-bet the flop (I called). The turn paired the board, which both reduces some bluff/fold equity and increases the chance Flash has a 6 (or will check-back). I have no pair but decent single-card equity to improve on the river: 3 J + 3 8 + 4 Q + 4 7 = 14 outs (~30% to hit the river). Out of position with a marginal holding, my priority is pot control and realizing equity rather than turning this into a bigger, marginal bluff spot. Betting or raising OOP here is too thin — it opens me to a shove or calls from many hands I’m behind. Checking keeps the pot manageable, allows weaker hands to bluff/catch on the river, and lets me fold cheaply if Flash applies heavy pressure and I don’t improve. Plan: check now; if Flash bets the river I’ll fold to large aggression without improvement, call small-to-medium bluffs, and if I pair or make the straight I’ll pursue value accordingly.

The board has paired on the turn with a six, and Minni has checked to me again. My King-high has very little showdown value, but after Minni called my flop continuation bet, it's likely they have a piece of this board (a 9, a 10, or a straight draw). Betting again here is risky because Minni has been sticky in previous hands. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, keep my remaining stack intact, and get to see a free river card which might improve my hand to a pair of Kings or allow me to reach a cheap showdown if Minni continues to check.

River: I paired my Jack (J♠) on J♥ bringing me to top pair with an 8 kicker on 9♦ T♠ 6♥ 6♦ J♥. Pot is 1,200 and both players checked the turn; Flash is a wide, sticky opener who checks back thin. In heads-up this is a clear thin-value spot — many worse hands (Tx, 9x, weaker Jx, missed broadways and backdoor draws) will call a small river bet. I want to extract value while keeping the pot controllable and avoiding committing a huge portion of my stack to a polarized check-raise. I will bet small (~1/3 pot) to get value from worse hands and deny a free showdown. If Flash check-raises large or jams, that line is typically polarized to very strong hands (trips/full house/better Jx) so I will heavily weigh folding to an all-in shove. Action: small value bet 400 chips.

On the river, the board is 9d-Ts-6h-6d-Jh. I have King-high (Kc-2s). Minni has bet 400 into a 1200 pot. While the pot odds are 4:1, my King-high has almost no showdown value. Minni's check-call on the flop suggests they have a piece of the board (like a 9 or a 10), and the Jack on the river potentially gives them a better pair or completes a straight (8-7). Even a missed draw like 8-7 still makes a straight using the 6-7-8-9-10. Since almost every hand in Minni's calling range beats me, I will fold and preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button with deep effective stacks. J♠4♣ is marginal, but in heads-up position it comfortably sits inside my opening range as a steal/bluff candidate and has some showdown value (can make top pair with a J). I want to take the initiative and avoid giving the BB great odds to limp/see a flop for cheap. A standard heads-up open to 3x BB (300 total) is consistent with table dynamics and keeps pressure on Flash while not bloating the pot with a weak hand. Plan: fold to a sizeable 3-bet preflop (J4o plays poorly out of position in 3-bet pots); if called, use position to continuation-bet on many flops, abandon when I miss heavily and face strong resistance, and extract value when I connect.

I am holding 5d 2c in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. This hand is extremely weak and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack sizes and my goal to maximize my stack over the 150-hand duration, it's better to fold now and wait for a stronger starting hand rather than commit chips with a sub-optimal range.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with a 92.5 BB stack. My hand, 9-7 offsuit, is a standard opening candidate from the small blind in heads-up play to maintain pressure and capture the blinds. Minni has been playing aggressively, but folding this hand pre-flop would be too tight. I will stick to my established opening size of 300 to see if I can take the pot down now or play a post-flop game where I have position.

Flash opened to 300 from the SB/button. I’m in the Big Blind with A♣2♠. The pot is 400 and I need to call 200 (300 total). Heads-up A2o is worth defending — I only need ~33% equity to make the call and against a wide SB opening range A2o has comfortable equity plus an Ace blocker. A min 3‑bet to 500 is very small and would bloat a pot out of position with a marginal hand; if I wanted to 3‑bet I’d prefer a larger sizing or a stronger ace. I’ll call to keep the pot manageable, realize equity, and play the flop carefully out of position.

Flop Td 5h 3d — I’m OOP with A♣2♠: only Ace‑high, no pair and no diamond to block the two‑diamond texture. Flash opened wide preflop so he’ll c‑bet a high frequency, but leading out OOP with a marginal hand is unnecessary and bloats the pot when I have little equity. I’ll check to control the pot, induce bluffs, and use the Ace as a blocker to some top‑pair combos. Plan: if he makes a small c‑bet I can call for pot odds and to realize my equity; fold to large-sized aggression.

On a board of Td-5h-3d, I have two overcards to the small pairs and a back-door straight draw. Minni checked, which is standard for the big blind after calling a pre-flop raise. This board is relatively dry, although it has a diamond draw. I will place a continuation bet of 300 to represent a Ten or an overpair, or simply to take advantage of the position and apply pressure to likely missed hands like small suited connectors or low pocket pairs that might fold now.

I’m OOP with A♣2♠ on Td 5h 3d. I checked to control the pot and Flash’s 300 is a relatively small c‑bet into this pot, indicating a wide/low‑confidence continuation range. Calling 300 into a 900 pot gives me ~25% pot odds; my chance to pair by the river is ~24% and the Ace acts as a useful blocker to many of his top‑pair hands. I’ll call to realize equity and keep the pot manageable, then fold to large turn aggression or re‑evaluate if he slows down.

I’m OOP with A♣2♠ on Td 5h 3d Qc after I checked/called the flop. The Q on the turn is a dangerous overcard that improves a lot of the button’s wide c‑betting range to Qx and also keeps many strong broadway combos; I have only Ace‑high and no diamond to deny flush draws. Betting out here as a bluff OOP will bloat the pot with very little fold equity (Qc hits his range) and worsens my decision tree if I face resistance. Pot control and seeing a river is the prudent line — check and evaluate the river/hero’s equities. Plan: check, call a smallish river bluff/bet if I get correct pot odds or fold to large aggression unless I pair up.

I have 9-high with no draw on a T-5-3-Q board. After my continuation bet on the flop was called, the Queen on the turn is a significant card. While it favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor, Minni's call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board or a draw. I have zero equity here. Betting again into a big stack who has shown willingness to call could be risky. I will check behind to see a free river and control the pot size.

Heads‑up, pot is 1,200 and I hold A♣2♠ on Td 5h 3d Qc Jd. Flash opened and made a small flop c‑bet (range includes broadways and diamond draws), we floated, both checked turn — his check turns his range more toward marginal high cards, missed draws, or slowplays. Crucially the river J♦ completes three diamonds on board (T♦,3♦,J♦), so many hands that were drawing now beat my Ace‑high, and Q/J/T combos also beat me. Betting here would be a thin bluff with limited fold equity (he already showed passivity on the turn and may call small bets with many beating hands or with any made pair), so I prefer pot control and go to showdown. I’ll check and fold to a sizable river raise; I’ll only call a very small stab that gives correct pot odds for a bluff catch.

I have 9-high with no showdown value. The board now contains three overcards to the flop (T, Q, J) and three diamonds. Minni has checked the river after calling my flop continuation bet and checking the turn. While the Jack of diamonds is a scare card, Minni's check-call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board or a draw that may have improved. Given the stack sizes and the fact that I have zero equity, I will check behind and concede the pot rather than attempting a high-risk bluff into a larger stack.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 4♦5♣ — marginal but playable: connected cards, some straight/two-pair potential and I have postflop position. Pot is 150 and I only need to put 50 more to call, but raising to the minimum (200 total) is the +EV play here: it applies pressure to a wide BB defending range, gains fold equity, and lets me take the initiative to c-bet favorable flops. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll respect it and fold this hand; if he calls I’ll use position to play postflop (c-bet many textures, proceed cautiously when I miss). The raise is small relative to both stacks so the risk is limited while maximizing steal potential.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 3c, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Although I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, 8-3 offsuit performs very poorly post-flop and I will be out of position.
