Given the stack depths and my current position in the tournament, there is no need to defend with such a weak hand against an aggressive opponent like Minni.

In a heads-up situation, Q2o is a marginal but playable hand from the button, especially since I only need to call 50 more to see a flop. However, Minni has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively. Given my stack size of 85.5 big blinds, I can afford to play this hand profitably by limping in or calling the small blind to see if I hit a pair or a strong draw, but I should be ready to fold if the action gets too heavy post-flop or if Minni raises significantly pre-flop. In this specific spot, I am the small blind and it is 50 to call. I will call to see a flop.

Heads-up, I’m in the big blind with 2♠6♣ — a very weak, unsuited hand. Flash (SB) has already called to see the flop; pot is only 200 and there’s nothing to call. Against a competent player with an 8.5k stack I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with junk; raising here gives them good odds to call and puts me OOP in a bloated hand. I’ll check to see a flop for free, preserve my deep stack, and only invest further if I hit or get a clear fold equity spot on the flop.

Heads-up out of position with 2♠6♣ on A♥9♣8♥ — I missed completely and have essentially no equity. Flash limped preflop and checked the flop, so his range includes many Aces, middle pairs, and heart draws that will call or raise a bluff. Pot is small (200) and Villain’s stack is large enough to call/raise any light bet, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with junk. Best play is to check and concede the street — fold to any significant bet and wait for a better spot to apply pressure or realize equity.

I have Queen-high on an Ace-high board with no draws. Minni checked, which indicates she might have missed, but my hand has virtually no equity if called. In a limped pot, there is no reason to bluff with a hand this weak when I can simply take a free card and hope to improve or just give up the hand without losing more chips. I will check back.

Turn (5♣) doesn't improve 2♠6♣ — I have essentially no showdown value and only modest raw equity (~10 outs to something, ~22% to improve, many of those still lose). Flash limp-called preflop and checked the flop, so his range is heavy with Ax, 9s/8s and some heart draws that will call or raise light. Pot is tiny (200) and I'm out of position; betting/raising here with garbage just bloats the pot and hands me a hard decision OOP. Best play is pot control: check and fold to any meaningful bet, then re-evaluate the river if he checks back.

In a limped pot, the board of A-9-8-5 is quite dry and favors a range that contains an Ace. Since Minni checked both the flop and the turn, she likely doesn't have an Ace or a strong pair. However, my hand (Q-high) has very little showdown value and no draws. While a bet might occasionally win the pot, there is no need to risk chips in a tiny pot with no equity. Checking back is the most prudent play to see a free river and potentially improve or win at showdown if she has pure air.

Board A♥ 9♣ 8♥ 5♣ 4♣ and my hand is 2♠6♣ — no pair, no straight, no flush (only 6-high). Pot is tiny (200) and Flash has been passive (limp/call then checked flop and turn), so my fold equity is minimal: a small bet gives him great odds to call and a large bet only invites a raise from any Ace or pair. With a deep stack and plenty of hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better bluff or value spot is correct. I’ll check and concede the showdown; I will fold to any river bet.

On a board of A-9-8-5-4 where both players have checked every street, it is highly likely that neither of us has an Ace or a significant pair. While Queen-high has some showdown value against complete air, it is not strong enough to bet for value, and there is little reason to bluff into a 200-chip pot when I can see the showdown for free. Checking back is the most logical choice.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 8♥5♥ — a playable suited connector heads-up that plays very well in position. Pot is 150 and I only need to put 50 more to see a flop, but opening is preferable: I want to seize the initiative, widen my range and realize my positional advantage. Flash (BB) has ~8600 (deep but not huge) so I’ll make a standard 2xBB/min-raise to 200. This sizing pressures his blind, keeps the pot controllable versus his deeper stack, and allows me to c-bet favorable flops or realize equity on wet boards with my backdoor/straight/flush potential. If he 3-bets big or shoves I’ll fold (5-8s has poor equity versus a shove/strong 3-bet range); I’ll only entertain calling smaller 3-bets to see a flop in position.

I am in the big blind with 6d 4c, a weak hand. Minni has raised to 200 from the button (small blind). I only need to call 100 more to see a flop. While the hand is weak, the pot odds are 3:1 (100 to win 300). However, given my current stack size relative to Minni's and the fact that I am out of position with a marginal hand that doesn't play well post-flop, folding is a standard and safe play to preserve chips for better spots.

In a heads-up situation, King-high on the button is a standard opening hand. Minni has a significant chip lead, so I should continue to play a solid range but also look for opportunities to chip up. Folding here would be too tight. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and take control of the hand early.

Heads-up on the big blind vs a standard button open. Q9o is well within my defend range here — I only need ~33% equity to justify the call (200 more into a ~400 pot), and Q9o has that against a wide CO/BTN opening range. I want to keep the pot manageable and realize my equity rather than inflate the pot preflop against an opponent with ~83BB who can shove or fold. Plan: call to see the flop; continue aggressively if I hit (pair, two pair, strong draws) or float against predictable C-bets; exercise pot control and fold to heavy action on completely missed boards. A tiny 3-bet/raise is unnecessary and a large 3-bet would risk committing too many chips without a premium hand. Call (300 total committed).

I have Qh9c on Jh-3c-4d: I missed but hold one overcard (Q) and a backdoor heart. The board is very dry and Flash opened wide from the button, so he'll c‑bet a high frequency. I'm out of position, so I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than bluffing OOP. I'll check to see his action — call small continuation bets to try and hit the turn (or float and bluff later), but fold to heavy pressure or large raises. Checking preserves chips and keeps better spots open.

I am heads-up on a dry board of Jh-3c-4d. As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here to put pressure on my opponent and take down the pot. King-high has some showdown value but is vulnerable to many turns. By betting 300 (half the pot), I represent a range that includes Jacks, overpairs, or stronger, which should force folds from most of Minni's non-pairing hands.

Heads‑up vs Button wide open — Qh9c on Jh‑3c‑4d is a clear marginal but defendable hand. Flash’s c‑bet is small (300 into a 900 pot), so I’m getting ~25% break‑even equity (300/(900+300)=25%). Against a wide BTN continuation‑bet range my Q9o has at least that much equity (one overcard, backdoor heart, and the ability to float OOP). I’m out of position so I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than polarize by raising. Plan: call the 300 to see a turn; fold to heavy pressure if he barrels big and I miss, but look to seize the pot if he shows weakness or I pair up. Call 300.

I missed the turn with Qh9c on J♥-3♣-4♦-7♣ — I only have Queen/nine outs (≈6 cards ≈13% to pair on the river) and I’m out of position. Flash is a wide, button opener who c‑bet small on the flop; that makes checking for pot control the right line rather than firing out OOP with a marginal hand. Checking keeps the pot small, lets me realize equity and see how he responds — I can call a small turn barrel if the price is right (and he’s likely bluffing), but I’ll fold to large turn aggression. If he checks back I get a cheap river to try to improve or bluff in a better spot. Check to control the pot and re‑evaluate on the river.

On the turn 7c, the board remains relatively dry, although some straight and flush draws have emerged. Minni called my flop continuation bet, which suggests she has at least a piece of the board, such as a Jack, a small pair, or a draw. Since King-high has some minimal showdown value but is unlikely to fold out a Jack or a better hand with a single bet, I will check back. This keeps the pot manageable, given the significant stack discrepancy, and allows me to see a river card for free.

Check. The river pairs the board (J♥ 3♣ 4♦ 7♣ J♣) and I hold Q♥9♣ — essentially a queen‑kicker on a paired board. Flash led with a small flop c‑bet and then checked the turn and now checked the river; that line looks more like a missed or medium showdown hand than a clear value slow‑play, but his wide BTN opening range still contains a lot of A‑x/K‑x/Q‑x hands that beat my queen kicker. Betting for thin value here is unattractive: many hands that will call (A/K highs, Qx) beat me, and many worse hands will fold. A small bet risks a check‑raise or an uncomfortable shove from his ~8k stack; the pot is only 1,200, so preserving my stack and taking the free showdown is the higher EV line. I’ll check back and go to showdown rather than bluff or thin‑value bet.

On the river, the second Jack appears on the board (Jh 3c 4d 7c Jc). Minni has checked for the third time (flop, turn, river), having called a continuation bet on the flop and seeing a check on the turn. My King-high has very limited showdown value, but it is unlikely to fold out any pair (3s, 4s, 7s) or a better King/Ace-high if I bet. Since I checked the turn, my range looks capped, and a river bluff is less likely to be credible against a chip leader who has been sticky. Checking back allows me to see the showdown for free and preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with 2♠8♦ — a very weak hand, but heads-up you should open a very wide range and use position. I want to take the initiative, apply pressure and avoid letting the BB see a cheap flop for free. Raising to 300 (about 3x the BB) is a standard, constructive size: it gives decent fold equity, keeps the pot controllable with a marginal holding, and prevents frequent multi-street calls that a tiny min-raise would invite. If Flash 3-bets big, this holding is usually a fold; if he calls, I’ll c-bet favorable flops and use position to realize my fold equity. Total commitment preflop: 300 chips.

I am holding Jack-Ten suited (Jh Th), which is a premium hand in heads-up play. It has great playability and connectivity. Minni has raised to 300 from the button, which is a standard opening size. While I could 3-bet this hand to take the initiative, calling allows me to keep the pot size manageable while out of position and see a flop with a hand that flops very well. I have a healthy stack of 91 big blinds, so there is no need to over-commit pre-flop against a larger stack.

I have middle pair (tens) and an open-ended straight draw (any King or Eight completes the straight). This is a very strong board for my range, but it also hits the button's raising range well (QJ, KQ, KJ, etc.). Checking to the aggressor is the standard play here. If Minni bets, I will most likely call or potentially check-raise depending on the sizing, as I have a significant amount of equity and some showdown value. For now, checking is the best way to control the pot while out of position.

I was the preflop aggressor in a heads‑up spot so I should c‑bet a large fraction of flops to leverage fold equity. The flop Qs‑9h‑Td is somewhat connected and hits a calling range, but it also contains many missed hands (Ax, low pairs, Kx, small connectors) that will fold to pressure. My holding 2♠8♦ has almost no showdown value — only a gutshot to a Jack (4 outs) and a backdoor spade possibility (~16% to improve by river). That small raw equity isn’t worth checking back and giving up fold equity. I’ll make a standard half‑pot continuation bet (300 into a 600 pot) to try to take it down now or keep the pot manageable if called. Plan: take the pot if he folds; if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (value/defend only when I improve or the turn helps my range) and fold to sizable check‑raises since my hand has very little equity versus a strong range.

I have middle pair (Tens) and an open-ended straight draw (any King or Eight). This is a very strong hand on this texture. Minni's half-pot bet is standard for a continuation bet. While I could check-raise to capitalize on my high equity, calling is a safer way to control the pot size out of position and realize my equity. I have plenty of outs to improve to the nuts or a very strong two-pair/trips, and I currently have some showdown value with the pair of Tens. I will call and see how the turn develops.

On the turn, I have completed a King-high straight (9-T-J-Q-K). This is a very strong hand, though I must be wary of AJ, which would make the Broadway straight. Since I am out of position and Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand, I will check to her. This allows her to continue betting with her perceived range, which likely includes sets (KK, QQ, TT, 99), two-pair hands (KQ, KT, QJ), or even a lower straight like J8. If she bets, I will look to raise to build the pot and extract value.

Check. I opened wide preflop and c-bet the flop, but the K on the turn completely misses my 2♠8♦ — I have no pair and only a J-gutshot (4 outs, ~8.7% to improve). Flash called the flop and now checks, which looks like pot control with at least Q/T/9 or a slow-played K; many of his calling hands still beat me. Betting the turn would be a thin bluff with limited fold equity and a real chance to run into a call or a check-raise that I must fold to. With deep stacks and almost no raw equity, I prefer to control the pot size and see a free river. Plan: re-evaluate on the river — if a J comes I can pursue value or a bluff catch; if a blank and he bets, I fold.

I have completed a King-high straight (9-T-J-Q-K). While AJ makes the Broadway straight and beats me, Minni's check on the turn makes it less likely she holds the nuts. On the river, the 4 of clubs is a brick. I am first to act and should bet for value, as checking might lead to Minni checking behind with her one-pair or two-pair hands. A bet of 800 (two-thirds of the pot) should be able to get called by a King, Queen, or even a Ten.

Fold. The river lead of 800 into a 2000 pot gives me only ~28.6% break‑even equity (800 / (2000+800)). I have 2♠8♦ — no pair, no river improvement and essentially zero showdown equity versus a sensible value-heavy calling/raising range (Qx, Tx, 9x, Kx, Js for a straight, two‑pair combinations). Flash’s line (call flop, check turn, small river bet) is exactly the sort of line many value hands — and thin value bets — take to extract a cheap call; it’s also consistent with a blocking bet from K/Q/T. Raising as a bluff (min raise to 1600 or larger) is unattractive: a min‑raise will often be called by hands that already beat me, and a larger jam is unnecessarily risky without blockers or showdown equity. I’ll preserve my big stack and avoid a marginal hero call/bluff in a low‑EV spot.

I am on the button with 2h 4d, which is a very weak hand. The pot is currently 150 and it costs 50 to call. However, out of position post-flop and with such a weak holding, folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips for better spots, especially given the significant stack disadvantage against Minni.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with A♠5♣. Pot is 150 and it’s 50 to call or 200 to raise (min). A5o is a very playable heads-up opener and I want the initiative and position — raising to 200 (2x BB) builds the pot, denies Flash cheap flops, and gets value/fold equity against his wide BB defending range. Flash’s effective stack (9,650) is much smaller than mine, so a standard 2x open preserves postflop maneuvering room while applying pressure; I’ll c-bet favorable A-high or dry boards and re-evaluate if he 3-bets or shoves. Therefore I open-raise to 200.

With T5o in the Big Blind, I am facing a small raise to 200 (100 more to call) from Minni. While the hand is weak, the pot odds are 3:1 (100 to call a 300 pot). However, given my stack size relative to Minni's and the fact that I've been playing conservatively, I'll choose to fold this marginal hand rather than play out of position against a dominant stack with a weak kicker.

I'm on the Button/Small Blind with A3o. In heads-up play, an Ace is a very strong starting hand and should almost always be played aggressively to gain the initiative. A 3x raise is my standard opening size, which is 300 total.

Flash opened to 300 from the button (small blind) — in heads-up that’s a very wide opening range. I hold 4♥2♠, which is one of the weakest hands. However, calling 200 more (300 total committed including my BB) gives me good pot odds (about 2:1) to see a flop and keeps the pot small. I won’t 3-bet as a bluff with 4-2o: it’s too unplayable out of position and could get me in trouble if Flash jams or wakes up with a strong hand. Plan postflop: play very cautiously OOP — continue only with a pair or a very favorable runout, otherwise fold to significant pressure (large C-bets or turn shoves). With my big stack advantage I don’t need to gamble here; defend cheaply and re-evaluate on the flop.

I hold 4♥2♠ on A♦ Q♠ 5♠: no pair and only a one-card backdoor spade possibility (very low equity). Flash opened wide from the button so they will c-bet a lot, but I should not invent action with this junk out of position. My stack is large relative to Flash, so pot control is the priority — check and let them show their hand. I’ll fold to any meaningful continuation bet (only consider calling an extremely small probe bet that gives correct odds). If Flash checks back I can evaluate turn for a delayed bluff or a free card, but for now checking is the disciplined play.

I have flopped top pair with my Ace. Although my kicker is weak, top pair is usually the best hand in heads-up play. The board has a Queen and two spades, which provides several drawing possibilities for my opponent. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to extract value from a Queen, a 5, or a flush draw, while also protecting my hand and maintaining the initiative.

Situation: pot 900, opponent bets 300 on A♦ Q♠ 5♠. I hold 4♥2♠ — no pair, only a very slim backdoor (need both turn+river spades ≈ 4% to make a flush). Calling 300 into 900 requires ~25% equity to break even. Against a reasonable BTN c-bet range (lots of A-x, Q-x, spade combos and some thin bluffs) my equity is far below that (single digits to low teens at best). 3‑bet/bluff here is ill-advised: out of position with one of the worst possible hands, I’d be risking a lot to fold out hands I still beat rarely. With a healthy chip lead I should conserve chips and pick better spots. Action: fold.

Heads‑up on the button (small blind) I should be wide and aggressive — opening to steal the big blind is standard. Q2o is marginal but has some showdown value and position, so I’ll make a small standard raise to apply pressure while keeping the pot controllable. If the BB folds I pick up the 150 pot; if they call I can play the flop in position and c‑bet favorable textures. I’ll fold to a large 3‑bet (or any shove) with this weak hand, and only continue vs a small 3‑bet if pot odds and range considerations justify it. Raise to the minimum open (200) to maximize fold equity and keep options postflop.

I am in the big blind with 8s7s and the pot is 300 total, with 100 more to call after Minni's 200 raise from the button. 8-7 offsuit is a reasonable hand to defend in a heads-up situation against a small button raise, especially with a stack size of nearly 100 big blinds. Calling allows me to see a flop cheaply and realize some equity, though I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand.

On a flop of 7c-Th-9h, I have bottom pair (sevens) and an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: any 6 or Jack). This is a very strong hand to play from the big blind. I will check to the aggressor (Minni) to see her action. Given my pair and the straight draw, I have significant equity and intend to continue if she bets.

Heads‑up as the preflop raiser with Qh2d on 7c Th 9h (opponent checked), this is a textbook continuation‑bet spot. I have two overcards and a backdoor heart (needs both turn+river) — not a made hand but good blocker to some of Flash’s calling hands and enough equity to bluff. Flash defended the big blind and checked; his range is wide and contains a lot of hands that will fold to a credible c‑bet (small pairs, weak broadways, many missed connectors). I’ll make a modest c‑bet for fold equity and to keep the pot controlled: 200 into the 400 pot (~50%). Plan: if called I’ll reassess on the turn and only continue aggressively with a pair or a clear improvement; if I face a large raise I’ll fold Q2o and preserve my deep stack for better spots.

On a flop of 7c-Th-9h, I have bottom pair (sevens) and an open-ended straight draw (any 6 or Jack). Minni bet 200 into a 400 pot. With bottom pair and a strong draw, I have significant equity and the pot odds to continue. Calling is the most prudent play here to keep the pot controlled while out of position, allowing me to potentially hit my straight or improve to two pair/trips on the turn.

The turn card is the Ace of Hearts (Ah), which is a significant scare card. It completes potential heart flushes and strongly favors the pre-flop aggressor's (Minni's) range. I currently hold bottom pair (7s) and an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: any 6 or Jack). Given that I am out of position and the board has become increasingly dangerous with three hearts and an overcard Ace, checking is the most prudent action. This allows me to see how Minni reacts to the Ace and potentially realize my equity for free or at a reasonable price if she bets, while avoiding bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I opened and c‑bet the flop as planned and Flash called. The turn A♥ is a scare card — the board is now 7♣ T♥ 9♥ A♥. I hold Q♥2♦: I have not paired and I only have four hearts (one card short of a flush). Per my pre‑turn plan I only continue aggressively with a pair or a clear improvement; I don’t have either. The Ace improves Flash’s calling range (Ax, top pairs, heart combos) and his check can be either pot‑control with a made hand or a trap with a flush — a pure bluff bet here will often be called by better hands and fold only the worst, so it has poor EV. With deep stacks and an 800‑chip pot it’s better to control the pot and realize equity. I’ll check, keep the pot manageable, and reassess on the river: call small bets with reasonable showdown hands or fold to sizable raises (and only bluff the river if it gives a very favorable texture).

On the river, the board is 7c-Th-9h-Ah-2c. I have bottom pair (sevens). Minni checked back on the turn Ace, which suggests she likely doesn't have an Ace or a flush. However, she could still have a better pair like a Nine or a Ten. My hand has some showdown value against her missed draws or total air, but betting is unlikely to get a better hand to fold or a worse hand to call. Checking is the most logical play to reach showdown cheaply.

The river 2♣ pairs my 2♦ — I now have a weak pair (deuces) with a Q kicker. Flash checked the river and the pot is only 800 while both stacks are deep. This is a classic thin‑value / fold‑out spot: I beat a lot of his missed broadways and weaker one‑pair/air hands that will check behind, and I block some of his Qx combinations. I’ll make a small value bet to extract calls from worse and to take the pot when he folds. I’ll size it conservatively (300) so worse hands call but a raise is exploitable: if Flash raises large I’ll assume he has a real hand (A, T, 9, two‑pair/trips or a rare two‑heart flush) and fold. If he calls I go to showdown and accept the result.

On a board of 7c-Th-9h-Ah-2c, I hold bottom pair (sevens). Minni's line is raise pre-flop, bet flop, check turn, and a small bet on the river. Her check on the turn Ace of hearts strongly suggests she does not have an Ace or a flush, as those hands would typically bet for value or protection on such a coordinated board. Her small river bet of 300 into 800 (giving me roughly 3.6-to-1 odds) looks like either a thin value bet with a marginal hand (like a nine or a small pocket pair) or a bluff with missed draws (like KJ or QJ). Given the excellent pot odds and the inconsistency of her turn check with a strong hand, I will call to see if my pair of sevens is good against her bluffs or missed draws.

I am on the button with A5o. In a heads-up situation, an Ace-high hand is strong enough to open for a standard raise to exert pressure and build the pot. Minni has a significant chip lead, but a standard 3x raise is the correct fundamental play here to take control of the hand.

Heads-up against Flash (button/SB) who opened to 300. I have Td8c — a playable, connected but marginal hand. Pot is 400 and I need to add 200 (300 total committed) which gives ~33% pot odds; versus a wide SB opening range my hand has reasonable equity and very good postflop playability with deep effective stacks (~100+ BB). I don’t want to 3-bet light OOP with T8o and inflate a pot I’ll be out of position in, so I’ll defend by calling to see a flop and realize equity / outplay on later streets.

I have T♦8♣ on 2♦8♦T♠ — two pair (Tens and Eights) in a HU pot. Flash opened widely from the button, so his range contains many single-pair hands and diamond draws. I want to both extract value from one-pair hands (Tx, 8x, weaker tens/eights, some broadways) and charge/deny free cards to diamond draws. Pot is 600; a bet of ~2/3 pot (400) accomplishes that — it keeps initiative, builds a pot when I am likely ahead, and forces opponents to pay a meaningful price to chase. If he calls I can re-evaluate on the turn and keep extracting; if he raises big he will usually have a set or a very strong diamond hand and I will re-assess (call a small-to-medium raise, fold to an overbet shove if the line and sizing strongly polarize to monsters). Betting 400 maximizes immediate value while protecting against the worst equity-turning runs.

On the flop of 2d-8d-Ts, Minni has led out for 400 into a 600 pot (making it 1000). I hold Ad5s. My hand has one overcard (the Ace) and a backdoor nut flush draw. While I have the Ad, which is a good blocker, my actual hand strength is low against a lead on this board. However, in heads-up play, Minni might be leading with a wide range, including air or weak draws. Given the pot odds (400 to call 1000), I can profitably call one street to see if I improve on the turn or if Minni slows down, especially since the Ad gives me some equity if a diamond comes. Folding immediately is too tight, but raising is unnecessary without a better made hand or a more robust draw.

I have T♦8♣ on 2♦ 8♦ T♠ 9♣ — top two pair heads-up. Pot is 1,400 and Villain is deep (≈9,950). The 9 on the turn does give a few new hands that beat me (T9 two-pair, J7/JQ straights, sets like 99/88/TT), but Villain’s calling range from the button includes many one-pair hands and diamond/straight draws that I dominate. I want to extract value from worse tens and lone eights and charge diamond and gutshot draws while keeping the initiative. A sizing around 2/3–3/4 pot balances value and protection, so I’ll bet 1,000 chips. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river; if he raises large it will usually be a polarized monster line and I’ll fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing.

On the turn, the board is 2d-8d-Ts-9c. I hold Ad-5s, giving me Ace-high and the Ad blocker. Minni has led out on both the flop (400) and the turn (1000). The turn card (9c) is highly coordinated, connecting with the 8 and 10 already on the board. Any Jack now has a straight draw, and hands like J7, QJ, or 78/89 have improved or have significant equity. With only three clean outs (the remaining Aces) and no straight or flush draw (I only have one diamond in hand and two are on the board), calling 1000 chips into a 2400 pot is not mathematically sound. Given Minni's significant chip lead and persistent aggression this hand, it is highly likely I am behind, and there is no guarantee an Ace on the river would even be the best hand. I will fold to preserve my stack for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a very wide range. J♣2♦ is marginal but perfectly raisable in position to seize initiative and pick up the blind — limp/calling surrenders initiative and gives the BB cheaper equity. Min raise is 200, I'll raise to 300 total (3x BB; that means I add 250 more on top of my SB). This size buys fold equity without bloating the pot with a weak hand; if Flash 3-bets big I'll fold, if he calls I'll use position to c-bet favorable flops and play cautiously on high-card/dangerous boards.

I have KQs in the Big Blind. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. My opponent, Minni, has a significantly larger stack and has been aggressive, but KQs is well ahead of her standard opening range. I could 3-bet here to take initiative, but given the stack depths and the fact that I'm out of position, calling and seeing a flop is a solid, lower-variance play to preserve my tournament life while still playing a premium hand.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a very dry rainbow board (Qs-3d-6h). This is a premium hand in heads-up play. Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor, I will check to her to allow her the opportunity to continue her aggression with a wide range of hands, including bluffs that she might fold if I were to lead out. I am prepared to call a bet or even check-raise depending on the sizing, but for now, checking is the most strategic way to play this strength.

I opened wide on the button and the BB just called — flop Q♠ 3♦ 6♥ is very dry and I completely missed with J♣2♦. I have no pair and practically no realistic draws (one diamond in hand + one on board cannot make a flush by river), so this is a pure bluff candidate. As the preflop aggressor heads-up I should c-bet high frequency on dry boards to seize the pot. I’ll use a small sizing (~1/3 pot = 200) to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot controllable if called. Plan: if Flash folds we win; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check/realize equity or barrel selectively on safe runouts); if he raises pot-sized or larger I’ll strongly consider folding this holding.

I have top pair, top kicker (King) on a very dry rainbow board (Qs-3d-6h). Minni made a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 600. This small sizing is often used on dry boards to induce calls or to keep the pot small. Since my hand is very strong but unlikely to be improved by many turn cards, and there are few draws out there, calling is a good option to keep her bluffs in. However, raising for value is also very viable here to build a pot while I'm likely ahead of her entire range. Given her aggressive history and the dry board, I'll call this street to hide the strength of my hand and allow her to continue bluffing on the turn.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a very dry board (Qs-3d-6h-2h). After calling a small continuation bet on the flop, checking the turn is the most effective way to extract value from Minni's bluffs and wide range. Leading here would likely only be called by hands that have me beat or a similar Queen, whereas checking allows her to continue betting with air or marginal hands. I am comfortably ahead of most of her range and will look to call a bet to keep the pot manageable while I'm out of position.

Heads-up I was the preflop aggressor (opened to 300) and c-bet the very dry Q♠–3♦–6♥ flop for 200; Flash called. The turn 2♥ pairs my 2♦ so I now have bottom pair (deuces) with a J kicker. Flash checked the turn which shows some weakness or pot-control. Against his check range (air, floats, some A/K/X overcards and occasional Qx pot-control) this is a turn to extract thin value and deny free cards rather than just give him a free river. My hand is real but vulnerable, so I don’t want to bloat the pot — a half-pot bet charges worse hands (A/K highs, Jx floats, small pairs), gets folds from pure air, and keeps the pot manageable if called. I will bet 500 (≈50% pot). Plan: fold to a sizable check-raise (I’m beat), call single raises of reasonable size and re-evaluate on the river (check back scary runouts, value or barreling depending on the river and his action).

I have top pair with the second-best possible kicker (King) on a very safe board of Q-3-6-2. I have played this hand passively so far by checking and calling to under-represent my strength and induce bluffs from Minni, who has shown a high frequency of aggression. Her 500 bet into a 1000 pot on the turn is a standard continuation. Raising here would likely fold out her bluffs and only get called by hands that have me beat (like a set or AQ) or perhaps a weaker Queen. By calling, I keep her wide range intact and allow her to potentially fire a third barrel on the river with air or marginal hands, maximizing my value while keeping the pot manageable out of position.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a very safe board. Throughout the hand, I have checked and called to maintain a deceptive line and induce bluffs from Minni, who has shown significant aggression in previous hands. On this river, the 4 of spades is a relatively blank card. By checking, I allow Minni to continue her aggression with her air or marginal hands that she would likely fold if I were to lead out with a bet. If she checks behind, I am very confident my hand is the winner, and if she bets, I have a very easy call for value.

I have J2 (bottom pair) on a Q–3–6–2–4 board. Flash called my flop and turn bets and then checked the river — a line consistent with medium-strength hands (Qx, 6x, 3x, small pairs) or a trap (any 5 made a 2–3–4–5–6 straight). Betting here will only get value from worse hands (A/K highs) while risking a costly check-raise or call from many hands that already beat me. The river is a “scary” runout that completes straights; earlier plan was to check back such runouts with a vulnerable pair. I’ll check and take the cheap showdown rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I have Qd 8d on the button (Small Blind). This is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation, offering both straight and flush potential, as well as high card value. With only 50 more to call into a pot of 150, I will definitely play this hand. To take the initiative and exert pressure, I will raise to 300 total, which is my standard open size this match.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300. I have J♦9♦ — a suited, connected hand with good equity and playability heads-up. Pot is 400 and I must put in 200 more (so 300 total) — roughly 2:1 pot odds (~33% equity needed). The button's opening range is wide here, so J9s has >33% equity and can realize a lot of implied odds on a deep stack. I don't want to 3-bet light out of position into a deep-stacked opponent (a small raise would invite calls or a 4-bet and put me in awkward spots), so the best line is to flat-call, see a flop, and proceed aggressively when I hit (pairs, draws) or control the pot / fold to strong aggression when I completely miss. Call to 300.

Heads‑up versus the preflop opener on a very dry 7♣-4♥-2♣ flop. I have J♦9♦ — two overcards and backdoor diamond equity but no made hand. The raiser’s range is wide here, so a continuation bet will pick up a lot of pots (small pairs, weak broadways, missed hands) and also realizes my equity when called. I don’t want to bloat the pot with only overcards, so I’ll make a medium c‑bet (~½ pot) to apply pressure and keep options. If villain makes a big raise I’ll give up; if he calls I can continue only when I improve (pair or diamond), otherwise control the pot on later streets.

On a dry flop of 7c-4h-2c, I have missed completely with my Qd-8d, holding only overcards and no significant draws. Minni has led out (donked) for 300 into a 600 pot. This 'donk' bet often represents a piece of the board (like a 7, 4, or 2) or a speculative draw that she wants to define her hand with. Given that the board is quite low and likely connects better with a Big Blind defending range than my Small Blind opening range, and considering I have no equity outside of hitting a Queen or Eight, calling here is unprofitable. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity to engage.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 8♠4♥ — a weak hand but position is a big advantage. Limping gives Flash easy odds and invites check-raises; opening a small standard steal to 200 (2BB) applies pressure, preserves fold equity and keeps the pot manageable if I face resistance. If Flash 3-bets big I'll fold this hand (poor equity and dominated by typical 3-bet ranges). If he calls, I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flop textures and control pot size.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack of Diamonds and Five of Diamonds. Minni, who is currently the chip leader, has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. This is a relatively small raise, and I only need to call 100 more to see a flop. J5 suited is a weak hand, but given the pot odds (3:1) and being heads-up, it is worth defending against a button raise, especially since I have the potential to make a flush or a straight. I'll call and see how the flop develops.

On a flop of 9h-8d-2h, I have missed completely, holding only one overcard (Jack) and a backdoor diamond flush draw. Standard play in the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor is to check and evaluate their continuation bet. Given Minni's significant chip lead and aggressive history, I should not look to contest this pot without improvement.

Heads-up on the button with 8♠4♥ on 9♥ 8♦ 2♥. I have middle pair (8s) with a weak kicker but I’m in position and Flash checked after calling preflop. This is a good c-bet spot: the board is relatively dry aside from two hearts, and my pair is ahead of a lot of his checking/calling range (missed overcards, small pocket pairs, weak Ax). A half‑pot bet (~200 into 400) extracts value from worse hands, denies equity to backdoor draws, and preserves fold equity without bloating the pot. If he folds I take it down; if he calls I can use position to control the turn; if he check‑raises big I can fold since I’m likely behind. Action: small value c-bet to 200.

On a board of 9h-8d-2h, I have absolutely nothing but one overcard and some very remote backdoor draws. Minni's 200-chip bet into a 400-chip pot is small, but my hand is simply too weak to continue with. I have no pair, no draw, and I am out of position. Attempting to float or bluff here against the chip leader is not a profitable strategy.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with J8o. This is a standard opening hand in heads-up play. Minni has a significant chip lead, so I want to maintain a consistent opening range without being overly reckless. Raising to 3x the big blind is my standard opening size.

Facing a standard SB open to 300 heads-up. I have Js6d — a marginal but playable hand vs a likely wide button opening range. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 more (total 300). Calling keeps the pot under control out of position, takes advantage of the opener’s wide range and the decent immediate pot odds (~33%), and avoids getting into tricky big pots with a weak kicker by 3‑betting. With effective stacks ~100bb I can realize equity postflop and play aggressively when I hit a jack, two pair, or good draws; otherwise I can fold to heavy pressure. Action: call to 300.

I have J♠6♦ on J♦ 3♠ A♥ — top pair with a weak kicker on an Ace‑high, fairly dry board. Flash was the preflop raiser from the button (wide opening range that contains a lot of A‑x and broadway combos), so many of his hands beat me. I'm out of position, so I want to control the pot size and let him take the initiative — betting here mostly gets called by better (A‑x) or folds small pairs, and there are no real draws to charge. Plan: check and call a standard c‑bet to keep the pot manageable; re‑evaluate on the turn (call small/medium bets, fold to very large overbets or raise only with a strong read/improvement). Action: check to realize showdown value and induce bluffs or value bets from worse Jx.

I have flopped second pair (Jacks) on an Ace-high board. While this is a decent hand, the presence of the Ace is a significant threat given Minni's range for calling a pre-flop raise from the Big Blind. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, minimize my risk against a potential Ace or a check-raise, and keep my range wide. This allows me to see a turn card for free and potentially reach a showdown with a hand that has good marginal value.

I have top pair (J♠6♦) but a very weak kicker on a board that contains an Ace and now a Queen (J♦ 3♠ A♥ Q♥). Flash was the preflop raiser and checked the flop, which is consistent with a lot of A‑x, Q‑x or a passive/air line. The turn Q overtakes many of my holdings and leaves me behind to many reasonable parts of his opening range. Betting here would build a pot where I’m often behind and will get called by better or shoved on; out of position I prefer pot control. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable, induce bluffs/value bets from worse, call small/medium bets, and fold to large raises.

I am currently holding a pair of Jacks, which was the second pair on the flop but has now become the third pair on the turn with the arrival of the Queen. My opponent, Minni, has checked for the second time. While my hand has some showdown value, the board is quite dangerous with both an Ace and a Queen present. Betting here might force out weaker hands that I already beat, but it's unlikely to get a better hand to fold. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, avoid a potential check-raise, and get to see a free river card. This keeps my range wide and allows me to evaluate the final board state before deciding how to proceed on the river.

I hold J♠6♦ on J♦ 3♠ A♥ Q♥ 6♣ — two pair (Jacks and Sixes). Heads‑up this is a very strong river hand that beats essentially all single‑pair hands and many of the preflop raiser’s missed/medium combos; only a narrow portion of his range (AJ, AQ, QJ, JJ, 66, etc.) actually beats me. Flash showed passivity (checked flop and turn), which increases the likelihood he has a medium pair or missed hand rather than a trap. I want value from worse Jx, single A/Q pairs and stubborn calls, so I’ll bet for value but keep the size controlled to avoid bloating the pot against the rare monster. Action: bet ~1/2 pot (300).

On a board of A-Q-J-3-6, I hold a pair of Jacks (third pair). Pre-flop, I raised and Minni called. Both of us checked the flop and the turn, which heavily suggests that neither player has a strong Ace or a Queen. Minni's 300-chip bet on the river is a half-pot lead that could represent a thin value bet (like a Queen that checked the turn or a Jack with a better kicker) or a bluff designed to take down the pot after I showed weakness by checking twice. Given the pot odds (3:1), my hand is a strong enough bluff catcher in a heads-up scenario to justify a call, as Minni could be betting with any missed draws or lower pairs.

Heads-up on the button (I’m also the SB) with A♠T♠ — a strong, highly playable hand in position. The pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call, so open-raising is standard to seize initiative and extract value from worse Aces, broadways and suited connectors. I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3x BB) — big enough to get fold equity and build a pot with position, but small enough to keep postflop maneuverability against a 9.75k effective stack. Postflop plan: c-bet on Ace/Spade/T-heavy or coordinated boards for value, use smaller bets or check-back on dry boards to control the pot; call reasonable 3-bets and re-evaluate, 4-bet/shove only versus very wide or short-stack 3-bets.

I am holding K9o in the Big Blind against a 3x raise from Minni on the Button. K9o is a strong hand in heads-up play, well above the average starting hand. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot that will be 600) and the stack sizes, this is a standard call to see a flop. I don't need to 3-bet here as K9o plays well enough post-flop and I want to keep the pot manageable while I am significantly out-chipped in the overall tournament standings.

I am in the Big Blind and it is my turn to act first on the flop. The board is 8c-8d-Ah, which is very dry and favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, as the Ace is a key card for the Button's raising range. With King-high (Kd9s), I have no pair and limited equity if I am called. Checking is the standard and most prudent play here to see how the Button (Minni) reacts to the board.

I raised preflop and was called; on A♠T♠ the flop A♠? (8♣ 8♦ A♥) gives me top pair, top kicker and range advantage as the preflop aggressor. Flash checked — this is a clear value/protection spot: bet to get calls from worse Aces, broadways and medium pocket pairs and to deny free cards. I’ll size to about half pot to extract value while keeping worse hands comfortable to call and avoiding bloating the pot too much vs potential trips (8x) or stronger A‑kickers that might check‑raise. If I get a big check‑raise I’ll re‑evaluate; if called I’ll proceed to the turn planning to barrel blanks or pot‑control on dangerous cards.

On a board of 8c-8d-Ah, I am holding King-high with no direct draws. Minni's continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot is a standard move on an Ace-high board that favors her pre-flop raising range. Since I have no pair and very little equity to improve to a winning hand on the turn, especially against an opponent who likely has an Ace or a pocket pair, there is no reason to float or bluff-raise here. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

In this heads-up scenario, J6 offsuit on the button is a marginal but playable hand. While it's not a premium holding, standard heads-up strategy dictates opening a wide range from the button to capitalize on positional advantage. Minni has a significant stack lead, so I want to avoid massive pots with mediocre hands, but folding pre-flop for just 50 more chips is too tight. Raising is the standard play to put pressure on the Big Blind, and a 3x raise is consistent with my previous play style.

Heads-up vs the button’s 300 open and I’m in the big blind with A♥2♦. Calling 200 (total contribution 300) gives me ~33% odds to see a flop, and A2o has enough equity vs a wide SB opening range to justify a defense. I’ll flat rather than 3-bet: out of position with a weak kicker, a 3-bet will often be called by better Aces or pairs and put me in tough multi-street spots. Keeping the pot small lets me realize equity and make more informed decisions on the flop — if I hit the Ace or pick up a good draw I can apply pressure; if I miss I can control the pot and fold to heavy aggression. Given Flash’s frequent 300 opens, a standard defend is the most +EV play here.

Heads-up on 4c 5h 7h with A♥2♦ and I'm out of position. I have Ace-high with useful blockers (I block many A-x and the nut-heart combos), a single-card gutshot to the wheel (any 3 makes A-2-3-4-5 — ~8.5% on the turn, ~16.4% by the river) and a backdoor heart flush (need both turn+river hearts — ~4.1%). Villain opened widely to 300 preflop, so his C-bet frequency is high and his range is often missed on this board. Checking is the correct, low-variance approach: keep the pot small, realize equity and call small-to-moderate continuation bets. Raising OOP with only Ace-high is too thin and will put me in tough spots against better Aces/pairs. Plan: check; call small bets (roughly <=1/3 pot) to see another card, fold to heavy aggression or large overbets.

On this 4-5-7 board with two hearts, I have Jack-high with a gutter-shot straight draw (needing an 8) and overcards. Minni has checked to me. This board texture is likely to have connected with many hands in her defending range from the big blind (any pair, straight draws like 6x or 8x). However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I should continue with a bet to represent a strong range (like 7x, 88+, or flush draws) and try to take the pot down now before the board gets more complicated. A half-pot bet of 300 should be sufficient to test her hand strength.

Flop: 4c 5h 7h. I have A♥2♦ — Ace-high with useful blockers (I block A‑x combos) plus backdoor wheel (3 completes A2345) and a backdoor heart flush (need both turn+river). Villain’s small 300 c-bet into a 900 pot (~1/3 pot) looks like a wide CB from a frequent opener. Pot odds: calling 300 into a 1200 pot requires ~20% equity — my hand has >20% vs a wide range and has reasonable showdown value. Raising OOP with just Ace-high is too thin and would put me in tough spots; I’ll call to realize equity, keep the pot manageable, and fold to significant raises. Plan: call now (total commitment 600), re-evaluate on the turn — continue with pairs/clean draws or check/fold to big aggression.

Turn comes Td — my hand (A♥2♦) is still only Ace-high. The backdoor heart flush is dead (needed two hearts after the flop), and my only realistic improvement is a 3 on the river (4 outs, ~8.7% equity). Flash is a wide preflop raiser who c-bet the flop small; that line contains a lot of missed hands that will fold to aggression but also a lot of pairs/top-pairs (T,7,5,4) that now connect with the turn. Betting OOP with thin Ace-high is too risky — I’ll often get called by better hands and face tough decisions vs raises. Pot-control is the priority: check and see how the button acts. If he fires a small turn bet I can call to realize my ~9% and potentially fold to big overbets; if he checks back I get a free river to re-evaluate. So: check to control the pot and avoid bloating it with a marginal holding.

The Turn Ten of Diamonds is a relatively blank card that doesn't significantly change the board texture, though it does add some higher straight possibilities. My Jack-high has no current value, but I still have a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8). Minni called the flop bet, indicating she likely has a pair or a draw. Given her stack advantage and recent tendency to call down, a second barrel here is unlikely to generate enough folds to be profitable. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and potentially realize my equity if I hit an 8 or possibly a Jack.

River 3♥ completes A-2-3-4-5 — I now have a 5-high straight (the wheel). Only a 6 in villain’s hand makes a higher straight (3-4-5-6-7), otherwise I beat essentially his whole continuing range (pairs, worse straights are impossible, most single-pair/top-pair hands). Flash opened wide preflop, c-bet the flop small and checked the turn — a line consistent with a lot of marginal pairs and missed broadways. A small value bet (~1/3 pot) extracts calls from 7x/5x/Tx and Ax hands that will pay, while keeping the pot manageable against the rare 6x that beats me. I’ll bet a small size to get value from worse and fold out pure air; if he jams I can re-evaluate but calling is reasonable given how few combos legitimately beat me.

On this river, the 3h completes both the flush draw and the A2 straight draw. I am holding Jack-high (Jh 6d). While the Jh is a decent blocker to some flushes, my overall hand strength is non-existent at showdown. Minni's lead for 400 into a 1200 pot (1/3 pot) looks like a thin value bet with a pair or a small completed draw. Since I checked back the turn, I have shown weakness, and she could be capitalizing on that, but even her bluffs could potentially be better than Jack-high (such as King-high or Ace-high). Given the board is highly coordinated (4-5-7-T-3 with three hearts) and her position as the massive chip leader, there is no merit in calling or attempting a high-risk bluff here. Folding is the most disciplined play.

9♦3♣ is a weak, offsuit hand, but this is heads-up and I'm on the button so my opening range should be wide. Raising preserves initiative and gives fold equity; postflop I'll have position which is a big advantage for bluffing and extracting value. Pot is 150 and calling 50 is cheap, but a standard 3x-ish open to 300 is a simple, effective size here — it pressures the BB, is not too large relative to stacks, and keeps my range wide. Plan: take it down preflop if Flash folds; if he calls use position to c‑bet on favorable (dry) boards and proceed cautiously on coordinated/drawy boards; fold to large 3‑bets since 9‑3o plays poorly facing heavy aggression. Raise to 300.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has been opening frequently from the button. My hand is well ahead of a standard button opening range. I will call the raise to see a flop and keep the pot manageable, as I am playing out of position.

I have flopped top pair with a nine kicker on an Ace-high board. While this is a strong hand heads-up, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most effective play to protect my range and allow my opponent to continue with bluffs or marginal hands. I will see how Minni acts before deciding whether to call or raise.

Heads-up on the button with 9♦3♣. Pot is 600 and Flash (BB) checked the flop A♦ Q♠ 8♥ (rainbow). My hand has no pair and negligible showdown equity, so my fold equity and initiative are the only ways to win this pot. This is a relatively dry board with two overcards but it's a common spot to c-bet versus a wide calling range. I want a small, efficient bet to fold out weaker holdings while keeping the pot controllable if called. I will bet ~1/3 pot (200). Plan: fold to a substantial raise (pot-sized or shove); if called, check the turn and re-evaluate — only continue if I improve or can credibly represent top pair.

I have flopped top pair on an Ace-high board with a Nine kicker. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play, but I need to be cautious as Minni's range can include better Aces or two-pair combinations. Minni's small bet of 200 into a 600-chip pot (33% pot) is a standard continuation bet that could be made with a wide variety of hands. Calling is the best option here to keep the pot manageable, retain Minni's bluffs in her range, and see how the turn develops without over-committing my stack while out of position.

On the Turn, the board is 8h-Ad-Qs-Th. I have top pair with a nine kicker. While this is a solid hand in heads-up play, the board is becoming increasingly coordinated, with potential straight draws (KJ, J9) and two-pair combinations (AQ, AT, A8, QT) now in play. Since I am out of position against Minni, who has been aggressive, checking is the most prudent move. It allows me to control the size of the pot, observe her action, and potentially induce a bluff from her wider range. I will evaluate my next move based on the size of any bet she makes.

Board is 8♥ A♦ Q♠ T♥ and I hold 9♦3♣ — no pair and essentially no showdown equity vs the calling range that checked-called my flop c-bet. The turn 10 is a dangerous card: it produces many made hands (T-x, QTs, A10) and even completes Broadway straights (e.g. KJ). Betting again would bloat the pot with air and would mostly fold only the very weakest hands (which likely already folded preflop or on the flop). I prefer pot control and keeping the initiative for a river decision: check to avoid getting raised off my chips, fold to significant turn aggression, and re-evaluate on the river (consider bluffing only on very favorable runouts).

On the river, the board is 8h-Ad-Qs-Th-2s. I have top pair with an Ace and a Nine kicker. After calling a standard continuation bet on the flop, the turn went check-check, which suggests Minni might have a marginal hand or was pot-controlling with a mediocre Ace or a Queen. While my hand is strong in heads-up play, it's not strong enough to value bet for three streets, especially with straight possibilities (KJ, J9) and various two-pair combinations on the board. Checking is the most prudent action; it allows me to see a showdown for free if Minni checks back, or evaluate based on her bet sizing if she decides to bluff or value bet a hand that might have me beat.

Check. My 9♢3♣ has no showdown value on A♦ Q♠ 8♥ T♥ 2♠. Flash’s line (call 200 on the flop, check turn, check river) is consistent with a lot of medium-to-strong hands — A‑x, Q‑x, T‑x, pocket pairs or even a slow‑played top pair — that will call a river bluff. I checked the turn already, so a river bet would be a weak, delayed story and will rarely fold the hands I beat while getting called by the hands that beat me. With the pot at 1,000 and Flash still deep (~8,450), a thin bluff is negative EV. I’ll keep the pot small and check to showdown; if Flash now bets I’ll fold this air hand.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with Ten-Four offsuit. While heads-up play often requires a wide opening range, T4o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially against a chip leader who has shown significant aggression. Given the stack disparity and the tendency of Minni to defend the big blind or play aggressively post-flop, I will fold this hand and wait for a more advantageous situation to commit chips.

Ad Kc is a premium heads-up hand on the button (small blind) with positional advantage postflop. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but I want value and fold equity while keeping stacks deep for postflop play. Raising to 300 (≈3x BB) is a standard heads-up sizing: it builds the pot, charges speculative hands, and often takes the lead. If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate (call or 4-bet depending on sizing); if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and use position to extract value. This sizing balances value, fold equity, and postflop maneuverability given my 30550 vs his 9300.

I am holding Q2o in the big blind. Minni has raised to 3x from the button. While I need to defend my blinds, Q2o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against a large stack advantage. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit my chips.

I am on the button with Q2o. In a heads-up situation, Q-high has some value, but Q2o is quite weak. However, the cost to call is only 50 chips more into a pot of 150. Given the stack depths, I will occasionally limp or fold here, but standard tournament play often involves folding the bottom range of hands. Minni has a significant chip lead. I will fold this weak hand and wait for a better spot to challenge Minni's large stack.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should be opening very wide. T6♥ is playable in position — can make top pair, has backdoor heart/straight potential and plays well postflop. The pot is only 150 and calling 50 is too passive; I raise to the min (200 total) to seize initiative, create fold equity, and keep the pot size manageable. Plan: if BB folds I scoop the blinds; if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable (dry) flops ~50–70% pot and play cautiously on coordinated or heart-heavy boards; if Flash 3-bets big I’ll usually fold T6♥, but I may call smaller 3-bets to realize equity given deep stacks.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play against a wide button opening range. Minni has min-raised to 200, giving me excellent odds (3:1) to see a flop. While 3-betting is an option for value, calling allows me to play a controlled pot out of position and realize my hand's equity against her likely wide range.

On a flop of 8-7-3 with two clubs, I have top pair with an Ace kicker. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Standard play as the Big Blind is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see if they continuation bet. This allows Minni to bet with a wider range of hands, including bluffs and draws, which I can then comfortably call or raise to extract value. Leading out (donk betting) here might scare away her weaker holdings.

Flop (3c 7d 8c) is fairly coordinated and actually favors the BB’s calling range (pairs, 7x/8x, club draws, suited connectors). I have T6♥: no pair, only a single-card gutshot to a 9 (4 outs) and a very unlikely double-heart backdoor. My turn/river equity vs a realistic calling range is small (~8–9% to hit on the turn, ~16–18% by river). C-betting here will get called too often by hands that beat me or have good draws, so I shouldn’t bloat the pot out of position. I’ll check to control the pot, realize equity, and keep options open — call small bets to see a cheap turn when appropriate, fold to large aggression, and consider betting on later streets if I pick up a pair or a favorable, dry card that gives fold equity.

I have top pair with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a relatively dry board. Minni checked back the flop, which often indicates a hand with some showdown value that didn't want to build a large pot, or a complete miss. By betting now on the turn, I can extract value from her middle pairs, small pairs, or drawing hands that she might have checked. A bet of 300 into a 400 pot is a strong value bet that also protects my hand against potential river cards.

Call 300. Heads‑up on the button with T♥6♥ I checked the flop to control the pot; on the turn Flash bets a small 300 into a 700 pot. I have 3 tens + 3 sixes + 4 nines = 10 outs (~10/46 ≈ 21.7% to improve by the river). Calling 300 into the 1000‑ish pot if I call requires ~23.1% equity, so raw equity is slightly behind. Because I'm in position, the bet is small, and I can both realize equity and use river position to bluff or extract value if I hit, a call is the lowest-variance, +EV line vs this sizing. I will call to see the river; if I pair or complete the straight I will bet for value; if I miss I’ll re-evaluate the river texture and opponent action and fold to heavy aggression.

On the river, the Ten is an overcard to my pair of eights. While I was ahead on the turn, Minni's turn call suggests she has a piece of the board or a draw. If she has a hand like T9, JT, or AT, she has now overtaken me. By checking, I can minimize losses against hands that improved and potentially catch a bluff from missed draws such as clubs or gutshots. My pair of eights still has significant showdown value in a heads-up match, especially given that Minni checked the flop, but betting now is unlikely to get called by many worse hands.

I paired my ten on the river (Th6h vs board 3c 7d 8c 2s Ts). We check-called the small turn probe, and Flash checked the river — a line consistent with missed club draws, broadways, or small pairs. This is heads‑up and my hand is strong enough for a value bet but not a hand I want to overcommit with if I face big resistance (two pair or a set beat me). A medium-sized bet (~60% pot) will: 1) extract value from worse one‑pair and high‑card hands (Kx/Qx/Jx/underpairs and some bluffcatchers), 2) charge missed draws, and 3) keep the price reasonable so villain has to fold many pure bluffs while still calling with worse. If Flash raises big I’ll assume he has two‑pair/set and fold; if he calls we usually beat his bluffs/weak pairs. Bet 600 into the 1000 pot.

I have a pair of eights with an Ace kicker. On the river, the Ten is an overcard, but the action so far suggests my hand might still be the best. Minni checked the flop, which often indicates she doesn't have a very strong hand like an overpair or a set. Her call on the turn suggests she has some piece of the board or a draw. While the Ten could have improved a hand like T9 or JT, it's also a card she might use to bluff if she missed a straight or flush draw. Given the pot odds and the heads-up context, my hand is too strong to fold against a single bet, making this a standard bluff-catch.

In Hand #100, I am on the button (Small Blind) with King-Deuce offsuit (K2o) and a stack of 8100 chips. Against Minni, who has a significant chip lead, I should maintain a reasonable range for my opens. K2o is a marginal hand, but in heads-up play, any King has decent equity from the button. Raising here is a standard play to take control of the hand pre-flop or win the blinds immediately. A standard 3x raise (to 300) has been my consistent sizing throughout the tournament, so I will stick with that to avoid giving off any timing or bet-sizing tells.

8♣2♠ (8h 2s) is one of the worst hands preflop — very little showdown value and terrible post‑flop playability. Flash opened to 300 from the button (I must call 200 more into a 400 pot, needing ~33% equity). Even heads‑up, this hand is usually dominated and has poor implied odds against a 78bb opponent. 3‑betting as a bluff here is unnecessary risk and calling leaves me in tough spots postflop. Preserve my large stack and fold to the raise.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 8♠7♥ — a playable, connected hand with good postflop playability and positional advantage. Pot is 150 (I’ve already posted 50); calling 50/passively lets the BB see a cheap flop and keeps their range wide. I want initiative and fold equity, so I’ll open to a standard 2x BB (total 200). That sizing pressures weaker BB hands, keeps the pot manageable vs a shorter-stacked opponent (Flash: 8150) and allows me to use position to c-bet and maneuver postflop. If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and SPR.

I am in the Big Blind with Ad 6d, a strong suited hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has opened from the Small Blind for 200, making it 100 more for me to call. Given my stack size of 8150 and the strength of my hand, calling is standard to see a flop, but a 3-bet is also viable to take the initiative. However, since Minni has been quite active, I will call and play the hand post-flop with a strong suited Ace.

On this flop, I have hit top pair with an Ace, though my kicker is relatively weak. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to Minni and see how she proceeds. Top pair in a heads-up pot is quite strong, so I intend to check-call most reasonable bet sizes to evaluate the turn, or potentially check-back to control the pot size given the kicker situation.

I missed this flop (A♣ Q♥ 2♠) with 8♠7♥ — no pair, no realistic flush (only one spade on board), just tiny backdoor equity. I was the preflop aggressor and I have position, so a standard heads-up continuation bet to take the pot now and fold out unpaired overcards / weaker holdings is the right play. Pot is 400; I’ll size to ~50% pot (200) to generate fold equity while keeping the pot manageable against a caller. If Flash folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and give up to a substantial check-raise since my hand has almost no showdown value. If he check-raises small, I can sometimes continue, but I’ll fold to large check-raises — this is essentially a bluff c-bet to steal the pot.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace. The board is dry with no immediate flush draws, though a gutshot is possible. Minni has been consistently aggressive, and a continuation bet on this texture is standard for her. While my kicker (6) is weak, top pair in a heads-up pot is too strong to fold to a single bet. I will call to keep the pot manageable and see how the turn develops, allowing Minni to continue with her bluffs if she has them.

On the turn, the board has paired with a Deuce. This is generally a neutral to good card for me, as it makes it less likely Minni has a set of Deuces. I still have top pair (Aces) with a weak kicker. After checking, Minni has checked back. This suggests she might be controlling the pot with a marginal hand like a Queen or a smaller pair, or she has completely missed. I will check again to continue controlling the pot size and to see if she tries to bluff the river, as my hand is a strong 'bluff catcher' and likely the best hand currently.

Board: 2♠ Q♥ A♣ 2♣. I hold 8♠7♥ — missed completely. I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the flop, Flash called. Turn paired the board (second deuce). Flash checks — this can be pot-control with A/Q or a float, or a trap with a deuce. I have almost no showdown equity and limited fold equity vs a caller from the flop. Betting here risks getting value‑raised by trips or called by all Ax/Qx and medium pairs; checking controls the pot, avoids bloating the pot with a bluff that has poor backup, and preserves my stack for better spots. Plan: check and re-evaluate on the river — I can bluff a harmless blank or give up to a large bet and fold.

On the river, the board has completed with a third Deuce, making the final board 2s-Qh-Ac-2c-2d. My hand is now a Full House (Deuces full of Aces), using the Ace in my hand and the Ace on the board as the pair. This is an extremely strong hand in heads-up play. The only hands that beat me are a Deuce (for Quads) or a higher Full House (unlikely given the board). Any other Ace results in a split pot (2-2-2-A-A). If Minni has a Queen or a pocket pair, her best hand is Deuces full of Queens or Deuces full of the board's high cards (A-Q), which I beat. Since Minni checked back the turn, she likely has a marginal made hand like a Queen or a weak Ace. I will bet 500 for value, as an Ace will definitely call (splitting the pot) and a Queen might call thinking I'm bluffing on a scary board.

I have 8♠7♥ on A♣ Q♥ 2♠ 2♣ 2♦. Villain (BB) called my flop c‑bet, both checked the turn (board paired), and now makes a small river bet 500 into a 1300 pot. I have essentially zero showdown equity — I beat only pure air/bluffs — and calling 500 to win 1800 requires ~28% equity. Villain’s line (call flop, check turn, small river bet) is exactly the type that includes lots of value: A‑x, Q‑x, pocket pairs and also the occasional deuce (quads) or paired hand that now makes a full house; those hands all beat me. A bluff‑raise would be thin: my blockers are poor (I don’t block A/Q/2 combos), the min‑raise to 1000 is unlikely to fold a large proportion of villain’s value/calling range, and if called I’m drawing dead. With a big stack advantage and many better spots ahead, folding preserves equity and avoids a negative‑EV call or risky bluff.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with King-Ten offsuit (KdTc), which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Historically, Minni has been quite active and has a significantly larger stack. However, my hand is well within the raising range for this position. Raising here helps me take control of the pot and define the range of the opponent. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate for this stack depth to build a pot while keeping some leverage.

Heads-up with Kc8c is a defendable, fairly strong hand — especially against a wide button open. Flash raised to 300; pot is 400 and I need 200 more, so calling would commit 300 total (including my posted big blind). The pot odds (~2:1) and equity vs a wide opening range justify a call. I’m out of position postflop, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a light 3-bet; calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me realize equity (backdoor flush + two overcards), and allows me to see how Flash proceeds on the flop so I can apply pressure or fold as appropriate. Call 300 total.

Heads-up on A♣6♣3♦ with Kc8c: I have two overcards to the 3 and 6 plus a very strong 4-club hand (9 outs to a club). I’m out of position so I don’t want to give Flash a free card or let them dictate the action every street. Betting is a semi-bluff that both picks up the pot now a decent percentage of the time and builds a pot where I have good equity if called. Pot math: pot is 600 — a 400 bet (~2/3 pot) forces a caller to have ~29% equity to profitably call, and my equity vs a reasonable calling range (Ax miss combos, medium pairs, broadways, some clubs) is in that neighborhood or better. If Flash raises large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a strong raise since Ace or a made club is strongly represented); if they call I still have meaningful redraw equity to improve on turn/river. Bet 400 to apply pressure and realize equity.

On a flop of Ac-6c-3d, Minni has led out with a donk bet of 400 into a 600 pot. My hand, King-Ten offsuit, has completely missed this board and has very little equity against a range that calls a pre-flop raise and then leads out on an Ace-high texture. While I represent many Aces in my range as the pre-flop aggressor, calling or raising here with just King-high is unnecessarily risky given the stack sizes and the likelihood that Minni has at least a piece of the board or a flush draw. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with Q8o — this is a hand I want to open-raise to seize initiative and steal the blind. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but raising gives fold equity and lets me play position postflop. I’ll make a standard 3x BB-sized open to 300 total: large enough to get folds and protect my hand, but not so large as to bloat the pot unnecessarily versus an 82bb effective opponent. Plan: if called I’ll continuation-bet favorable flops; fold Q8o to a large 3-bet (or re-evaluate vs a small 3-bet and proceed cautiously).

I have Td 4s in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind (Button). In a heads-up situation, T4o is a very weak hand to defend with, especially against an opponent who covers me significantly in chips and has been aggressive. Calling 200 more into a 400 pot with such a weak hand out of position is generally a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my 82.5 big blinds.

Jack-Ten offsuit is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, particularly from the button. I'll open with my standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and take initiative, as this hand has good playability across many flops.

I have A♠J♠ in the big blind facing a 300 open from the button. Heads-up this is a strong hand that plays well both preflop and postflop; I want to seize the initiative, deny equity to worse A-x and broadway hands, and build the pot for value. A standard 3-bet to 1200 is a good size here — large enough to generate fold equity and isolate the button, but small enough to keep worse hands in and to play postflop if called. If Flash 4-bets big or shoves I will re-evaluate (AJs can fold to a polarized shove from a very tight player but will call reasonable 4-bets), so I’m taking an aggressive but controlled line.

I am in Hand #104 of 150. Minni (30,550 chips) has just 3-bet my initial raise of 300 to 1,200. I am holding Jack-Ten offsuit in position. While Jack-Ten is a reasonable hand to defend against a standard 3-bet in heads-up play, the context of the match matters significantly here. Minni has a massive chip lead (nearly 4x my stack) and has been very aggressive with 3-bets lately (Hands #36, #45, #52, #58, #65, #68, #70, #103). In Hand #70, Minni 3-bet me with Queen-Jack and eventually made a full house against my Ace-Jack. Given my current stack of 7,950, calling 900 more (representing over 10% of my remaining chips) with a non-premium hand out of position post-flop (though I have button position, the 3-bet size is large) is risky. However, in heads-up play, Jack-Ten is often too strong to fold to a single 3-bet. I will call to see a flop, relying on my position to navigate the rest of the hand.

Flop: Kd 2d Ks with A♠J♠ — I have no pair and no diamond draw, just Ace-high. Flash called my 3-bet preflop, so his call-range is fairly strong (AX, broadways, pocket pairs, Kx). On this paired-king board I'm often behind to any king or pocket pair, and betting here would mostly turn my hand into a bluff against a range that will frequently call or check-raise. I want to control the pot size and avoid committing chips with a marginal hand. Checking lets me see what Flash does (call small bets to realize equity, fold to big aggression) and re-evaluate on the turn. Therefore I check to pot-control and gather more information.

Holding Jack-Ten on a King-King-Deuce board after calling a 3-bet, I have very little showdown value but significant position. Minni's check on this board is interesting; while it's a board that should favor a 3-better's range (containing many AK, KQ, KJ, KT hands), a check can signify a trap or a total miss with hands like AQ or AJ. However, betting here into a polarized range might lead to a check-raise that forces me to fold my equity. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and see how Minni reacts on the turn. This allows me to potentially bluff later or value bet if I improve.

Turn (Kd K s 2d 2h) has turned the board into a double‑paired nightmare for my A♠J♠. Any K or 2 in Flash’s preflop call range (Kx, 22, 2x, many pocket pairs, AK, etc.) is now a full house or better; he called my 3‑bet preflop so his range is weighted toward those hands. I have only ace‑high with no diamond draw and very little fold equity against a range that can happily call or shove. Betting here would be a thin bluff into a range that rarely folds and risks committing a big portion of my stack to a hand that’s almost certainly behind. Pot control is the right line: check and re‑evaluate on the river — call small river bets to realize showdown only when appropriate, fold to big value shoves unless the river changes things. Action: check to keep the pot manageable and avoid getting stacked with air.

On a double-paired board of K-K-2-2 after Minni 3-bet pre-flop, my J-T has extremely low showdown value. Most of Minni's 3-betting range (Aces, pocket pairs, or any King) is currently crushing me. Her second check on the turn could be a trap with a King or a sign that she's giving up with a hand like A-Q or A-J. However, betting here is unlikely to fold out an Ace given the board state, and I would be vulnerable to a check-raise. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and control the pot size with a weak hand.

River is Kd Ks 2d 2h 2s — the board itself is a full house (three 2s and two Ks). With A♠J♠ I cannot make a better five‑card hand than the board; only a king (to make kings full) or a deuce (to make quads) in Flash’s hand beats me. Flash flat‑called my preflop 3‑bet and then checked down, so his range contains many Kx and medium/strong pairs that will happily call a river bluff. Betting here would be a thin bluff that folds the hands I’d otherwise chop with and gets called by the hands that beat me — negative EV. Pot control and showdown is best: check and accept the split unless Flash shows a king/deuce (in which case I lose to a legitimately better hand).

The board has completed as Kd-2d-Ks-2h-2s. This means the board itself is a full house: Deuces full of Kings (2-2-2-K-K). My hand, Jack-Ten, does not improve this; my best five-card hand is the board itself. In this situation, I lose to any King (making Kings full of Deuces), any Deuce (making Four of a Kind), or pocket Aces (making Deuces full of Aces). I chop the pot with any other hand (like Ace-high or other pocket pairs). Minni 3-bet pre-flop, which heavily weights her range toward big cards and pairs. Since she has checked the river, and my hand only plays the board, there is no value in betting as she will not fold a hand that beats me or a hand that chops. Checking back is the only sensible move to realize my share of the pot.

QhJh suited is a strong heads‑up hand with good playability and nut‑flush potential.
