I’m on the button (postflop position) and the pot is only 150 with 50 to call — this is a clear open-raise spot to seize initiative and get value from a wide BB calling range. I’ll open to 300 (a standard 3x BB sizing at this table) to: 1) build the pot when I’m likely ahead, 2) gain fold equity to pick up the blind/isolate the shorter-stacked opponent, and 3) keep the postflop SPR manageable so I can leverage my position. If Flash calls I’ll continue with positional aggression and c‑bet appropriate textures; if he 3‑bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a crushing shove but consider calling or 4‑bet against smaller, polarized 3‑bets).

I am in the Big Blind with Jc2c and Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. J2 suited is a weak hand, but in a heads-up situation, it's often worth defending against a button open, especially with the suitedness providing some playability. However, Minni has a significant chip lead (31,450 vs 8,150). Looking at past hands, Minni has been quite aggressive. I'm getting 2:1 odds to call (200 more to win a pot that will be 600). While I could fold to preserve my stack, calling occasionally with hands like this helps maintain a balanced defending range. I will call and see a flop, looking for a flush draw or a Jack.

I have missed the flop completely with Jack-high and no significant draws. The board texture (Q-T-4) is quite favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range (Minni). I will check to see how the opponent acts. If Minni bets, I will likely fold given my lack of equity and the stack disparity.

Flop: Qd Ts 4d. I hold QhJh — top pair with a good kicker in a heads‑up spot and I'm in position. Flash’s calling range to my 3x open is wide (many Qx, Tx, broadways, small pairs and diamond draws), and this board is relatively dry aside from two diamonds. I have range and hand advantage and should c‑bet for value and to charge draws. A half‑pot bet (~300 into 600) extracts value from worse queens, tens and second‑pair hands, prices out some equity of diamond draws, and keeps the pot controlled given effective stacks (~7950). Plan: if called evaluate turn — continue betting safe, non‑diamond cards for value, and be cautious (check or pot‑control) if a diamond or a scary card arrives; fold to a polarized, large check‑raise that strongly represents the nuts. Bet 300 (≈50% pot).

I defended the Big Blind with Jc2c pre-flop. The flop of Qd-Ts-4d does not hit my range at all; I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. Minni has continued with a bet of 300 into a 600 pot. Given the board connectivity (Queen and Ten), it is highly likely this flop connected with Minni's pre-flop raising range. With Jack-high and no real equity to improve to a winning hand, there is no reason to continue. I will fold to preserve my remaining 7,650 chips.

I have Ace-Eight of hearts on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a very strong hand (top 15-20% of hands). I will make a standard opening raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand. My stack is relatively short compared to Minni, but I cannot afford to play passively with premium starting hands.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300 with Ts8s in the big blind. This is a playable, suited connector with good multi-street potential against a wide opening range. Pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call (total commitment 300), giving ~3:1 pot odds (≈25% break-even equity) — well within range for Ts8s vs a wide SB opener. I’m out of position postflop, so I’d rather defend and see a flop than 3-bet light (a small 3-bet won’t gain much and a large one bloats an OOP pot). Call 300 to continue and plan to play the flop cautiously based on texture and opponent action.

Check. I'm out of position with T♠8♠ on a very dry 5♠-6♥-4♥ flop. My equity is largely backdoor: four 7s give me a straight (≈16% to hit by the river) and I have a tiny two-card spade backdoor (~4%). Betting OOP with a marginal hand here just bloats a pot I don't want to play big out of position. Checking preserves pot control, lets me realize equity cheaply and gives me the option to call small continuation bets or check‑raise if Flash barrels wide. Plan: check and reassess on the turn — call small/medium bets to see another card, fold to large commitment unless I pick up meaningful improvement (7 or a spade turn).

On a board of 5s-6h-4h, I have the nut flush draw with Ace-Eight of hearts and one overcard. This board is very coordinated and hits a lot of the Big Blind's calling range, but I have significant equity with my draw. I will place a continuation bet of 350 into the 600 pot to maintain aggression and build the pot in case I hit my flush, while also potentially folding out weaker hands or overcards that missed.

Call 350. Pot is 950 and Flash's bet of 350 is a small (~35%) c-bet into a heads‑up pot — likely a high-frequency continuation bet. I need ~27% equity to break even on a call; my hand (T♠8♠) has realistic multi‑street equity: ~16% to hit a 7 for the straight by the river and a ~4% two‑card spade backdoor flush plus additional chances to pair a Ten or Eight on turn/river. That puts my combined chance to improve into the 20–30% range — borderline but acceptable given the tiny bet and the wide button opening/c-bet range. I'm out of position, so I don't want to bloatedly 3‑bet OOP with mostly backdoor equity; a raise here would commit me to a bigger pot without much fold equity. Plan after the call: fold to heavy turn aggression if I don't improve; if I hit a 7, a pair (T/8), or run spades I will proceed for value (raise/bet accordingly).

I just completed a made straight (4-5-6-7-8) with T♠8♠ on 5♠-6♥-4♥-7♠. Heads-up this is extremely strong versus Flash’s wide opening/continuation range — most pairs, overcards and single-card draws he’s been betting with are now behind. I should be building the pot for value and denying a free river that could bring a 9 (the main card that would beat me) or complete a backdoor flush. Pot is ~1300; I’ll size a value bet around 2/3–3/4 pot to charge pairs and worse draws but not overcommit and to keep many worse hands in. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river; if he raises, his range narrows to the few combos that beat me (mostly 9x) or a bluff — I’ll decide based on sizing and reads then. Betting now maximizes value and protects my hand.

On the turn, the 7s completes a straight for my 8h (4-5-6-7-8). While a 9 in the opponent's hand would make a higher 9-high straight, my hand is exceptionally strong, especially since I also hold the Nut Flush Draw (Ah8h on a 4h6h board). Minni's lead of 900 into 1300 is a strong action, often indicating a made hand or a powerful draw on this coordinated board. Raising here might overplay my hand against a potential 9-high straight, but folding is impossible given my current straight and massive draw equity. I will call to see the river and evaluate based on the final card.

I turned the 4‑5‑6‑7‑8 straight on the turn and the river 8d pairs the board but does not remove my straight — the only hands that now beat me are any hand containing a 9 (they make a 9‑high straight). Flash opened, c‑betted the flop and called my sizable turn bet, so his continuing range is wide and contains a lot of one‑pair and medium hands that are now behind my straight. I should be extracting value rather than checking and giving up money. I’ll size a value bet ~2/3 pot to charge pairs, 7x/6x and weaker holdings while not overcommitting to a potential check‑raise. Bet 2000 into the 3100 pot; if he raises a small/medium amount I’ll call and reevaluate (his raising range narrows toward 9x and bluffs), but I will respect an extreme overbet/jam and fold if the sizing makes it almost certain he has the 9‑high straight.

The board has completed a five-card straight (4-5-6-7-8). My current hand, Ace-Eight of hearts, is now playing the board's eight-high straight. In heads-up play, when a straight appears on the board, it is common for a player to bet to represent the higher straight (in this case, any hand containing a 9) or to pressure the opponent into folding their half of the split pot. While Minni's lead on the turn and bet on the river could indicate she holds a 9, I cannot fold an existing straight on the board in this situation. A call is necessary to protect my share of the pot. Raising is not advisable here as it would only be called by a 9 that beats me, and it's unlikely she would fold her share of the chop to a raise given the pot odds.

Heads-up on the button with Q♦2♠ — a weak, but playable hand in position. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to complete; opponent (BB) has 7,850 so effective stacks ~78 BB. In heads-up you should open a wide range to seize initiative and use position postflop. Folding is too nitty and calling is passive (gives BB free equity and surrenders initiative). I’ll make a standard small open to 2BB (total 200) — this size keeps the pot manageable, applies pressure to marginal holdings, and lets me play postflop with position. If Flash calls I’ll continuation-bet appropriate textures and control pot size when behind; if they 3-bet I can fold or proceed depending on sizing and read. Total commitment: 200 (I’ve already posted 50, so +150).

I am in the Big Blind holding 8s 3h. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 200. This is a small raise, requiring only 100 more for me to call into a pot of 300 (offering 3:1 odds). However, 83o is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given Minni's large stack advantage and the fact that I want to preserve my remaining 78.5 big blinds for better spots, I will fold this hand and wait for a more favorable situation.

In heads-up play, 97o is a hand within the standard opening range from the Small Blind. I have approximately 78 big blinds, which gives me enough room to play. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds, or play a post-flop game if called.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the BB with 5♥3♦ — a weak, offsuit rag with poor playability. Pot is 400 and calling 200 (bringing my total committed to 300) gives ~25% pot odds, which is roughly the threshold vs a wide button opening range heads-up. I don’t want to 3-bet-bluff here because if called I’ll be OOP with a garbage hand; better to defend and see a flop, check-fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, and only continue with strong pairs or meaningful draws. Call to keep the pot manageable and realize equity.

I completely missed this dry flop with 5♥3♦ on T♦ 2♠ 7♥ — no pair and only very remote backdoor outs (a double-heart runout or runner-runner straight). Heads-up vs the button’s wide opening range, this board actually hits his range (T,7,2, broadways) more often than mine. I don’t want to lead out or bluff-raise OOP with garbage and risk bloating the pot against a player who can call or shove. Check to keep the pot small and follow my preflop plan to check-fold to typical continuation bets; if Flash checks back I’ll see a free turn, if he bets I’ll fold to most sizes (only consider calling tiny probes or if I pick up a meaningful draw).

I hit second pair (Sevens) on a dry Ten-high board. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Minni checked to me, a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) is appropriate to extract value from weaker hands, protect against overcards, and maintain initiative.

I checked the flop with 5♥3♦ and completely missed the dry T♦–2♠–7♥ board. I have no pair and essentially no realistic draws (with one heart in hand and one on the board a flush is impossible; the only live outs are remote runner‑runner straights). Flash’s c‑bet of 300 into the current pot (pot shown 900; calling 300 would require ~25% equity to be +EV) puts me OOP versus a wide but still heavily-favoring button range (T/7/2/top pairs, broadways, some draws). My actual equity with 5‑3o here is well below the required threshold, and calling just bloats the pot with almost no chance to improve or fold equity on later streets. With a healthy stack advantage I should be disciplined and preserve chips — fold this marginal holding.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with 7♥9♦. In heads-up play I should be opening a wide range to seize initiative — I have position postflop which makes this hand playable. The pot is small (150) and a minimum raise to 200 is cheap relative to my 31.8k stack and will fold out many marginal BB defenses or allow me to play the flop in position if called. Plan: min-raise to apply pressure, c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on highly coordinated boards; fold to a large 3-bet. This is a standard button open to maximize EV in this spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 3d. Minni, on the Button/SB, has raised to 200. I need to call 100 more to see a flop. While 73o is a very weak hand, the pot odds are 3:1. However, out of position against a player with a significant chip lead who has been aggressive, 73o is generally a fold in this spot to avoid being trapped in a marginal post-flop situation where I am easily outdrawn or bluffed off a weak pair.

I am on the button with Jack-Four offsuit. In a heads-up situation, a Jack-high hand has some moderate strength, but Jack-Four is quite weak. However, given the current stack dynamics and the tournament stage (Hand 110 of 150), maintaining some aggression or at least seeing a flop when the price is low (it's only 50 more to call) is a consideration. However, I have been playing somewhat tight-aggressive. Jack-Four offsuit is a standard fold from the Small Blind/Button in many heads-up ranges unless I intend to steal with a raise. Given my opponent's significant chip lead, I will choose to fold this weak hand and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with 2♥6♣: a weak hand but highly open-raiseable in heads-up because position and fold equity matter most. Limping (calling 50) is too passive and surrenders initiative to the BB. I’ll raise to 300 total (3×BB) — a compact, standard heads-up sizing that maximizes fold equity while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: if Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold this hand; if called I’ll use position to c-bet many flops and play cautiously toward showdown since 2-6 offsuit has low showdown value.

I am in the Big Blind with J7o and facing a 3x open (300 total) from Minni on the Button. The pot is offering me 2:1 odds to call. Looking at previous hands, Minni is a frequent raiser from the button and often continuation bets. J7o is a marginal defending hand heads-up, but given the pot odds and the fact that we are deep enough to play post-flop, a call is acceptable to see a flop. However, my stack is significantly smaller than Minni's (7,900 vs 31,700), so I need to be careful not to bleed chips with weak top-pair or middle-pair hands. At this stage, folding a marginal hand like J7o against a 3x raise is also a disciplined option to preserve stack for better spots, but calling to defend the big blind is standard in a heads-up situation.

I have flopped second pair (Jacks) on an Ace-high board with a weak kicker. As the Big Blind acting first post-flop against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser. This allows me to see how Minni responds; if she bets, I have a decision to make, but leading into an Ace-high board when I don't have an Ace is generally risky and gives away information.

Flop: Jc 2c As. I hold 2h6c — bottom pair (pair of twos) with a weak kicker and only a backdoor club possibility. Heads-up my preflop raising range includes lots of Aces, Jx and bluffs, so betting here is the standard line: take the initiative, get value from worse hands (small pairs, Jx that check back, lone clubs, Ax that check for pot control) and deny free cards. I don’t want to bloat the pot with such a weak pair, so size for protection and fold equity: half-pot keeps pressure on air/overcards but lets me fold to big aggression. Plan: bet 300 into 600; call any small single raise but fold to a large check-raise/all-in and re-evaluate if called on the turn.

I have flopped middle pair (Jacks) on an Ace-high board. Minni's 300 bet into a 600 pot is a standard continuation bet. While the Ace is well within her pre-flop range, she also bets with many hands I am currently beating, such as air, lower pairs, or draws. Folding second pair here would be too exploitable, and raising would likely only get action from better hands. Calling is the most disciplined play to see a turn and re-evaluate.

The King on the turn is a significant scare card for my hand, as it is a common card in Minni's button opening range. I now hold third pair (Jacks) on a board of A-K-J-2. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor here would likely result in being called or raised by better hands (Aces, Kings, or even Q-10 which completed a straight). I will check to control the size of the pot and see how Minni responds to the change in board texture.

I have 2♥6♣ — bottom pair (pair of deuces) on J♣ 2♣ A♠ K♥ with a weak kicker. Pot is 1,200 and Flash checked the turn after calling my flop c‑bet. His flop call range likely contains many A‑x, J‑x, small pocket pairs and club draws that beat or have similar equity to my 2; a turn bet will mostly get called by better hands and just bloat the pot. Facing a 7,400 stack behind, I don’t want to build a big pot with a marginal holding. I’ll exercise pot control and check the turn to see the river cheaply, induce bluffs from missed draws, and fold to clear large river aggression unless I improve (or he jams as a polar bluff).

The river completes a board of A-K-Q-J-2. I have a pair of Jacks (one in hand, one on board), but the board is extremely coordinated. Any Ten makes a straight, and any Ace, King, or Queen (all of which are in a standard pre-flop opening range) beats my pair. Minni checked the turn, which may indicate a lack of a top-tier hand, but she could still hold a Queen or a King. I will check to see if I can reach showdown cheaply; my hand beats pure bluffs or missed club draws, but it is too weak to bet for value or to turn into a bluff.

I check. I hold 2♥6♣ — bottom pair (pair of twos) with a weak kicker on J♣‑2♣‑A♠‑K♥‑Q♠. Flash called my flop c‑bet, then pot‑controlled (checked) the turn and checked the river as well. That line is most consistent with showdown‑value hands (Ax, Jx, Kx/Qx turned into a show‑down line) or cautious medium holdings — hands that beat me and will call a river bet. Betting here would mainly get called by better or only fold the very thin part of his range I already beat, so it’s not worth bloating the pot. With the pot at 1,200 and a 7,400 effective, I prefer pot control and a cheap showdown. If he now leads out into me I’ll re‑evaluate by sizing (call small probes, fold to large aggression), but the correct action now is to check and conserve chips.

I am on the button with 9d 6d, a hand that has good playability in heads-up play. With 85 big blinds, I have plenty of room to play post-flop, but I need to start closing the chip gap with Minni. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand and define the range of the big blind.

Heads-up, I’m the big blind with 4♠8♣ and Flash (Button/SB) opened to 300. Pot is 400 and I must put 200 more to call (min-raise to 500). Effective stacks: Flash ~8,300 (~83bb), I have 31,300. 4-8o is a weak hand, but in heads-up the BB should defend fairly wide. Calling 200 gives me immediate pot odds of 200/(400+200)=33% to continue — versus a wide button opening my equity is around that threshold. This isn’t a hand I want to 3-bet OOP or build a big pot with, so I’ll call and see a flop. Postflop plan: continue only with a pair/strong draw or favorable board texture; otherwise play cautiously and fold to significant continuation bets.

Heads-up on a dry 7♠ 8♦ 3♥ flop I’ve flopped middle pair (8♣) with a weak 4-kicker. Against Button’s wide open-raising range I’m currently ahead of most unpaired overcards and many floats, and I want to both extract value and deny free cards that can pick off my pair on the turn. Checking lets the opener take the initiative and realize equity cheaply, so I prefer to bet a standard half‑pot to get called by worse (A/K/Q/x hands, single-pair floats) and charge any backdoor draws. I’ll size half‑pot — 300 into 600 — which keeps the pot controllable relative to stacks and leaves room to fold or re-evaluate if Flash raises big. Plan: if he calls I proceed cautiously on turn; if he raises substantially I will re-evaluate given my weak kicker and his range.

I have an open-ended straight draw (9, 8, 7, 6) and a backdoor diamond draw on this flop. Minni's lead of 300 into the 600 pot (a donk bet) often indicates a marginal hand or a draw. I will raise here to 900 as a semi-bluff to take initiative and build the pot for if I hit my straight. This also puts pressure on her middle-pair or weak-top-pair hands. If she calls, I still have significant equity to improve on the turn.

I have middle pair (8♣) with a weak 4-kicker on 7♠‑8♦‑3♥. I bet a standard half‑pot to get value and deny free cards; Flash’s sizable raise to 900 polarizes his range (strong made hands and bluffs). Calling 600 more (900 total committed this street) gives me ~25% break‑even equity (600/(1800+600)), which my hand likely exceeds versus a wide HU raising range, so calling is the +EV, pot‑controlling play. Folding is too tight and reraising would bloat the pot with a mediocre kicker; I’ll call and re‑evaluate on the turn — folding to heavy turn aggression on a very scary card, or extracting thin value on safe runouts or if he checks.

I have middle pair (8♣) with a very weak 4 kicker on 7♠-8♦-3♥-6♠. Flash’s flop raise polarized his range toward strong value hands (8x, 7x, sets) and some bluffs — the turn 6♠ is a card that realistically improves many of his value hands (6x) while only giving me a single-card straight draw (four 5s). Pot is already 2,400 and his remaining stack (~7,400) is large enough to risk jamming over a turn bet. With a marginal made hand and a vulnerable kicker my priority is pot control: checking keeps bluffs in, allows me to call a small turn bet to realize my limited equity (~4 outs to a straight, ≈8–9% to hit), and fold to large turn aggression. If he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river for thin value or showdown. Therefore I check to avoid bloating the pot with a weak hand.

On the turn, the 6s gives me a pair of sixes to go along with my open-ended straight draw. Minni checked after calling my raise on the flop. While I could continue betting as a semi-bluff, checking here allows me to realize my equity and see a free river card. I have some showdown value now with the pair, and if I hit my straight on the river, the pot is already a decent size. Checking also protects me from a check-raise that might force me to fold my draws.

I have second pair (8♣) with a very weak 4‑kicker on 7♠‑8♦‑3♥‑6♠‑T♦. Flash check‑raised my flop bet (900) — that line is polarized to either strong value (7x, 8x with a better kicker, sets) or bluffs. He then checked the turn, which often indicates pot‑control value hands or bluffs that gave up. The river 10♦ doesn’t improve my hand and actually helps some turn/river catch‑ups (T‑x) and overcard boats. Betting for thin value is unlikely to work: most hands that beat me will call, and most worse hands I want to charge will fold. With pot = 2,400 and a very vulnerable kicker, the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV play is to check and take it to showdown. If Flash now fires a small river bet I’ll call reasonable sizes for pot odds; if he jams big, I’ll fold to preserve chips against likely value.

I have completed a straight (6-7-8-9-10) using the Nine in my hand on a board of 7-8-3-6-10. Although J-9 would make a higher straight, Minni's passivity on the turn and river (checking both streets) strongly suggests she holds a one-pair hand rather than the nuts. I checked the turn to realize my equity and protect against a check-raise while I only had a pair and a draw; now that I have improved to a straight, I must bet for value. I will bet 1,600 into the 2,400 pot, a sizing designed to get called by any pair of Tens, Eights, or Sevens.

Recap: I hold second pair (8♣) with a very weak 4-kicker. Flash opened pre, I c-bet the flop and he check‑raised — a polarized line (value + semi‑bluffs). We both checked the turn. The river 10♦ doesn’t improve me and helps some T‑x combos, but Flash’s 1,600 into a 2,400 pot is a standard ~2/3‑pot sizing. Pot odds: calling 1,600 gets me to a pot of 5,600 → I need ≈28.6% equity to break even. Against Flash’s river betting range (value: 8x/7x/sets/overpairs/Tx; bluffs: missed 9‑10/5‑6/etc.), I still beat a significant portion of his bluffs and worse pairs, and my hand has enough showdown value to justify a call for those pot odds. Raising would bloat the pot with a weak kicker and invite a shove or call from many better hands; folding would fold out too much equity given the price. I’ll call and take the showdown; if he were to shove over my call I’d re‑evaluate given his stack and the exact sizing, but for now a call is the +EV, low‑variance line.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with A♥K♣ — a textbook premium hand. Pot is 150 and I only need 50 to call, but limping is passive and loses value here. I want to build the pot and apply pressure while keeping postflop maneuverability, so I’ll raise to a standard 3x BB (300 total). This isolates the BB’s wide defending range and gives me good equity and position; if he 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate by sizing (call or 4-bet shove vs very large shoves depending on exact size), and if he calls I’ll play aggressively on favorable flops.

I am in the Big Blind with 3h 2h, which is a very weak hand despite being suited. Minni, who currently holds a significant chip lead, has raised to 3x the big blind. While the pot odds are roughly 2:1, 32s has very poor post-flop playability and often suffers from reverse implied odds. Given that I need to preserve my stack to find better spots to close the gap in this tournament, I will choose the disciplined path and fold this hand.

I am on the button with Qc3s, which is a weak hand even for heads-up play. However, it is standard to open a wide range from the button in a heads-up match. The cost to call is only 50 chips to potentially win a pot of 150, but raising is generally a better way to exert pressure and win pre-flop. Given the stack sizes, I'll attempt a standard 3x raise to take down the blinds or see a flop in a raised pot.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the button in heads-up, K♦5♦ is well within a defending range — suited king with decent high-card and flush/backdoor equity. Calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity OOP without turning a marginal hand into a bigger commitment. A min-3bet to 500 is too small to extract value or fold out equity and I don't want to inflate the pot with K5s. I'll call (add 200 more, 300 total committed preflop) and proceed cautiously on the flop, folding to strong aggression if I miss and continuing when I hit king/flush/draws.

I have top pair (K♦) with a weak 5 kicker on a very spade-heavy flop (T♠ K♠ 4♠). Button opened wide preflop and will have a lot of single-spade draws and some two-spade combos (rare). I want to charge and deny equity to single-spade draws while extracting value from worse kings, tens and other calling hands, but keep the pot manageable vs a possible strong raise. A half-pot c-bet accomplishes that balance. I'll bet 300 (≈50% pot); if he raises big or jams I’ll assume a strong flush/very strong hand and fold, but proceed cautiously if he merely calls.

On the flop of Ts Ks 4s, I have the Queen of Clubs and the Trey of Spades, giving me a small flush draw (the 3 of spades). The board is highly coordinated and very dangerous. Minni has led out with a bet of 300 into a pot of 600 after calling my pre-flop raise. While I have the Queen of Clubs, it's not a spade, and the 3 of spades is the lowest possible spade for a flush. Minni's lead often indicates a piece of the board, a spade, or a draw. Given my hand is very weak and my draw is the lowest possible one, calling here is unprofitable against her range. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play.

Heads-up on the button/SB I should be playing aggressively and widening my opening range. K♠6♦ is a playable hand in this spot: it has a high-card value and performs well postflop in position. A limp (just calling the extra 50) surrenders initiative and invites cheaper calls or random raises from the BB. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) to apply pressure, build the pot when ahead, and fold out weak hands. This sizing keeps the pot manageable with a marginal kicker; if Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (usually fold or take a cautious line depending on sizing). Total to commit preflop: 300.

In Hand #115, I'm in the Big Blind with Tc 3s. Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. This hand is significantly below average, especially out of position against a player who has me covered roughly 2.5 to 1. While the price to call is only 200 more into a pot that will be 600, 10-3 offsuit performs poorly post-flop and doesn't offer much in terms of playability or equity. I've been involved in several hands lately and my current strategy should be to preserve my remaining 10,900 chips for a better spot to double up rather than bleeding chips with weak hands like this. I'll fold and wait for a more advantageous starting hand.

As the button in a heads-up situation with a suited Ten, I should be opening a wide range of hands to maintain pressure and not become too predictable, especially since Minni has been very active. T3s is a marginal hand but acceptable for a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot early.

Heads-up in the big blind with 5♦4♣ vs a button/SB open to 300. Calling 200 more (300 total) gives me good pot odds (≈3:1) and with deep stacks this is a reasonable speculative hand to see a flop. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal 3-bet — a min-raise to 500 won’t fold enough and a larger 3-bet is over-committing with 54o. I’ll call to realize equity, proceed cautiously postflop (fold to heavy aggression on dry high-card boards, continue with realizable straights/2-pair/sets), and look to extract value or fold based on texture and opponent actions.

Heads-up out of position on a Q♠-6♠-2♣ flop with 5♦4♣. I have no pair, no spade, only very weak backdoor possibilities and I don’t block much of the button’s likely c-bet range (Qx, Kx, Ax, spade draws, small pairs). Betting here as a bluff is thin — villain opened wide and will call often, and I don’t want to inflate the pot with essentially zero equity. Best line is pot control: check and evaluate on the turn. If Flash bets a reasonable size I’ll fold; if he checks back I can see a free card and decide later.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and the flop of Q-6-2 is relatively dry, which favors the initial raiser's range. Since Minni checked, I will fire a continuation bet of 300 (half-pot) to try and take the pot down immediately. My Ten-high has very little showdown value, so I need to maintain aggression.

Flop Q♠-6♠-2♣, I have 5♦4♣ — no pair, no spade, only weak backdoor possibilities. Flash’s bet 300 into the 900 pot means calling 300 to win ~1,200 (≈25% required equity). My real winning equity is well under that: only 4 outs (3s) to make the nut-straight on the turn (~8.5%) or ~16.5% by the river, and while pairing a 4 or 5 increases raw improvement odds, many of those outcomes still lose to Qx/Kx/6x and spade holdings. Check-raise bluff is thin vs a wide opener who will call a raise too often, and I’m out of position — saving chips for better spots is higher EV. Fold.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with 6s7s suited — a very playable hand in position. Flash in the big blind will be defending widely, so I want to take the initiative and build the pot with position and postflop maneuverability. A standard 3x-ish open to 300 is appropriate: it gives fold equity, keeps the pot manageable given deep stacks, and is consistent with table sizing. If he folds I collect the blinds; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable/dry boards, play draws/connected boards aggressively, and re-evaluate vs a 3-bet (fold to very large 3-bets, consider calling smaller 3-bets given the deep stacks).

In this heads-up situation, I am in the Big Blind with 6c4c. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Although 64 suited is a speculative hand, the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600) and the playability of suited connectors in heads-up play justify a call. I have a healthy stack of 11,100, and seeing a flop with a hand that can flop draws or two-pair is reasonable against Minni, who has been active. Folding here would be too tight.

I have flopped an open-ended straight draw with my 6c4c on a 5sQs7c board. In heads-up play, being out of position, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most balanced play. If Minni bets, I have enough equity with 8 outs to a straight to call and see a turn card. If she checks behind, I get a free chance to hit my straight or pick up more equity.

I hold 6♠7♠ on a 5♠ Q♠ 7♣ flop — second pair (7s) plus a strong spade flush draw (9 outs). Heads-up in position this is a spot to take the initiative: it has both immediate showdown value and a lot of equity. Flash checked, so I’ll c‑bet to extract value from worse pairs, charge spade draws and Qx hands, and deny free cards. I size up to ~2/3 pot (400 into 600) — big enough to charge draws and get value, but not so big that a single raise ends the hand automatically. If he calls I still have strong turn equity; if he check‑raises small/medium I’ll usually call with pair+draw and re‑evaluate on the turn, and I’ll fold or proceed more cautiously to an extreme overbet/shove depending on sizing and read.

On this flop of 5s-Qs-7c, I have an open-ended straight draw (4-5-6-7) with my 4c6c. Minni has bet 400 into a 600 pot. To call, I need to commit 400 to win a total pot of 1,400, which requires roughly 28.6% equity. While my immediate probability of hitting the straight on the turn is about 17%, the implied odds in heads-up play are significant if I hit my hand, especially since an 8 or a 3 would be relatively disguised. Given our stack depths and the fact that I can also potentially win the pot later by bluffing if the board texture changes or if she shows weakness, calling is the most profitable and standard play here.

On the turn, the 8h has completed my open-ended straight draw, giving me a 4-5-6-7-8 straight. This is an extremely strong hand in a heads-up pot. Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand (and the match), so checking here is the most effective way to disguise the strength of my hand and induce a bet from her. If she bets her top pair (Queens) or continues with a bluff/semi-bluff (like a spade draw), I can then check-raise to maximize the value. Leading out (donk betting) here might scare her off if she's on a pure air bluff or a marginal hand.

I hold 6♠7♠ on 5♠ Q♠ 7♣ 8♥ — second pair plus a huge spade flush draw and an open-ended straight draw. After c-betting the flop and getting called, I have very strong equity vs Flash’s calling range (Qs/X, spade draws, 5x, pocket pairs). Pot is 1,400; betting keeps initiative, charges draws, denies a free card, and extracts value from worse Qx/5x and single spade hands. I size to ~2/3 pot (900) — large enough to punish free cards and get value but small relative to our deep stack so we can still continue vs a reasonable raise. Plan: call a modest check-raise with pair+draw and re-evaluate on the river; fold or proceed cautiously to an all‑in overbet that polarizes to very strong hands.

On the turn, the 8h has completed my straight (4-5-6-7-8). While this isn't the absolute nuts (as 9-6 or 9-T would make higher straights), in a heads-up match, a straight is an extremely powerful hand. Minni has been the aggressor pre-flop, on the flop, and now on the turn, betting 900 into a 1400 pot. My previous plan was to check to induce a bet and then check-raise for value. By raising now, I maximize the pot against her range, which likely includes top-pair (Queens), sets, or two-pair hands that she is unlikely to fold. A raise to 2600 puts significant pressure on her while still leaving room for a river value bet or shove if the board remains relatively safe.

Flash check‑raised my 900 turn bet to 2,600. Pot is 4,900 and I must call 1,700 more (2,600 total). I have 6♠7♠ on 5♠ Q♠ 7♣ 8♥ — second pair plus the nut spade flush draw and an open‑ended straight draw. Unique outs: 9 spades + 6 non‑spade straight cards (4s/9s) + 2 remaining 7s = 17 outs (~37% to hit the river). My break‑even equity to call is only ~25.8% (1,700 / (4,900+1,700)), so calling is +EV: I already have showdown value and huge draw equity vs Flash’s likely check‑raise range (Qx, single spade draws, 5x, medium pairs). A re‑raise here would over‑polarize and risk folding out worse hands or getting called by hands that currently dominate me; folding throws away a lot of equity. I’ll call 1,700 more to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the river (extract value if I hit, or fold/raise depending on river action).

On the river, the 8s is a highly unfavorable card for my straight. It completes a potential spade flush (5s Qs 8s) and pairs the board, meaning any hand with an 8 now has trips, and any previous set (QQ, 77, 55) has improved to a full house. Given that Minni called my significant check-raise on the turn, her range is very strong and likely contains many hands that either already beat me or improved on this river. By checking, I can attempt to get to a relatively cheap showdown and avoid losing more chips to a superior hand. Betting would likely only result in being called or raised by hands that have me beat, while better hands are unlikely to fold in this heads-up dynamic.

Board: 5♠ Q♠ 7♣ 8♥ 8♠. I hold 6♠7♠ — I just made a spade flush (Qs-8s-7s-6s-5s). Flash check‑raised my turn bet, so his range includes Qx, spade draws, and some 8x/sets. On the river many of those check‑raise combos (single Qx, missed spade draws, small pairs) are now behind my flush; only a narrow subset (A♠/K♠ higher flushes, or full house/quads with 8x/Q8/88) beat me. Flash checked the river, which looks like pot control or a call‑down hand rather than an all‑in with the absolute nuts. I should extract thin value from the hands I beat while keeping the pot manageable versus the small range that dominates me. A small-to-medium value bet (~1/3 pot) will get called by Qx and worse spade/one‑pair hands but won’t overcommit me if he raises big. I’ll bet 2,200 into 6,600 to price in calls from worse while folding to (or re‑evaluating vs) a large raise that would represent the very narrow beating range.

On the river, the 8s is a very scary card for my straight as it completes a potential spade flush and pairs the board, meaning any hand with an 8 now has trips or a full house, and any set (QQ, 77, 55) has improved to a boat. However, in this heads-up dynamic, I have a very strong hand (an 8-high straight). Minni has bet 2200 into a 6600 pot (total pot 8800). To call, I only need about 20% equity. While her range contains many hands that beat me (flushes, full houses), she could also be value-betting a Queen (AQ, KQ) or a smaller straight, or potentially bluffing if she thinks the board texture change allows for it. Given the pot odds and the strength of a straight in heads-up play, folding is too exploitable, though I must be prepared to be behind.

Holding 5s 2c on the button (SB) in a heads-up situation with a stack of 56.5 big blinds. Given the high blinds (50/100) and 2:1 pot odds to call, normally it's tempting to complete, but 52o is one of the worst hands in poker. Minni has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively. Completing often leads to folding to a raise or being out of position with a junk hand post-flop. To conserve my stack and wait for a better spot to double up, I will fold.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) Qc8h is a decent opening hand — I have position and a big stack advantage (34,300 vs Flash's ~5,550). Limping is passive; I want to seize initiative, apply pressure to a shorter-stacked BB and isolate heads-up. I’ll make a standard opener to 200 (2x BB) to get value/fold equity while keeping the pot controllable. If Flash shoves I can call given my huge stack and re-evaluate, and if called I’ll play post‑flop in position — c-bet good flops, control on dangerous boards.

I am in the Big Blind with 9c5c and the pot odds are 3-to-1 to call Minni's small raise (100 to call a 200 total pot). While 95o is a weak hand, in a heads-up situation with these odds and given my relatively short stack (55.5 BBs), defending occasionally is acceptable. However, Minni has a massive chip lead (341 BBs) and has been aggressive. I've been losing chips steadily and need to be more selective with the hands I play out of position. 95o is easily dominated and hard to play post-flop. I'll fold this and look for a better spot to commit chips.

In a heads-up game with 55 big blinds, Ace-Six offsuit is a strong hand on the button (small blind). My strategy throughout this match has been to play aggressively from the button. Given the tournament status and my stack size relative to Minni's, a standard opening raise is appropriate to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Fold. 9c2c is one of the weakest playable hands — very poor connectivity and often dominated. Flash opened to 300 from the Button (acts last post‑flop), so if I call I’ll be out of position and face post‑flop pressure. Pot is 400 and calling 200 requires ~33% equity (200 to win 600) — 9‑2s won’t have that vs a reasonable button opening range. 3‑betting with this hand is overaggressive. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with K♦8♦ (suited K8) — this is a strong, playable hand in position. I’ve already posted the 50 SB; calling 50 to just see a flop surrenders positional advantage and misses many opportunities to take the pot preflop. Flash (BB) has ~55BB, so he can defend but is not so deep that I have to fear commits. I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300 total) to: 1) exploit button advantage and a wide heads-up opening range, 2) charge speculative hands and isolated weak defenders, and 3) build a pot when I have decent equity (backdoor diamond flush + K high). Plan: if Flash folds I take the blinds; if he calls I play position postflop (continuation bet favorable textures, fold to large aggression when equity is poor); if he 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (fold to big non-committal 3-bets, consider calling smaller 3-bets depending on sizing).

I have A-10 suited (Ad Td) in the Big Blind. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. With a stack of 5,550 and a 400 pot, calling is standard, but a 3-bet is also highly effective here to take control of the hand or potentially take it down pre-flop. Given the stack depth difference (Minni has a huge lead), I should play somewhat aggressively with my premium holdings but avoid over-committing to a massive bluff. However, A-10s is definitely worth a raise for value and protection. I will 3-bet to 900.

Facing a 3-bet to 900 (I must add 600) heads-up on the button with K♦8♦. This is a playable, suited King with backdoor diamond equity and a useful King blocker; in position I prefer to keep the pot controllable and use my postflop advantage. The call gives ~2:1 pot odds (need ≈33% equity) and vs a likely wide 3-bet range I have reasonable equity and many profitable runouts. A 4-bet here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and invite shoves or strong 4-bets; folding is too tight. Plan: call to see the flop, c-bet favorable textures, and fold to heavy aggression on dry misses unless I improve or get correct odds to continue.

I 3-bet pre-flop with A-10 suited, a strong hand in heads-up play, and was called by Minni. The flop is Qc 3h 8h. As the aggressor, I should continuation bet here to maintain initiative. This board is relatively disconnected, though the Queen is a significant card. A 1/3 pot bet of 600 chips is a standard sizing that puts pressure on Minni's marginal hands and allows me to represent a strong range (like AQ, KK, or AA) without overcommitting my remaining stack.

Flop: Qc 3h 8h; I hold Kd8d — second pair (middle pair) with a decent K kicker. Flash 3-bet preflop so his range is weighted to strong hands, but his small flop sizing (600) is consistent with a wide c-bet/probe as well as value bets and bluffs (he can have Qx, hearts draws, 8x, or overcards). Calling 600 into a 2400 pot gives ~20% equity required — my pair has well more than that against a large portion of his line, and being in position I should keep the pot controllable and use postflop advantage to extract value or fold to clear strength. Raising to 1200 would bloat the pot with a marginal holding and can induce shoves or big calls from better hands; folding is too nitty. Plan: call now, re-evaluate on the turn — if I improve (K or 8) or get a safe card I’ll start building for value; if a Q or heart appears or he shows heavy aggression I’ll proceed cautiously and fold if the action strongly indicates top pair/set/flush.

On the turn, the 4s is a blank card that doesn't significantly change the board texture. I 3-bet pre-flop and continuation bet the flop, representing a strong hand like top pair or an overpair. However, Minni called the flop bet, suggesting she has at least a piece of the board (like a Queen or an 8) or a draw (like hearts). With only Ace-high and no direct draws, my showdown value is marginal. Checking here is the best play to control the pot size and avoid overcommitting my remaining stack. If Minni checks back, I might still have the best hand or can re-evaluate on the river. If she bets, I will have to decide based on her sizing, but I am likely behind.

Board: Qc 3h 8h 4s. I hold K♦8♦ — second pair with a King kicker. Villain 3‑bet preflop and c‑bet the flop, then checked the turn: that line is consistent with both thin c‑bets and medium strength hands (Qx, small pairs, missed heart draws). The turn 4♠ is a relatively safe card, so this is a good spot to take the initiative: I want to extract value from worse pairs/8x hands and charge heart draws while keeping the pot controllable. I’ll make a value/protection bet of ~1/2 pot (1,500) — large enough to charge draws and get called by weaker holdings, small enough to avoid bloating the pot against a likely stronger portion of his range. If he check‑raises big or jams, I’ll give strong credit to a set/Qx and fold; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and proceed cautiously to river aggression unless I improve or the action indicates I’m beat.

On the turn, I have checked with Ace-high (Ad Td) and Minni has bet 1,500 into a 3,000 pot. While I have the Ace of diamonds, the board texture (Qc 3h 8h 4s) is not favorable for my hand. Minni calling a 3-bet pre-flop and calling a flop continuation bet suggests she likely holds a Queen, a medium pair, or a strong heart draw. With only 4,150 chips remaining, calling 1,500 would leave me with only 2,650, putting me in a precarious position on the river with a hand that currently has no showdown value against any pair. I do not have sufficient equity to continue, and a bluff-shove is too risky given her demonstrated willingness to call down. Folding allows me to preserve my remaining 41 big blinds for a better opportunity.

I have been dealt Ace-King of clubs (AKs), which is a premium starting hand, especially in a heads-up situation. My stack is relatively short at 4,100 chips compared to the blinds (50/100) and Minni's massive stack. This is a clear spot to raise for value and to build a pot I am happy to play for my entire stack. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

Heads-up vs the button's 300 open: pot is 400 and I need to call 200 (total 300 committed). I have Ts8s — a suited, connected hand that plays very well heads-up and has decent equity vs a wide SB open. Calling here gives ~3:1 on the pot (I need ~25% equity), so a call is +EV while keeping the pot manageable out of position. A tiny 3-bet to 500 won't achieve much and a large shove needlessly risks a lot versus a relatively short 38.5BB effective stack. Plan: call, see the flop, and then proceed aggressively on spade/paired/T/8 flops or control the pot / fold to strong resistance on dry boards.

Heads‑up on a dry Q♣9♣2♦ board with T♠8♠. I have 10 clean outs (3 tens, 3 eights, 4 jacks) — ~21% to hit turn, ~38% to improve by river — and only a tiny backdoor spade line. I’m out of position against a short‑stack opener and my hand is marginal, so I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than barreling thinly into a likely Qx/club‑draw heavy range. Plan: check and see what the button does. I will call small–medium bets to chase my outs but fold to large bets or a shove, and if the turn brings a spade/10/8 (or a controllable runout) I’ll either take the lead or continue aggressively depending on sizing.

On a flop of Qc-9c-2d, I have the nut flush draw plus two overcards (Ace and King). This is a very strong semi-bluffing hand. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and the opponent checked to me, I will lead with a continuation bet. With my stack size, I want to build a pot where I can comfortably commit the rest of my chips if I hit my draw or if the opponent shows aggression, as my equity is high against most of her range. A bet of 350 (slightly over half-pot) is standard here to keep the pressure on.

I have T♠8♠ on Q♣9♣2♦. That gives me 10 clean outs (3 tens + 3 eights + 4 jacks — any J completes 8-9-10-J-Q straight), ~=10/47 ≈ 21% to hit the turn and ~38.5% to improve by the river. Flash’s bet is small (350 into a 950 pot) and he is a short stack (3,500) — this looks like a standard c-bet/probe against a wide opening range. Calling 350 gives ~2.7:1 on the pot (I need ~27% equity), so calling is +EV. I’m out of position so I want to keep the pot controlled and realize equity rather than inflate it with a marginal raise. Plan: call now, fold to a large shove/very strong turn action if I miss, and if the turn brings a T/8/J (or a very favorable spade runout) I will proceed aggressively and potentially take the pot.

The turn checked to me (no bet to call). On Qc-9c-2d-2c with Ts8s I have no made hand and ~10 clean outs (3 tens + 3 eights + 4 jacks) — about 10/46 ≈ 21.7% to hit the river. Pot is 1,300 and Flash is short (~3,500). Betting here risks a shove that I shouldn’t call with ~22% equity, and a small probe-bet will often be called by a wide range, so checking to realize equity is the higher EV play. Plan: check and see the river; if I hit a T/8/J I will apply pressure for value, if I miss I will fold to large river aggression but will call small river probes when pot odds justify it.

I have completed the nut flush on the turn with the King of clubs and Ace of clubs. While the board has paired with deuces, reducing the likelihood of a full house from my opponent based on her passive play thus far, I have an extremely strong hand. I will bet for value, targeting any Queen-x, Nine-x, or smaller flush draws she might be holding. A bet of 800 into a 1,300 pot builds the pot nicely and sets up an easy all-in on the river given my remaining stack size.

Board: Qc 9c 2d 2c, my hand Ts 8s. I have ~10 outs (3 tens + 3 eights + 4 jacks) = 10/46 ≈ 21.7% to hit the river. The pot is 2100 and I must call 800, which means I need ≈ 800/(2100+800) ≈ 27.6% equity to break even. My raw equity (21.7%) is below the pot‑odds threshold, so a call is -EV. Worse, many of those outs are not clean against the opponent’s line — the turn is a club that completes a lot of two‑club (made flush) combos, so J/T/8 rivers can still lose to a flush. Flash’s line (button open, flop c‑bet, turn bet on a clubed board) is weighted toward value (Qx, 9x, or made club flushes) and he’s short‑stacked, so raising is likely to be called or shove‑called and would put me in a miserable spot. Given the math and the range reading, the correct play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with A♠9♦ (strong heads‑up hand). I want to take the initiative, get value from worse aces/broadways and fold out some of BB’s speculative hands. A standard 3xBB open (300 total) applies pressure, keeps the pot manageable if called, and prevents the BB from seeing cheap flops with a very wide range. Given my very deep stack vs Flash’s ~47BB, I don’t need to min‑raise; 300 balances fold equity and postflop playability — if he 3-bets large I can re-evaluate, if he calls I’ll play in position.

I am in the Big Blind with Q2 suited. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. The pot is offering me 2:1 odds to call (200 more to win a total pot of 400). In a heads-up match, a suited Queen is generally strong enough to defend the Big Blind against a wide opening range, especially given the pot odds. My stack is 4700, which is around 47 big blinds, so I have enough room to play post-flop without being committed. I will call and see if I can flop a flush draw or a pair, but I will remain cautious since my kicker is very weak.

I have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw on a relatively dry, low board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the most standard play is to check and see how Minni reacts. Leading here doesn't make much sense given my hand strength.

Heads-up on a very dry 4d-3c-7h flop and Flash checked after calling my preflop raise. I have A♥9♦ — Ace-high with position and decent showdown value against his wide BB calling range. A half-pot c-bet (~300 into 600) is the right play: it extracts value from worse Axs, broadways and small pairs that will call, applies pressure to pure floats and garbage hands, and keeps the pot manageable vs his shorter effective stack. Size is chosen to induce calls rather than folds while still giving me fold equity; if he check-raises big or jams (polarized to sets/overpairs/strong hands) I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold the Ace-high to a shove, but I’m happy to see a call and continue playing in position. Betting 300 balances value and protection on this dry board.

I have Queen-high on a low, dry board of 4-3-7. While I have one overcard (the Queen) and a backdoor club draw, calling a continuation bet here out of position against an aggressive opponent with a massive chip lead is likely to lead to a losing situation on the turn. My kicker is extremely weak, and hitting a Deuce likely doesn't help me against Minni's range. Given my stack size of 45 BBs, I prefer to preserve my chips for a more favorable spot rather than chasing a marginal draw with Q-high.

I am holding a pocket pair (2c, 2d) on the button. While it's the smallest pocket pair, it's a strong hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Minni, has a significantly larger stack, but I need to look for opportunities to rebuild. Raising is the standard play from the button to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop or build it for when I hit a set. I will make a standard raise to 300.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300 with J♥4♦ in the big blind. This is a marginal hand but heads-up opening ranges are wide, and the pot is 400 so I only need 200 more to call (total 300) — about 25% break-even equity. Folding is too passive; a small 3-bet would be a thin bluff and could get me into uncomfortable spots against a 42BB opponent (they can call or shove). I’ll call to see a flop, keep the pot manageable, and proceed cautiously post‑flop: continue/raise only with real equity (pair/draw or strong reads), otherwise check/fold to significant aggression.

Check. I completely missed the flop — J4 with only a backdoor diamond (needs runner–runner) and no pair. The Ace on board is squarely in BTN’s opening/c-bet range, so many of Flash’s continuing hands (Ax, overcards, pairs) beat me. I called preflop to keep the pot small and will not voluntarily build it with this marginal holding. Plan: check and fold to meaningful c-bets (or only peel a very tiny probe vs a proven frequent bluffer); if he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the turn and look for real equity or a clear bluffing spot before investing chips.

On this flop of Ad-7s-8d, my pocket deuces are very weak as there are three overcards, including an Ace which is a major scare card. While I have the range advantage as the pre-flop raiser, betting into this board with only a pair of deuces is risky, especially since Minni has a large stack and can comfortably call with any Ace, 7, 8, or diamond draw. By checking back, I control the pot size and can see how the turn develops. If Minni is weak, I might get to a cheap showdown, but I am not looking to commit many chips to this pot unless I somehow improve.

I have J♥4♦ on A♦ 7♠ 8♦ 6♠. I have no made hand and only runner-runner/backdoor prospects — about 10 unique river outs (3 jacks, 3 fours, 4 fives) → ~21–22% to improve on the river. Flash checked both flop and turn, which shows either weakness or a slow-played strong hand (Ax or a pair) — I can’t credibly represent a made hand here without a real draw. The pot is small (600) and Flash still has ~4200 behind; betting now as a bluff risks a shove/call from better hands and unnecessarily bloats a pot when I’m drawing only to the river. Pot control is the priority: check back the turn, see the river for free, and only invest more if I actually improve or get a clear, low-frequency fold equity spot. If Flash checks the river I can consider a small bluff; if he bets and I miss I’ll fold to meaningful aggression.

The turn card is the 6 of spades, making the board Ad-7s-8d-6s. My pocket deuces have very little value here as almost any card in Minni's range that isn't complete air likely beats me. However, Minni has checked for the second time, suggesting she might not have a strong Ace or a made straight. While a bet could potentially fold out some of her weaker hands or draws, my stack is relatively small compared to hers, and a check-raise would be devastating. By checking back, I'm taking the free card and looking to get to a cheap showdown or potentially fold if she bets big on the river. There's no reason to turn my hand into a bluff right now when I can control the pot.

The river 5♣ completes a 4-5-6-7-8 sequence — my 4♦ + board (5-6-7-8) gives me an 8-high straight. That’s a very strong heads-up holding; the only common hands that beat me are any 9x (making 9-high straight) or a 4 in Flash’s hand (which would tie). Flash checked both flop and turn, which weakly signals either a missed/cautious hand or a slow-play; either way I can extract value. Pot is small (600) relative to stacks, so I want a size that gets called by A-x, medium pairs (7s/8s/6s) and busted draws rather than folding them. I’ll bet a half-pot (300) to maximize thin value while keeping worse hands in; if Flash raises I’ll re-evaluate (call a reasonable-sized raise for value, fold to an over-aggressive shove that strongly polarizes to 9s/monsters).

I am holding pocket deuces on a board of Ad-7s-8d-6s-5c. While the pot odds are 3:1 for a call, my hand is extremely weak in this spot. The board is highly coordinated, and almost any hand Minni defended with from the big blind (including any 4, 9, or even a pair of 5s or 6s that hit the board) beats my pair of deuces. Minni's bet of 300 into 600 after checking twice suggests she either improved on the river or is betting a middle pair for value. Since I lose to almost everything in her range except a total air bluff, and even some of her potential bluffs could beat 22 (like pocket 3s or 4s), folding is the most prudent move to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with K♠2♣. Pot is 150 and I only need to put in 50 to call, but Kx is a strong heads-up holding and I have positional advantage postflop. I want to play aggressively with a wide stealing range and take the initiative, so I’ll open-raise to a standard 2x BB (total 200). Size is large enough to pick up the pot often but small enough to keep marginal hands in and preserve fold equity against Flash’s 41BB stack. Plan: if called use position to c-bet favorable (dry) boards and control the pot; fold or seriously re-evaluate versus a large 3-bet or shove.

I am in the Big Blind with 4s 2h. The Button (Minni) has raised to 200, making it 100 more to call. Given my current stack of 4100 compared to Minni's 35600, I need to be selective about the pots I enter. 4-2 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker and does not play well post-flop, especially against a large stack that can apply significant pressure. I will preserve my remaining 41 Big Blinds for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 6s 3s, which is a marginal hand even in heads-up play. However, given my short stack of 4050 compared to Minni's 35800, I need to look for spots to accumulate chips without taking excessive risks. Since I am in the Small Blind/Button, I only need to put in 50 more to call. Given the odds, it's worth seeing a flop with a suited hand, although folding is also reasonable. Raising is too risky with such a weak hand against a massive chip leader. I will call the 50 to see if I can hit the flop well.

Heads-up in the big blind with no raise and Flash (SB) just limped/called 50. My hand 7h4c is very weak, unsuited and disconnected; it has poor playability out of position. With the pot already small (200) there’s no need to bloat the pot with a marginal hand — check and see a cheap flop. Post-flop I’ll fold to significant aggression unless I pair or pick up a strong draw; if the SB checks back I keep pot control and can apply pressure on later streets when appropriate given my huge stack advantage.

Heads-up vs a limp/call from the SB — Flash’s range is very wide (overcards, small pairs, suited connectors). I hold pair of fours (4c) with a 7 kicker: a made but vulnerable hand — likely ahead of many unpaired hands but behind 9x/3x/4x and vulnerable to aggression. Pot is small (200) and I’m out of position with a huge stack advantage, so I want value and to charge draws while keeping the pot controlled. I’ll make a modest value bet of 150 (~75% pot) to extract from worse hands and deny free cards to overcards/draws. If Flash raises significantly or shoves, I’ll fold this marginal holding; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously (check/control vs aggression, or continue-for-value if I improve).

I have bottom pair (threes) on a 9-4-3 rainbow board. In a heads-up limped pot, bottom pair is often enough to continue against a single bet, especially when I have a backdoor flush draw as well. Minni's lead of 150 into 200 is a significant bet, but folding bottom pair here would be too exploitable. I will call to see how the turn develops.

The King on the turn is a dangerous overcard to my pair of fours and likely improved a lot of hands in Flash’s wide calling range (Kx, K9, maybe 3x/9x/4x). I have a vulnerable one-pair hand out of position and the pot is still small (500). I don’t want to bloat the pot or face a shove from a short-stacked opponent with a marginal hand that dominates me, so I’ll control the pot size and see how Flash reacts. If Flash bets big I can fold and save chips; if he checks back I keep a small pot and can reassess the river. Check to pot-control and avoid committing with a marginal holding.

On the turn, the King of Spades has been dealt, which gives me a flush draw to go with my bottom pair of threes. Minni has checked, which often indicates weakness or a desire to control the pot size. While I could bet to try and take down the pot now, my hand has decent showdown value and some equity to improve on the river. By checking back, I can see a free river card and potentially realize my equity or catch Minni bluffing on certain river cards. I will check to keep the pot small given my short stack.

The river 4 completes my hand: I have trip FOURS (4c + 4s + 4h) — a very strong holding heads-up. Flash limped pre, called my flop bet and then checked turn and river, which points to a wide/sticky calling range (Kx, 9x, medium pairs, missed draws). Very few hands beat me here (primarily pocket 9s making a full house); the one remaining 4 would produce quads but that would usually tie the pot. The pot is small (500) and Flash is short-stacked (3,850), so I should extract value rather than surrender showdown. I’ll bet a size that gets called by worse (Kx, single pairs, missed draws) but isn’t so large it folds those hands — bet 1,000. If Flash jams over this, I’m committed to call with trips against his likely value shoves.

The river 4 paired the board, which unfortunately counterfeited my pair of 3s. My final hand is now a pair of 4s (from the board) with a King, Nine, and Six kicker. Minni's bet of 1000 into a 500 pot is a massive overbet. Given that any card higher than a 6 in Minni's hand (including all Aces, Queens, Jacks, Tens, etc.) beats my hand, and she could easily have a 4, a King, or a Nine, I have no reason to call. Even if she is bluffing, she often holds a better high card than my 6. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with K♦8♣ — a playable hand in this spot. Against a single big blind I want to be aggressive and take the initiative: raising opens a wide heads-up range, gains fold equity, and lets me play postflop in position if called. The opponent has ~3,750 (≈37.5BB), so he can 3-bet/shove, but K8o has decent equity vs a wide defending range and I can fold to extreme aggression if his shove looks tight. A standard min-open to 200 (2x BB) is efficient here: it pressures the BB, keeps pot manageable, and maximizes long-term EV in heads-up play. If called I'll continuation-bet favorable flops and control size on dangerous boards.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 3s, which is a very weak hand. The pot is offering me 2:1 odds to call (100 more to win a total pot of 300), but I am significantly out-chipped (3,750 vs 35,950) and out of position. Minni has been aggressive throughout the match. Conserving my remaining 37.5 big blinds for a better spot is more prudent than defending with 8-high offsuit, especially since the goal is to survive and maximize stack over the final 24 hands.

I am on the button with J9o. This is a solid starting hand in a heads-up situation. With 37 big blinds, I have a decent stack to work with, although I am significantly out-stacked by Minni. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or see a flop with control of the hand.

Flash opened to 300 from the button heads-up. I’m in the big blind with Q♠6♥. Pot is 400 and I need to add 200 to call; calling would make the pot 600 so I’m getting about 3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity). In heads‑up play the BB defends wide and Q6o has reasonable equity vs a BTN stealing range. My stack is much deeper than Flash’s (36150 vs 3450) so I don’t need to force the issue with a bluff 3‑bet — a small 3‑bet would commit extra without much fold equity or value. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed cautiously out of position, folding to very strong action or a shove if I don’t improve.

Check. I have Q♠6♥ on A♦ T♣ 7♦ — no pair, no meaningful backdoor (only very remote two-card runouts). I’m out of position vs the preflop raiser whose continuing range is heavily weighted toward Ax/broadways and is well-hit by this flop, so a C‑bet from me would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity. I’m also massively deeper than Flash, so there’s no need to force the hand; checking controls the pot and lets me fold to clear strength (a sizable c‑bet or shove) or call a very small probe for proper pot odds. If checked to me on later streets I’ll re‑evaluate — look for a queen/6 or a safe scrub card to bluff — otherwise preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

On a board of 7d-Ad-Tc, I am the pre-flop aggressor with a gutshot straight draw (needs an 8). Although I haven't hit the board, the presence of the Ace favors my perceived range as the raiser. Minni has checked, giving me the opportunity to continuation bet and potentially take the pot down immediately. A bet of half the pot is a standard sizing to represent an Ace or a strong Ten while keeping the pot manageable if I am called.

Heads‑up on a wet Ad Tc 7d flop. Pot is 900 and Flash bets 300 (≈1/3 pot). I hold Q♠6♥ out of position. Calling 300 gives me 300/(900+300)=25% break‑even equity. I have ~6 clean outs (3 queens + 3 sixes) for ~24% to hit a pair by the river (plus a very small runner‑runner backdoor chance) — so raw equity is marginally below the break‑even point. Still, this is a small probe from a short‑stacked preflop raiser who will c‑bet a wide range heads‑up; calling is cheap and lets me realize equity without committing chips OOP. A raise would be a pure bluff with poor blockers and risks a shove I don’t want to face; folding to a 1/3‑pot bet is too tight. Plan: call the 300 to see a turn, fold to a sizable shove or clear strength if I miss, and if I pair a Q or 6 on the turn look to extract value or apply controlled pressure.

Heads‑up vs a short‑stack (Flash ~3,150). I hold Q♠6♥ on 7♦ A♦ T♣ 4♣ and there is no bet to me on the turn (pot 1,200). I have no pair and almost no clean equity; betting here would be a thin bluff with poor blockers and risks getting shoved by any Ace/Ten/top‑pair hands — I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot, lets me see a cheap river, and preserves my large stack advantage. Plan: check and evaluate the river — if I pair my Q (or 6) I can extract value; if I miss and face clear strength I fold.

The turn 4c is a blank. I have Jack-high with a gutshot to the low end of a straight (needing an 8). Minni called the flop bet, suggesting she has a piece of the board, likely an Ace, a Ten, or a draw. Since I have no made hand and limited equity, checking back is the most sensible play to see a free river and potentially realize my slim equity or evaluate on the final street without committing more chips to a pot where I am currently behind.

River paired my queen — I now have a pair of queens (Q♠) on 7♦ A♦ T♣ 4♣ Q♣. Flash (3150) showed a standard c-bet on the flop then checked the turn, which looks like a wide/weak continuing range (Ax, Tx, mid pairs, missed draws and air). My hand is second pair behind any Ace but ahead of a lot of his missed or medium holdings (T-x, pocket pairs below Q, 7x, many bluffs). With a short opponent I want to extract value without bloating the pot or inviting a large jam from a polarized strong range. A small-to-medium value bet (~1/3 pot) pressures pure air and gets called by worse hands while keeping the decision easy if he shoves. I’ll bet ~400 to take value now and fold to a big shove if he repolarizes — that preserves my large stack and steals thin value from his calling hands.

The board has evolved into 7d-Ad-Tc-4c-Qc. I have Jack-high and missed my gutshot draw. Minni checked-called the flop, checked the turn, and is now leading for 400 into a 1200 pot. This small lead on the river often signifies thin value (like a Ten or a weak Ace) or potentially a pair of Queens that improved on the river. Given that I have no showdown value with Jack-high and the pot odds don't justify a call against a range that likely has me beaten, I will fold and preserve my remaining stack.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with 9♠7♠ — a suited, connected hand that plays very well in position. Pot is 150 and calling costs just 50, but I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure to the shorter-stacked BB (Flash has ~30 BB). A standard 3x BB sizing is appropriate: it gives good fold equity, keeps postflop playability, and doesn’t overcommit me against a possible shove. I’ll raise to 300 total (I’ve already posted 50 SB, so this adds 250). If Flash 3-bets large showing a polarized range I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold); if he calls I’ll play aggressively in position.

I have 2d 4s in the Big Blind and am facing a raise to 300 from Minni. My stack is quite short (3050 chips, which is about 30 BBs). Holding 42o out of position is extremely weak and doesn't play well post-flop, especially against a dominant chip leader. I should fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my remaining chips.

I am on the button with 8d 6c. My stack is quite low (3000 chips) compared to the Big Blind who has over 36,000. In a heads-up situation, 8-6 offsuit is a marginal hand. However, given my stack size, I need to look for opportunities to pick up pots. Calling 50 more to see a flop is a low-cost investment to potentially hit a favorable board, though folding is also reasonable if I want to wait for a stronger hand. Considering the tournament progress (hand 130 of 150), I will complete the small blind to see if I can find a profitable flop.

Heads-up in the big blind with Q♦4♠. Flash (SB) just completed to the blind (limped), pot is small (200) and I have a huge stack advantage (36,850 vs 2,950). Q4o is marginal but in heads-up play it’s worth taking the initiative and denying free flops. I’ll make the minimum preflop raise to 200 total to isolate, apply pressure to a wide limp/call range, and keep the risk controlled. Plan: if he folds I pick up the pot; if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable flops and give up to heavy resistance on cards that miss my hand.

I am in position on the button with 8d 6c. Minni has raised my limp by a small amount (100 more). Given the pot odds are 3:1 and I have position for the rest of the hand, I will call the additional 100 to see a flop. With a stack of approximately 30 BB, I need to look for opportunities to chip up, and 8-6 offsuit is a playable hand heads-up when the price is right.

Heads-up vs a shorter-stacked caller who called my preflop raise. Board 2h-7h-8s is fairly dry and misses a large portion of his calling range; I have an overcard (Q) but no draw. As the preflop aggressor I should take the initiative and c-bet to fold lots of marginal hands and draws while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal hand. I’ll make a standard ~50% pot continuation bet (200 into 400). Plan: if he raises/shoves I’ll give this up — Q4 has poor showdown value vs heavy resistance; if he calls I’ll reassess on the turn and proceed only if I improve or get a clear sign of weakness.

I have hit top pair (eights) on a 2h-7h-8s board. While my kicker (6) is weak, top pair is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has led for 200 into a 400 pot. I will call to see how the turn develops. Raising here might isolate me against better eights or overpairs, whereas calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and potentially catch bluffs on later streets.

Check to control the pot and avoid risking a big chunk of my huge stack with Q4o. I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the fairly dry 2h-7h-8s flop to fold lots of marginal hands, but Flash called — his range now likely contains pairs (8x/7x/2x), floats, and some A-x. The turn A♥ is a bad card for my range: it improves many hands that would call a flop bet and makes any further aggression by me thin. I have no pair and no meaningful draw (no hearts), so I should not build the pot. Plan: check and pot-control; fold to significant turn pressure or shove; only continue to a small probe bet if the price is extremely cheap, otherwise reevaluate on the river if I improve or if he shows weakness.

The turn card is the Ace of hearts, which is a significant scare card. It completes potential heart flushes and is a card that very often hits a pre-flop raiser's range. Although I have second pair (eights), the Ace likely improves Minni's range or makes her more cautious if she held a pocket pair like 99-KK. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, exercise pot control with a hand that has some showdown value but is now vulnerable, and I get to see the river for free. This avoids a potential check-raise from a stronger hand and keeps my remaining stack protected.

I have Q♦4♠ on a final board 2♥-7♥-8♠-A♥-2♠. I have no pair or meaningful showdown value and the river paired the deuce, giving any 2 trips and leaving many of Flash’s plausible callees (A‑x, 7x, 8x, 2x) ahead of me. A bluff shove would need an unrealistically high fold rate (≈76.8%) to be profitable; even a small probe bet (~400) needs ≈33% folds — unlikely given he called the flop and then pot‑controlled the turn. I’m massively deeper than him, so there’s no need to gamble chips with a pure bluff here. I’ll check and take the showdown; if he leads I’ll fold to significant river pressure.

I have two pair, eights and deuces, with an Ace kicker from the board. The board shows a potential flush and an Ace, which are both significant scare cards. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, checked both the turn and the river. This indicates she likely does not have an Ace or a flush, but she could still be holding a marginal hand like a seven or a small pocket pair that might beat me or result in a split. Given my relatively small stack of 2650 chips and the fact that I have decent showdown value, checking back is the safest play. It allows me to see the showdown for free without risking a check-raise from a potentially trapped hand, ensuring I preserve my stack as we approach the final hands of the tournament.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide. 4♣3♥ is weak but playable in position and the cost to see a flop is only 50; instead I’ll take the initiative and raise to a standard 3x-BB size (300 total). This sizing gives decent fold equity against Flash’s 3,350-chip stack, keeps the pot manageable if called, and lets me leverage position post‑flop. I’ll preserve the option to fold to a large 3‑bet or proceed cautiously on unfavorable flops — primary goal is to steal the BB or see a flop in position with a cheap speculative hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s2c, a very weak hand. Minni, who has a massive chip lead, has raised to 300. Calling 200 more with 92o out of position is a losing play, especially given my relatively short stack of 3350 chips compared to the blinds (100). I need to conserve my chips for better spots where I have more equity or a better chance to bluff. There is no reason to defend such a weak hand here.

With 8h 6s on the button in a heads-up situation, I have a marginal hand. My stack is quite short (33 BBs) compared to the opponent. While 86 offsuit isn't a premium hand, I can occasionally complete the small blind to see a cheap flop, especially against a large stack that has been aggressive. However, I have already folded many times recently to Minni's aggression. Completing the small blind for 50 more chips is a reasonable way to stay involved without risking too much of my remaining stack, but folding is also a safe option given the quality of the hand. I will choose to fold to preserve my stack for a better spot, as I'm getting low and need a stronger hand to commit chips against such a massive chip lead.

Heads-up on the button (I’m small blind) with 2h6h suited — a weak hand but has some playability and I have the positional advantage. Flash in the big blind has ~32bb (3200) while I’m very deep, so a standard aggressive open is appropriate to steal the blind and take initiative. I’ll open to a normal 3x-ish size (300 total) — if he calls I can play postflop in position; if he 3-bets heavily or shoves I’ll fold this marginal holding. Raising to 300 applies pressure while keeping risk reasonable relative to my stack.
