I am in the Big Blind with 3h 5s. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. I have a very short stack (3200 chips, 32 big blinds) compared to Minni's massive stack (36400). 3-5 offsuit is a very weak hand and does not play well out of position, especially against a large stack that can apply significant pressure. There is no need to defend this hand and risk chips given the current situation.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-6 offsuit, which is a weak hand even in heads-up play. With a stack of about 31 big blinds, I need to be selective about the hands I play, especially since my opponent, Minni, has a massive chip lead and has shown aggressive pre-flop behavior. Folding here allows me to preserve my stack and wait for a stronger starting hand or a better position to make a move.

Heads-up on the button with K♥Q♦ — this is a strong, playable hand and I have the positional advantage. Flash is effectively ~30bb (3050) so I want to apply pressure and isolate rather than limp. A raise to 1,200 total (~12bb) achieves that: it folds a lot of his weak holdings, induces shoves from wide ranges, and keeps the pot manageable if he calls so I can play postflop in position. If he shoves I’m prepared to call — KQ has good equity vs a wide shove range — if he calls I can continue with position and initiative. I avoid just calling 50 (limping) because that gives him cheap odds and lets him control the pot from the BB.

I am in the Big Blind with J3s. Minni has raised to 1200 from the Small Blind, which is a very large raise (12x the big blind) considering my stack size of only 3050. I have only about 30BBs total, and calling 1100 would commit more than a third of my stack with a mediocre hand out of position. Minni has an overwhelming chip lead (35k vs 3k) and has shown aggression in previous hands with large pre-flop sizing (Hand #45, #52, #65, #104). Given the tournament situation where I need to find a better spot to double up or preserve chips, folding J3s against such a large open is the most prudent play.

I am on the button with 7c 3c, a weak speculative hand. I have a very small stack (3000) compared to Minni (36850). With only 15 hands remaining in the tournament, I need to look for better spots to commit chips or find a high-equity shove. While suited, 7-3 is too weak to play profitably given the massive stack disadvantage and the current blind pressure. I will fold and wait for a stronger hand to double up.

Heads-up, on the button (I'm the small blind) J♠8♥ is well within a standard opening range. I only have to call 50 to complete, but I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to Flash's ~29bb stack, and play postflop in position. Min raise is 200, but opening to 300 (3bb) is a standard, balanced size that gives good fold equity while keeping pot controllable. If he 3-bets big I'll re-evaluate (likely fold or defend depending on sizing); if he calls I'll use position to c-bet favorable boards and realize equity on others.

I have a premium hand (Pocket Tens) in a heads-up situation. My stack is currently very short at 2900 chips (29 big blinds), while my opponent, Minni, has a massive lead. Given the tournament is nearing its end (Hand 137 of 150), I need to find opportunities to double up. Pocket Tens is well ahead of Minni's typical button opening range. Shoving all-in here maximizes my fold equity and ensures that if I am called, I am likely either a significant favorite or in a coin-flip situation. There's no reason to play a post-flop game with this stack-to-pot ratio; committing the full stack now is the most effective play.

I opened to 300 on the button with J♠8♥. Flash 3‑bet to 2,900, so calling requires 2,600 into a ~3,200 pot (≈44% pot odds). J8o is a marginal hand that is often dominated by a large 3‑bet range and has poor equity against a polarized/value-heavy 3‑bet. A 4‑bet shove as a bluff is too risky without a clear read, and calling commits a big chunk of my stack into a difficult postflop spot. With only 13 hands left in the tournament and no specific read that Flash is 3‑betting extremely light here, preserving my stack and folding is the +EV play.

I have been dealt pocket nines (9h 9c) on the button. With approximately 32.5 big blinds remaining in my stack, this is a premium hand in heads-up play. My goal is to maximize value and potentially double up to stay in the tournament. I will start with a standard raise to 300, which is consistent with my previous opening sizes. If my opponent re-raises, I am prepared to commit the rest of my stack pre-flop, as pocket nines are very strong in this configuration.

Heads-up in the big blind with 9♠4♠ facing a standard open to 300 from a 3,000-stack button. The extra to call is only 200 into a 400 pot — cheap enough (about 33% break-even equity) to defend with a suited, playable hand in heads-up. I don’t want to 3-bet or shove with a weak, unsuited-ish hand and lose position; a small reraise gives up postflop leverage and risks playing a big pot dominated. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to heavy action if I miss, and play aggressively if I pick up a pair/flush/strong draw. If Flash jams a large overbet, I’ll reassess with pot odds and his shove range (likely fold 9♠4♠ to a full shove).

Flop is 2♦‑2♠‑3♥ and I have 9♠4♠ — no pair, only a very weak backdoor/runner flush possibility. Flash opened wide heads‑up and can easily have a deuce, a 3, any pocket pair or many hands that currently beat me. Pot is small relative to stacks and Flash is short (3,000), so I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with essentially air and give him cheap spots to shove. Best play is to control the pot and see a turn: check and fold to clear, committed aggression unless I pick up a pair or a very favorable runout. If he bets a small probe I’ll consider calling only if the price is right; otherwise fold to heavy pressure. For now: check (0).

On a very dry board of 2-2-3, my pocket nines are effectively an overpair and likely the best hand. Minni's check is expected. I will place a continuation bet of half the pot to get value from hands like A-high, small pocket pairs, or straight draws (4-5). Given my short stack relative to the blinds and the total chips in play, I want to build the pot now so I can comfortably commit the rest of my chips on a safe turn.

Flop is 2♦‑2♠‑3♥; I hold 9♠4♠ — no pair, only a very slim backdoor spade runner-runner. Flash leads small (300 into a 900 pot). Calling 300 here requires ~25% equity, which is reasonable given his wide HU opening/CB range; I can realize equity and keep the pot manageable OOP. Raising with essentially air is a mistake — it bloats the pot and creates bad spots if he calls or shoves. Plan: call the small probe to see a turn card, fold to serious turn shove or heavy sustained pressure if I don’t improve, and only continue aggressively if I pair or get a clear draw.

Check. I'm out of position with 9♠4♠ on 2♦-2♠-3♥-8♣ — no pair, only a remote runner-runner spade flush. Flash opened wide preflop and c-bet the paired board, so his range contains many hands that currently crush me (any deuce, a 3, pocket pairs, even some made hands or better single-paired hands). Pot is 1,200 and Flash is short (~2,700) while I am very deep — there is no reason to bloat this pot OOP with air. Checking controls the pot and preserves chips; I will fold to any meaningful turn bet or shove because my hand has essentially no showdown equity. If he checks back I can re-evaluate the river and look for a cheap opportunity to steal or realize that I need to fold to aggression on later streets.

The turn is an 8c, which is a safe card for my pocket nines as it does not complete any obvious draws besides a gutshot with 4-5 or 6-7. Minni has checked again, indicating weakness or a potential trap with a deuce. With my stack size (2700 remaining) and the pot (1200), I want to continue extracting value from pairs lower than nines, or Ace-high. A bet of 600 (half pot) sets up a potential shove on the river while leaving me some fold equity if the board gets very scary, though given my stack depth, I am mostly committed to this hand.

I checked the turn with 9♠4♠ and Flash bet 600 into a 1800 pot. I have no pair and the turn (8♣) removed my backdoor runner-runner equity — the only reasonable outs are pairing my 9 or 4 (3 nines + 3 fours = 6 outs), ~6/46 ≈ 13% to hit the river. Calling 600 into a 1800 pot requires roughly 25% equity (600/(1800+600)=25%), so a call is -EV. Raising as a bluff is poorly priced OOP and will likely induce a shove from a short-stacked opponent who can easily have any deuce, a 3, or a pocket pair that already beats me. I’ll preserve my large stack and fold to this turn aggression.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with T8o — a playable, reasonably connected hand. Opponent (BB) is relatively short (≈38 BB) so raising to take the initiative and steal the blind is profitable; I keep the sizing small (min-raise to 200) to preserve fold equity while keeping the pot controllable with a marginal hand. In position postflop I can c-bet or probe and fold to heavy 3-bet shoves if Flash jams (T8o is not a calling-hand vs a tight all-in range). Raise to 200 to open the pot and play with position.

I have AcKc, which is a premier starting hand. Minni has a significant chip lead, so I am looking to double up or build a pot here. Since I am in the Big Blind and Minni has raised to 200, I will 3-bet to put pressure on and build the pot for my value hand. Given my stack size of 3800, a 3-bet to 700-800 is standard, but I want to be aggressive here to potentially get it all in if she 4-bets or calls and I hit the flop.

Flash 3‑bet to 750 into my 200 open. T8o is a marginal hand to continue multi‑street against a 3‑bet, but Flash is very short (3150 behind = ~31.5BB) and often 3‑bets aggressively. I have a massive stack advantage (35900) so I can profitably apply pressure: a 4‑bet to 3900 puts Flash all‑in. That either wins the current 950 chip pot immediately when he folds, or creates a single all‑in pot (both committed 3900) where risking ~3150 is a small fraction (~11%) of my stack. Calling 550 and playing post‑flop with a marginal hand is inferior (limited implied odds vs a short stack) and a small 4‑bet (to 1300) gives less fold equity. I 4‑bet shove to maximize fold equity and exploit the short stack; if he calls we play a straightforward all‑in for his stack.

I hold AcKc, which is a premium hand. Minni's 4-bet to 3900 is effectively an all-in since I only have 3150 chips remaining in my stack. In a heads-up situation, AK-suited is far ahead of a wide 4-betting range and is essentially a must-call or must-shove for this stack depth. Given the odds and the strength of my hand, I am committing the rest of my chips.
